introduction.purpose: This document provides a detailed specification for the **video search** software. Its primary purpose is to enable users to efficiently search multiple web platforms for streaming videos and torrents, consolidating results into a single interface. This specification outlines the system's core features, operational specifics, and critical constraints that may influence its development and deployment.
introduction.documentConventions: This Software Requirements Specification adheres to the guidelines established by IEEE Standard 830-1998, 'Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications'. All requirements are expressed in clear, unambiguous language, with unique identifiers to ensure traceability. Key terms and acronyms are defined in Appendix A to ensure consistent understanding across all stakeholders.
introduction.intendedAudience: This document is primarily intended for the development team and project managers involved in the construction of the **video search** software, serving as a foundational guide. It also targets potential users and stakeholders to gather feedback, facilitating iterative improvements and revisions throughout the product lifecycle.
introduction.productScope: The **video search** software is designed as a standalone system leveraging an active internet connection to query numerous websites for streaming video content and torrents. Its scope encompasses providing a unified search experience, significantly reducing the time and effort typically associated with locating specific videos or genres across disparate platforms. The system incorporates internal databases of search sources, which are updateable to expand its search capabilities.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.
http://www.youtube.com
http://www.megavideo.com
http://www.facebook.com
http://www.surfthechannel.com
http://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/
http://www.google.com

overallDescription.productPerspective: The **video search** program is introduced as a novel, self-contained product, developed in response to a clear market demand identified through ethnographic studies. These studies highlighted a prevalent user behavior of spending considerable time searching various websites for streaming content or downloadable video files. The **video search** system aims to streamline this time-consuming process, offering a more efficient solution.
productFunctions: Search engine for locating torrents and streaming video sites.
Filtering options for search results based on content and age restrictions.
Ability to filter or restrict searches to specific websites.
Storage facility for users' favorite videos for later access.
Management of search sources by authorized personnel.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: These users will interact solely with the front-end interface of the **video search** system. Their primary objective is to find videos efficiently across various online sources. They are expected to range from novice to experienced computer users, seeking a simple and effective tool for video discovery.

This class comprises the development team responsible for maintaining and enhancing the **video search** system. They possess technical expertise and are authorized to modify the system's search capabilities, including adding or updating websites that the system queries. Their decisions are based on criteria such as site safety, compatibility, search speed, and utility.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The **video search** system is engineered for portability, ensuring compatibility across diverse operating systems including **Microsoft Windows XP**, **Windows Vista**, **Mac OS X**, and various **Linux** distributions. It is also designed to function seamlessly with prominent web browsers such as **Internet Explorer**, **Firefox**, and **Safari**.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The **video search** system requires a continuous, active internet connection for all search functionalities. This dependency necessitates a reliable network connection for optimal operation. The system's internal databases, which store compatible torrent and streaming video sites, must be designed for efficient online updates to ensure a comprehensive and current search range.

The software must adhere to strict performance requirements for query times and result sorting, ensuring a responsive user experience. It will utilize PHP or similar server-side languages for communication with external servers to query websites. Design must allow for easy integration with default web browsers for opening hyperlinks.
userDocumentation: The **video search** system will be delivered with comprehensive user documentation, which may include user manuals, integrated on-line help functionalities, and guided tutorials. These resources are designed to assist users in understanding the software's features, navigating its interface, and troubleshooting common issues, ensuring a smooth and effective user experience.
assumptionsAndDependencies: It is assumed that users will have a reasonably up-to-date computer with an active internet connection via a modem. The system relies heavily on the availability and responsiveness of third-party torrent and video streaming websites, as it does not host content directly. Any changes to the APIs or structures of these external sites could impact the system's functionality.

The system assumes compliance with legal requirements regarding the indexing of external links, operating under the premise that linking to content, rather than hosting it, remains within legal boundaries. Continuous monitoring of these external sites for legal and safety compliance is a critical dependency.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: The **video search** system will present a single, cohesive main screen, centralizing all functionalities for user convenience. This design philosophy eliminates the need for navigating multiple screens, allowing users to consistently view their search results and available options. The interface prioritizes simplicity and ease of navigation, aiming to appeal to both novice and experienced computer users by offering a streamlined and intuitive experience.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The **video search** software will interface directly with standard computer hardware components. This includes compatibility with modern processors and sufficient RAM to ensure smooth operation. It requires a network interface card or integrated modem for establishing and maintaining an active internet connection, which is fundamental to its core search capabilities. No specialized hardware is anticipated beyond a conventional personal computer setup.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: The **video search** system will employ standard software interfaces, primarily through the use of hyperlinks. These hyperlinks will enable seamless integration with the user's default web browser, allowing search results to be opened directly for viewing or downloading. Furthermore, the system will interface with internal databases that store a dynamically updateable list of compatible torrent and streaming websites, necessitating robust data management and retrieval mechanisms.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: The **video search** system's communication functions will primarily rely on standard web communication protocols, such as **HTTP** and **HTTPS**, to interact with external web servers. These protocols will facilitate the querying of different websites and the retrieval of search results. While **PHP** or similar server-side scripting languages are mentioned for querying external servers, the application will encapsulate these interactions behind its user interface, presenting results within its own display. Message formatting will adhere to web standards to ensure interoperability with various online sources. Appropriate security measures for data transfer, such as encryption for sensitive communications, will be implemented if user data or secure interactions become part of future enhancements.

systemFeatures.name: Torrent Search
systemFeatures.description: This feature allows users to submit a search term to query a curated database of compatible torrent websites. The system processes these queries and displays the relevant results as web links within a dedicated tab on the program’s main interface. Each result provides essential information such as the source website, seed and peer counts, file size, and upload date. This functionality is considered a cornerstone of the **video search** application and is therefore assigned a high development priority due to its direct impact on user value by simplifying torrent discovery.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects the torrent search option. Response: The system includes torrent websites in subsequent search queries.

Stimulus: User enters a search term and initiates a search. Response: The system queries its database of torrent websites, retrieves relevant information, and displays the results in the torrent tab, including website, seeds, peers, size, date posted, and a webpage link.

Stimulus: User clicks a column header in the search results. Response: The system sorts all displayed torrent results in ascending or descending order based on the selected column (e.g., name, size, date).
Stimulus: User clicks the 'Next Page' button. Response: The system navigates to and displays the subsequent set of torrent search results.
functionalRequirements: The system shall share a single search bar between torrent and streaming searches (VS-REQ-001).
The system shall provide a mechanism to update the database of torrent sites via the internet (VS-REQ-002).
The system shall include a selectable option (e.g., a tick box) for the user to include torrent searching in their overall query (VS-REQ-003).
The system shall retrieve and display the number of seeds, peers, file size, date posted, and a link to the webpage for each torrent result (VS-REQ-004).
The system shall display the message "No results were found for this search." if no torrent results are found for a given query (VS-REQ-005).
The system shall allow torrent results to be arranged by size, date, or alphabetical order upon clicking their respective column headers (VS-REQ-006).
The system shall provide pagination controls for users to navigate through multiple pages of torrent search results (VS-REQ-007).

systemFeatures.name: Video Stream Search
systemFeatures.description: This feature enables users to search a comprehensive database of compatible video streaming websites, encompassing both direct video hosts (e.g., YouTube) and sites that aggregate links to videos hosted elsewhere (e.g., surfthechannel.com). Search results are presented in a dedicated tab on the main window, logically divided into two sections for hosted videos and linked videos. This dual approach maximizes content discovery and is identified as a high-priority feature, central to the **video search** application's core offering. Its ability to consolidate diverse streaming sources significantly enhances user convenience and broadens accessible content.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects the streaming host search option. Response: The system includes video hosting sites in subsequent search queries.
Stimulus: User selects the streaming links search option. Response: The system includes video link aggregation sites in subsequent search queries.

Stimulus: User enters a search term and initiates a search. Response: The system queries its video streaming databases, retrieves relevant information (full video name, length, date for hosts; show/episode name for links), and displays results in the video stream tab, separated by host and link categories.

Stimulus: User clicks a column header in the video stream search results. Response: The system sorts all displayed streaming results in ascending or descending order based on the selected column (e.g., length, date, alphabetical order).
Stimulus: User applies filters for specific websites. Response: The system updates the displayed results to exclude unselected websites, making the search more relevant.
Stimulus: User clicks the 'Next Page' button. Response: The system navigates to and displays the subsequent set of video stream search results.
functionalRequirements: The system shall share a single search bar between torrent and streaming searches (VS-REQ-008).
The system shall provide a mechanism to update the database of video hosting and video linking sites via the internet (VS-REQ-009).
The system shall include a selectable option (e.g., a tick box) for the user to include video host searching in their overall query (VS-REQ-010).
The system shall include a selectable option (e.g., a tick box) for the user to include video link searching in their overall query (VS-REQ-011).
The system shall retrieve and display the full video name, length, date posted, and a link to the video itself for results from video hosting sites (VS-REQ-012).
The system shall retrieve and display the show's name, episode name, and a link to the webpage itself for results from video link sites (VS-REQ-013).
The system shall display the message "No results were found for this search." if no streaming results are found for a given query (VS-REQ-014).
The system shall allow streaming results to be arranged by length, date, or alphabetical order upon clicking their respective column headers (VS-REQ-015).
The system shall provide pagination controls for users to navigate through multiple pages of video stream search results (VS-REQ-016).

systemFeatures.name: Search Filtering and Preferences
systemFeatures.description: This feature enhances the user's ability to refine and personalize their search experience within the **video search** application. Users can apply various filters to exclude specific content, manage parental controls, or narrow down searches to preferred websites. This functionality improves the relevance of search results and empowers users to tailor the search environment to their individual needs and preferences. It contributes significantly to usability and user satisfaction, offering a customized and controlled content discovery process.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User accesses the filter options from the menu. Response: The system presents a dialog with various filtering criteria, including content type, age restrictions, and specific websites.

Stimulus: User selects filtering criteria (e.g., enables parental controls, specifies preferred websites). Response: The system applies the selected filters to all subsequent searches and updates currently displayed results.
Stimulus: User saves filter preferences. Response: The system stores the user's filtering selections for future sessions, ensuring consistent application of preferences.
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide a filter option in the menu bar to allow users to set parental controls and content restrictions (VS-REQ-017).
The system shall allow users to specify and filter out or only search certain websites (VS-REQ-018).
The system shall persist user filter preferences across sessions (VS-REQ-019).

systemFeatures.name: User Favorites Management
systemFeatures.description: The **video search** system offers users the capability to store links to their favorite videos. This feature addresses the common need for users to revisit content found during previous searches without the need to repeat the entire search process. By providing a dedicated 'Favorites' section, the system enhances user retention and convenience, allowing for easy access to preferred videos even after the application has been restarted. This feature adds significant personal value by creating a curated library of desired content.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects an option to add a video result to favorites. Response: The system stores the link and associated details of the video result in the user's favorites list.
Stimulus: User accesses the 'Favorites' option from the menu. Response: The system displays a list of all previously saved favorite video links and their details.
Stimulus: User selects a favorite video link. Response: The system opens the video's webpage in the default web browser, or removes the item if deletion is requested.
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide a 'Favorites' option in the menu bar to allow users to store links to their preferred videos (VS-REQ-020).
The system shall enable users to retrieve and view their stored favorite video links (VS-REQ-021).
The system shall allow users to remove stored video links from their favorites list (VS-REQ-022).

systemFeatures.name: Admin Search Source Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature empowers system developers to manage and update the list of websites that the **video search** application queries. It provides a secure interface for adding new sources, deactivating incompatible ones, or modifying existing entries based on criteria such as safety, performance, and relevance. This capability is crucial for maintaining the quality and breadth of search results, ensuring the system remains current and effective in a dynamic online environment. It directly supports the reliability and maintainability of the **video search** engine.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: An authorized developer accesses the search source management interface. Response: The system presents a list of all currently configured torrent and streaming websites, along with options to add, edit, or delete sources.
Stimulus: Developer adds a new search source. Response: The system validates the new source, adds it to the appropriate database (torrent or streaming), and makes it available for searches.
Stimulus: Developer deactivates or removes a search source. Response: The system updates the database, ensuring the deactivated source is no longer queried in subsequent searches.
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide a secure interface for authorized system developers to manage (add, edit, delete) the list of torrent and streaming websites (VS-REQ-023).
The system shall allow developers to categorize search sources as either 'torrent', 'streaming host', or 'streaming link' (VS-REQ-024).
The system shall enable developers to activate or deactivate individual search sources dynamically without requiring a full system redeployment (VS-REQ-025).

performanceRequirements: The system shall ensure that any query to an external website returns results within 5 seconds to provide a responsive user experience (VS-REQ-026).
The system shall open hyperlinks in the default browser within 1 second from user interaction, minimizing perceived delays (VS-REQ-027).
The system shall load and become fully operational within 10 seconds of program initiation, ensuring quick access for users (VS-REQ-028).
The system shall automatically filter out and not display any torrent results that have 0 seeds, as these cannot guarantee successful downloads (VS-REQ-029).
The system shall sort search results (torrent or streaming) in less than 0.1 seconds after a user clicks a column header, providing immediate feedback (VS-REQ-030).
The system shall display a maximum of 100 results per page, ensuring manageable data presentation and efficient loading times (VS-REQ-031).
The system shall filter out and not display any torrent results with an overall rating of less than 1, as such results are deemed untrustworthy by users based on ethnographic studies (VS-REQ-032).
safetyRequirements: The development team shall conduct a thorough monthly review of all websites listed in the **video search** database to ensure they do not expose users to illegal, harmful, or inappropriate content. This proactive measure aims to safeguard users and maintain the system's reputation as a secure and reliable platform.
securityRequirements: The **video search** system shall not store or maintain any user-specific data that could compromise privacy or security. It shall also not host any external content, thereby minimizing its attack surface and mitigating risks associated with content ownership or distribution. This approach ensures user anonymity and reduces the system's liability concerning data breaches.
softwareQualityAttributes: Usability: The system shall provide an intuitive and straightforward user interface, enabling users of varying technical proficiencies to easily navigate and utilize its search functionalities. This focus on ease of use enhances user adoption and satisfaction.

Reliability: The system shall consistently provide accurate and up-to-date search results by regularly updating its database of external sources and promptly filtering out non-functional links. This ensures that users can depend on the system for effective video discovery.

Maintainability: The system's architecture shall support modularity, allowing developers to easily update search source databases, introduce new filtering criteria, and fix bugs without significant re-engineering. This flexibility ensures the long-term viability and adaptability of the **video search** application.

Portability: The system shall be compatible with major operating systems (Windows, macOS, Linux) and popular web browsers, ensuring a broad user base can access its features without platform-specific limitations. This wide compatibility enhances market reach and user convenience.
businessRules: A comprehensive legal review of the **video search** software must be conducted prior to its public release to ensure full compliance with all relevant laws and regulations. This review is essential for establishing legal indemnification and mitigating potential liabilities associated with content linking.

The **video search** system shall strictly operate as a link aggregator, providing only references to external video streams and downloads without hosting any content on its own servers. This fundamental principle ensures the system remains within legal boundaries concerning intellectual property and content distribution.

glossary.definition: An instance of a **BitTorrent** client running on a computer connected to the internet, actively participating in data transfer for a **torrent** file.
A complete copy of a file being made available for download within a **BitTorrent** network, ensuring the file's availability to **peers**.

In the context of the internet, a file utilizing **BitTorrent** technology to host data for peer-to-peer (P2P) file download and sharing. It contains metadata about the files and their network locations.
Digital video content transmitted in a continuous flow over the internet, allowing users to watch it in real-time without fully downloading the file.
An external website (e.g., torrent site, video hosting site, video link site) from which the **video search** system retrieves search results.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart visualizes the primary process flow of the **video search** application, from user interaction to result display. Node IDs are alphanumeric, without spaces or reserved words, adhering to strict Mermaid syntax. Labels containing spaces are enclosed in double-quotes. The diagram follows a top-down (TD) direction. Please note that while the original document's Appendix B referred to a UI layout diagram, the allowed diagram types (Flowchart, Sequence, ERD) are not suitable for detailed user interface wireframes or mockups. This flowchart therefore focuses on the operational logic and user journey through the search process rather than specific UI elements.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
  StartProcess["User Launches **video search** App"]
  InputSearch["User Enters Search Term"]
  SelectTypes{"Select Search Types (Torrent/Stream)"}
  SearchButton["Click Search Button"]
  SearchLogic{ "Initiate Search?"}
  QueryTorrent["Query Torrent Sources"]
  QueryStream["Query Streaming Sources"]
  CombineFilter["Aggregate & Filter Results"]
  ResultsFound{"Results Available?"}
  DisplayResults["Display Search Results"]
  NoResults["Display 'No Results' Message"]
  EndProcess["End Process"]

  StartProcess --> InputSearch
  InputSearch --> SelectTypes
  SelectTypes --> SearchButton
  SearchButton --> SearchLogic
  SearchLogic -- "Yes" --> QueryTorrent
  SearchLogic -- "Yes" --> QueryStream
  QueryTorrent --> CombineFilter
  QueryStream --> CombineFilter
  CombineFilter --> ResultsFound
  ResultsFound -- "Yes" --> DisplayResults
  ResultsFound -- "No" --> NoResults
  DisplayResults --> EndProcess
  NoResults --> EndProcess
flowchartDiagram.caption: **video search** Main Search Process Flow

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram illustrates the temporal interactions between the user, the **video search** application, its internal databases, and external search sources during a typical video search operation. Participants are identified by simple, unquoted alphanumeric aliases. Messages clearly depict the flow of information and requests. Activations are implied by the `->>+` (send and activate) and `-->>-` (receive and deactivate) notations, ensuring proper lifecycle management of participants. No explicit `activate`/`deactivate` blocks are used inside control flow structures to adhere to strict compliance.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
  participant User as User
  participant VSApp as "**video search** App"
  participant TSourceDB as "Torrent Source DB"
  participant SSourceDB as "Stream Source DB"
  participant ExtTorrent as "External Torrent API"
  participant ExtStream as "External Stream API"

  User->>+VSApp: entersSearchTerm("query", torrentSelected, streamSelected)
  VSApp->>TSourceDB: getActiveTorrentSources()
  TSourceDB-->>VSApp: returnTorrentSources(sourceList)

  loop For each source in sourceList
    VSApp->>+ExtTorrent: queryTorrentSite(sourceURL, "query")
    ExtTorrent-->>-VSApp: returnTorrentResults(results)
  end

  VSApp->>SSourceDB: getActiveStreamSources()
  SSourceDB-->>VSApp: returnStreamSources(sourceList)

  loop For each source in sourceList
    VSApp->>+ExtStream: queryStreamSite(sourceURL, "query")
    ExtStream-->>-VSApp: returnStreamResults(results)
  end

  VSApp->>VSApp: processAndFilterAggregatedResults()
  VSApp-->>-User: displaySearchResults(filteredResults)
sequenceDiagram.caption: **video search** User Search Workflow

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) models the core data entities within the **video search** system, their attributes, and relationships. Entities with spaces in their names, like "SEARCH SOURCE TYPE", are enclosed in double-quotes. Attributes are defined with their data types and primary key (PK), foreign key (FK), or unique key (UK) constraints. Multiple keys are comma-separated for identifying relationships (e.g., PK,FK). Relationship cardinalities (e.g., one-to-many, one-to-zero-or-one) are explicitly indicated, and each relationship includes a descriptive, quoted label. The diagram strictly adheres to the rule that a foreign key implies a Many-to-One relationship from the FK-holding entity to the PK-holding entity, and avoids implicit Many-to-Many relationships without explicit junction tables.
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: **video search** Database Schema ERD

tbdList: VS-TBD-001: Consider adding a system to also search UseNet Binaries in future versions.

introduction.purpose: This document meticulously outlines the comprehensive software requirements for the **evla corr**, an essential component within the National Radio Astronomy Observatory's EVLA project. Its primary objective is to furnish a precise and exhaustive specification of functional and non-functional requirements. This specification will guide the system's design, development, and subsequent validation. The document aims to ensure clear communication and alignment among all project stakeholders regarding the system's capabilities and operational parameters.
introduction.documentConventions: This Software Requirements Specification adheres strictly to the guidelines established by the IEEE 830-1998 Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications. All requirements are systematically numbered to facilitate traceability and reference throughout the project lifecycle. Key terms and acronyms are formally defined within the glossary appendix to ensure consistent understanding across all stakeholders. Markdown bolding is selectively applied to enhance readability, highlighting critical system names, platforms, technologies, and specific application roles within the document.
introduction.intendedAudience: The principal audience for this Software Requirements Specification includes project leaders, system architects, software developers, and the operational end-users of the **evla corr**. This document also serves as a crucial reference for EVLA project scientists and engineers who require an understanding of the correlator's operational parameters and interfaces. Furthermore, it offers a valuable resource for individuals engaged in similar astronomical instrumentation projects, providing insight into the complex requirements for monitor and control systems.
introduction.productScope: The **evla corr** acts as the crucial interface between the WIDAR Correlator hardware and the broader EVLA Monitor and Control System. This system is responsible for the complete configuration, real-time operation, and diagnostic servicing of the correlator. Its scope encompasses the reception and translation of configuration data, processing and transfer of dynamic control and monitor data, autonomous fault detection and recovery, and limited real-time data processing for diagnostic purposes. The system is designed to provide comprehensive access for testing and debugging, ensuring the correlator's optimal performance and reliability within the EVLA array.
references: ANSI/IEEE Std 830-1998, IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.
ANSI/IEEE Std 1233-1996, IEEE Guide for Developing System Requirements Specifications.
EVLA Memo No. 15, Scientific Requirements for the EVLA Real-Time System.
EVLA Project Book.
EVLA System Requirements (SyRS).
EVLA Architecture and Design.
Refined EVLA WIDAR Correlator Architecture, NRC-EVLA Memo# 014, Brent Carlson, Oct. 2, 2001.
EVLA Correlator Monitor and Control System, Test Software, and Backend Software Requirements and Design Concepts, NRC-EVLA Memo # 015, Brent Carlson, Jan. 23, 2002.
EVLA Correlator Backend, Software Requirements Specification.

overallDescription.productPerspective:
The **evla corr** operates as an integral component within the larger EVLA Monitor and Control Structure, serving as the central hub for configuring, monitoring, and controlling the WIDAR Correlator hardware. It introduces a vital abstraction layer, effectively modularizing the complex correlator system within the expansive EVLA environment. The primary access point to the correlator's functionalities is facilitated through the **Virtual Correlator Interface (VCI)**, implemented as a software component residing on the **Master Correlator Control Computer (MCCC)**.

Architecturally, the **evla corr** is designed as a Master/Slave network, where the **MCCC** coordinates activities of multiple intelligent hardware control processors. The **MCCC** manages the bulk of external monitor/control interfaces, while the slave processors, such as **Correlator Monitoring and Interface Boards (CMIBs)**, focus on direct correlator hardware control. This distributed topology strategically places real-time computing demands at the slave layer and manages quasi-real-time, network-intensive loads at the master layer. This structure significantly enhances the isolation, serviceability, and programmability of the correlator hardware from the broader EVLA M&C environment. The system is engineered for high modularity and incorporates redundancy in critical operational areas to ensure continuous performance.
productFunctions: Receive and translate configuration data for the correlator hardware.
Process and transfer dynamic control data and essential monitor data.
Monitor correlator and subsystem health, initiating autonomous recovery actions for faults.
Perform real-time data processing and probing for diagnostic purposes.
Provide accessible interfaces and tools for system testing and debugging.
Manage data input for correlator parameters, including models and time standards.
Ensure robust and timely data output to the **Backend Data Processing System** and **EVLA M&C** network.
Implement comprehensive recovery mechanisms for **CMIB** and **MCCC** failures, including hot-swapping and failover capabilities.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: The Array Operator's primary contact with the **evla corr** will be via status and error messages channeled through the overall Monitor and Control System. This user class requires clear, concise, and time-referenced alerts and summaries to maintain high-level operational oversight and ensure continuous array operation. Their interactions focus on monitoring system health and verifying operational stability rather than direct low-level control.

Engineers and Technicians are responsible for the reliability and uptime of the Correlator System, involving corrective and preventive maintenance, along with performance testing and upgrades. This user class requires advanced tools to remotely inspect and monitor individual **CMIB** layer devices, perform detailed fault tracing to specific hot-swappable subsystems, and conduct diagnostic tests. Their interactions demand in-depth technical access and sophisticated diagnostic capabilities to ensure rapid diagnosis and repair of hardware and software faults.

Software Developers are primarily responsible for the development, testing, and troubleshooting of the **evla corr** software components. This user class requires remote access to the system, enabling them to troubleshoot and verify proper functionality away from the physical EVLA site and during non-working hours. Their demands include comprehensive access to system logs, configuration settings, internal states, and debugging interfaces to ensure the system's robustness and correct operation.

The Web User class consists of a limited number of authorized individuals who require restricted access to specific parts of the **evla corr**, typically for high-level monitoring or status checks. Access for this user class will be controlled, focusing on providing an overview of system health and key operational parameters through a web interface, rather than enabling direct control or detailed configuration changes.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment:
The **evla corr** is designed to operate within a highly specialized and critical astronomical data acquisition environment at the National Radio Astronomy Observatory. The system will run on robust, high-availability, general-purpose computers, specifically **Master Correlator Control Computers (MCCCs)** and **Correlator Power Control Computers (CPCCs)**, which may feature hot-swappable or redundant **CPU** devices. These primary control systems will interact with embedded **Correlator Monitoring and Interface Boards (CMIBs)**, conforming to **PC104+** standards, which directly interface with the correlator hardware via **PCI** or **ISA** buses. These **CMIBs** require the capacity to boot and operate a generic **COTS** operating system in a near real-time environment from local non-volatile storage.

Network communications within the **evla corr** system primarily utilize **100Base Ethernet (IEEE 802.3 compliant)**, with fiber optic pathways used for penetration into shielded rooms to mitigate **Radio Frequency Interference (RFI)**. Critical communication paths, such as between the **MCCC** and **CPCC**, also include redundant links like **RS-232C** for remote reboot capabilities. The entire system relies on **UPS** devices to ensure safe shutdowns during prolonged power outages. The operating environment necessitates a high degree of modularity, fault detection, and recovery mechanisms to ensure continuous operation and minimize astronomical data loss.
designAndImplementationConstraints: Criticality of the Application: The **evla corr** is a critical component in the astronomical data path; its unavailability leads to irreversible data loss. This mandates high reliability, availability, and robust fault-tolerance mechanisms, influencing all aspects of system design and implementation.

Computer Hardware Limitations: System functionality must be highly modular to facilitate fault detection and repair, ensuring the stability of the **CMCS** network and control computers. Hardware interfaces must conform to specific bus standards like **PCI** or **ISA** to integrate with existing correlator hardware.

Computer Software Limitations: The software must provide comprehensive, high-level data integration to offer users a logical and coherent interface, ensuring ease of use and flexibility while maintaining full access to system functionalities. This requires careful attention to software architecture and design patterns.

Network Protocols and Topology: All internal and external network interfaces must adhere to **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3 compliant)** standards, with a minimum data rate of **100 Mbits/sec**. Fiber optic pathways are mandated for **RFI** sensitive areas, and physical network isolation is required for **MCCC-CMIB**, **MCCC-CPCC**, and **MCCC-EVLA M&C** networks to prevent interference and enhance security.

Real-time Requirements: The system must support near real-time operations for correlator hardware control and monitoring, particularly for **CMIB** operating systems, to avoid data loss or corruption. This necessitates careful selection of operating systems and programming languages capable of meeting hard deadlines.

Redundancy and Self-Healing: Critical system areas, especially the **MCCC** and **CPCC**, must incorporate redundancy and self-healing capabilities to maintain service availability. Redundant communication paths are required between **MCCC** and **CPCC** to ensure system recovery and continuous operation even during primary link failures.

Power Management: All critical computers and peripherals must be powered through **UPS** devices with sufficient capacity for coordinated system-wide shutdowns during prolonged power outages, including signaling capabilities to the **CMCS**. This is a fundamental constraint for system safety and data integrity.
userDocumentation: The **evla corr** system will be delivered with comprehensive user documentation tailored to various user classes. This includes operational manuals for array operators, detailing routine monitoring procedures and high-level control instructions. Engineers and technicians will receive in-depth diagnostic guides and maintenance procedures, covering fault tracing, hardware replacement, and software upgrade processes. Furthermore, a detailed technical reference manual will be provided for software developers, outlining **API**s, internal system architecture, and debugging protocols. All documentation will be maintained and updated in conjunction with system revisions, ensuring accuracy and relevance to the deployed software version.
assumptionsAndDependencies: Configuration Data Stream: It is assumed that the correlator will receive configuration data from the **EVLA M&C System** in a format that is unambiguous, valid, and guarantees a convergent and physically achievable hardware configuration. Any deviations from this format or validity would impact system functionality.

Auxiliary Data Provision: All necessary auxiliary data, such as delay models, fiber round-trip phase corrections, time codes, and dynamic configuration data, will be consistently supplied by the **EVLA M&C System** or dedicated external servers. The availability and accuracy of this data are critical for the real-time update of correlator parameters.

Outgoing Data Stream Capacity: It is assumed that both the **Backend Data Processing System** and the **EVLA M&C System** possess the requisite capacity to accept the full range of output data rates generated by the **evla corr**, encompassing both required and on-demand data streams. Inadequate capacity could lead to data backlogs or loss.

Existing EVLA Infrastructure: The **evla corr** depends on the robust operation and communication capabilities of the overarching EVLA Monitor and Control System for its external interfaces and overall operational context. Failures or limitations in this external infrastructure will directly impact the correlator system.

Off-the-Shelf Components: It is assumed that Commercial Off-The-Shelf (**COTS**) operating systems and hardware components used within **CMIB**, **MCCC**, and **CPCC** systems will reliably meet the specified near real-time performance and stability requirements. Deviations in **COTS** product behavior could compromise system integrity.

Network Reliability: The underlying **100Base Ethernet** network infrastructure is assumed to provide reliable, high-bandwidth connectivity essential for inter-component communication and data transfer, with appropriate measures to manage network traffic. Network instability or bottlenecks would severely impact real-time operations.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces:
The **evla corr** system mandates several user interfaces to accommodate various access levels and operational needs. A primary graphical user interface (**GUI**) will be developed, residing on the **MCCC**, acting as the **Virtual Correlator Interface (VCI)**. This **VCI** will enable array operators and engineers to configure the correlator hardware through intuitive table structures, facilitating both routine operations and specific goal-oriented configurations. This interface will be accessible remotely for both operational control and diagnostic purposes.

Beyond the primary **VCI**, the system will provide specialized test software equipped with a dedicated **Graphical User Interface (GUI)**. This **GUI** will offer authorized users full remote access to all messaging, monitoring, and control traffic within the **CMCS** for comprehensive testing, debugging, and offline control of the correlator. Additionally, a command-line interface will be available at the **CMIB** level, providing low-level access for advanced diagnostics and maintenance by engineers and technicians. For a subset of authorized individuals, a **Web User** interface will offer restricted access to specific system parts, primarily for high-level monitoring and status checks.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces:
The **evla corr** system requires robust interfaces with underlying hardware components to ensure precise control and monitoring of the correlator. The **Correlator Monitoring and Interface Boards (CMIBs)** will establish communication with the correlator carrier boards primarily via either the **PCI** or **ISA** buses, with provisions for alternative serial or parallel connections as specific hardware demands. Each **CMIB** must be capable of reading a 16-bit identifier from its host correlator board, which is crucial for generating unique **IP** addresses and enabling hot-swap module functionality.

Furthermore, the **CMIB**s are required to read back the contents of all meaningful writeable hardware control registers, enabling comprehensive monitoring of the correlator hardware state for fault tolerance and diagnostics. The **CMIB** must also possess the capability to initiate hardware 'warm boots' upon external command from the **MCCC**. To provide immediate physical status, the carrier board for each **CMIB** shall incorporate an externally visible indicator, such as an **LED**, to visually display its operational status (e.g., red for fault, green for operational), assisting on-site technicians with rapid health assessments.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces:
The **evla corr** system integrates with several critical software components, necessitating well-defined interfaces. The **Master Correlator Control Computer (MCCC)** serves as the central hub, interfacing with the broader **EVLA Monitor and Control System** via **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3 compliant)**. This interface is responsible for receiving high-level correlator configurations and control instructions, which are then translated by the **Virtual Correlator Interface (VCI)** software module residing on the **MCCC**. The **MCCC** also manages the interface with the **Correlator Power Control Computer (CPCC)**, utilizing both **Ethernet** and a redundant **RS-232C** serial link for robust control and remote reboot capabilities.

Internally, the **MCCC** communicates with numerous **Correlator Monitoring and Interface Boards (CMIBs)** over a dedicated **Ethernet** network, transmitting specific hardware configuration tables and receiving monitor data. The **evla corr** system further provides specific data sets, such as state counts and auto-correlations, to the **Backend Data Processing System** over a secondary virtual network. Ancillary monitor data, including system health, error messages, and configuration echoes, are spooled for reliable transfer to the **EVLA M&C Network**, even during temporary communication disruptions. This ensures data integrity and continuous system visibility.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces:
Communication within the **evla corr** system and with external entities is predominantly governed by **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3 compliant)** protocols, ensuring a minimum data rate of **100 Mbits/sec**. The interfaces between the **CMIBs**, **MCCC**, and **CPCC** all adhere to this standard, leveraging transformer-coupled copper twisted pair connections for reliability, with provisions for other materials to mitigate **RFI** or accommodate long distances. Network switches are strategically employed within correlator racks to optimize traffic distribution and simplify wiring complexity.

The interface between the **MCCC** and the external **EVLA M&C** networks also utilizes **100Base Ethernet**, with critical pathways penetrating shielded rooms employing fiber optic or other low-**RFI** materials to meet stringent **RFI** specifications. Security at this interface is paramount, requiring network routers and/or switches to protect the **MCCC** from unauthorized access and irrelevant network traffic. A redundant communication path, specifically **RS-232C** or an equivalent serial connection, is mandated between the **MCCC** and **CPCC** to enable remote reboot functionalities, ensuring system recovery in the event of primary network or computing failures.

systemFeatures.name: Correlator Configuration and Control Management
systemFeatures.description:
This feature enables the **evla corr** to receive high-level configuration directives from the overarching **EVLA M&C System** and translate them into actionable, specific hardware configurations for the WIDAR Correlator. It encompasses both initial setup and dynamic adjustments, facilitating efficient operation and scientific observation. The system prioritizes accurate translation and robust application of control parameters to ensure optimal correlator performance.
Priority: High. This feature is fundamental for the correlator's operational readiness and ability to conduct scientific observations.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: **EVLA M&C System** sends a new correlator configuration request. Response: The **MCCC** receives the request, the **VCI** translates it into specific hardware configuration tables, and these tables are dispatched to the relevant **CMIBs** for hardware programming.

Stimulus: Authorized user accesses the **VCI GUI** to manually adjust correlator parameters. Response: The **VCI** processes the input, generates corresponding hardware configuration tables, and sends them to the **CMIBs**, updating the correlator in real-time.

Stimulus: **MCCC** attempts to configure a **CMIB** with new parameters. Response: The **CMIB** receives and applies the configuration, then reads back register contents to verify successful application, reporting status to the **MCCC**.
functionalRequirements: EC-REQ-001: The system shall receive correlator configuration information from the **EVLA M&C System**.
EC-REQ-002: The system shall translate received configuration information into specific, goal-oriented correlator hardware configuration tables via the **Virtual Correlator Interface (VCI)**.
EC-REQ-003: The system shall transfer dynamic control data, including models and filter parameters, to the correlator hardware.
EC-REQ-004: The system shall provide a human **GUI** interface for direct configuration of the correlator hardware via the **VCI**, utilizing the same table structures as external configurations.
EC-REQ-005: The **CMIB** shall be capable of writing and reading back the contents of all writeable hardware control registers.
EC-REQ-006: The **CMIB** shall accept external data feeds for models, time standards, and fiber-link phase corrections to package with control data.

systemFeatures.name: Correlator Health Monitoring and Data Output
systemFeatures.description:
This feature ensures continuous surveillance of the correlator's operational status and the efficient, reliable output of critical monitoring data. It provides system-wide visibility into all correlator system states, enabling both real-time operational feedback and comprehensive post-analysis. The design prioritizes robust data transfer and sophisticated filtering of status and error messages to enhance diagnostic capabilities.
Priority: High. This feature is essential for maintaining operational awareness and ensuring the quality and integrity of scientific data.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A **CMIB** detects a change in its supervised correlator hardware state. Response: The **CMIB** generates a monitor data packet, timestamps it, and transmits it to the **MCCC**.

Stimulus: **EVLA M&C System** or **Backend Data Processing System** requests specific monitor data. Response: The **CMCS** retrieves the requested data (e.g., auto correlation products, state counts) and transmits it in a timely fashion.

Stimulus: An error condition is detected by a **CMIB** or **MCCC** process. Response: An error message is generated, time-stamped, categorized, and made available to upper system levels, with options for content-controllable delivery and filtering.
functionalRequirements: EC-REQ-007: The system shall provide **EVLA** system-wide access to all correlator system states, including the **M&C** supervisor system state.
EC-REQ-008: The system shall provide time-synchronous monitor information as required by the **Backend**, monitor archive, and data archive systems.
EC-REQ-009: The system shall present other monitor information on a request basis.
EC-REQ-010: The system shall provide specific data sets (state counts, auto-correlations) to the **Backend Data Processing System** over a secondary virtual network in a timely and robust fashion.
EC-REQ-011: The system shall spool ancillary monitor data (system health, error messages, configuration echoes) to prevent loss during temporary network communication disruptions.
EC-REQ-012: The system shall allow for full control of data sample rates and contents via either the **EVLA M&C** or the **Backend** processing controller.
EC-REQ-013: All lower system error and debug messages shall be present at the **MCCC** layer, accessible without direct **CPU** access.
EC-REQ-014: All system error and debug messages shall be categorized logically to allow filtering by content, detail, and message rate.
EC-REQ-015: All messages passed between **CMCS** system layers shall include **UTC** and wall clock time stamp information.
EC-REQ-016: The **CMIB** carrier board shall have an externally visible indicator (**LED**) to provide a physical indication of **CMIB** operational status.

systemFeatures.name: System Fault Recovery and Self-Healing
systemFeatures.description:
This feature is critical for maintaining the continuous availability of the **evla corr**, enabling the system to autonomously detect and respond to various hardware and software failures. It incorporates self-monitoring, automated recovery attempts, and redundant component activation to minimize downtime and prevent data loss. The system is designed to provide robust failover capabilities and alert appropriate personnel when manual intervention is required.
Priority: High. This feature directly impacts system uptime and astronomical data integrity, making it paramount for reliable operation.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A **CMIB** subsystem fails or becomes unresponsive to health checks. Response: The **CMCS** attempts a reboot, and if unsuccessful, issues an alert notice to personnel while automatically restarting and reconfiguring the **CMIB** into the operational environment.

Stimulus: Internal software processes (watchdogs) detect a non-recoverable **MCCC** system failure. Response: The **CPCC** or external human intervention automatically activates the backup **MCCC** system, which takes over using maintained **CMCS** state information.

Stimulus: A power outage occurs, signaled by the **UPS** device. Response: The **CMCS** initiates a safe, system-wide shutdown of the correlator hardware, coordinating efforts across all affected components.
functionalRequirements: EC-REQ-017: The system shall attempt recovery from failure or hot-swapped hardware devices.
EC-REQ-018: If a **CMIB** subsystem fails and does not respond to reboot/self-heal attempts, the system shall issue an alert notice and automatically restart and reconfigure the **CMIB**.
EC-REQ-019: **MCCC** health shall be monitored by internal software watchdogs and external systems (**CPCC**).
EC-REQ-020: Upon non-recoverable primary **MCCC** failure, the backup **MCCC** system shall be automatically activated via the **CPCC** or human intervention.
EC-REQ-021: Both primary and secondary **MCCC** systems shall maintain full **CMCS** state information to enable seamless failover.
EC-REQ-022: Each computer system in the **CMCS** shall have a hardware-based watchdog timer configured to reboot the system in case of a system hang.
EC-REQ-023: Reboots caused by watchdog timers shall result in minimal system interruptions, with the **CPU** reconfiguring and returning to service autonomously.
EC-REQ-024: All computers and peripherals shall be powered through **UPS** devices with sufficient capacity for safe correlator hardware shutdown during prolonged power outages.
EC-REQ-025: **UPS** devices shall signal the **CMCS** upon power outage and apprise it of remaining backup power time.

EC-REQ-026: The **CMCS** shall be self-monitoring, capable of detecting, reporting, and automatically remedying or mitigating abnormal conditions such as hardware failure, OS hangs, environmental deviations, performance issues, and communication failures.
EC-REQ-027: The system shall be able to continue processing correlator configuration/control events until queues are exhausted and external communications are restored.

systemFeatures.name: Remote Access and System Maintenance
systemFeatures.description:
This feature provides authorized personnel with secure, comprehensive access to the **evla corr** for diagnostic, maintenance, and development activities. It supports remote logins for troubleshooting, facilitates software and hardware upgrades, and ensures that all system components are readily serviceable. The emphasis is on maintaining system health and enabling rapid fault resolution with minimal operational impact.
Priority: Medium. This feature is critical for the long-term maintainability, operability, and rapid problem-solving capabilities of the system.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: An authorized engineer attempts to remotely log in to a **CMCS** computer system. Response: The system authenticates the user and provides direct access for maintenance and monitoring.

Stimulus: A software developer needs to debug a **CMIB** application module. Response: The system allows simulation of inputs and outputs for the module, facilitating isolated debugging without affecting normal operations.

Stimulus: Technicians need to replace a faulty **CMIB** hardware module. Response: The module is readily accessible for removal and replacement, with the system capable of detecting the new hardware and assigning a unique **IP** address.
functionalRequirements: EC-REQ-028: All computers within the **CMCS** system shall provide authorized users with direct access for maintenance and monitoring through remote logins.
EC-REQ-029: Software shall be provided allowing authorized users full access to all messaging, monitor, and control traffic throughout the **CMCS** for testing, debugging, and offline control.
EC-REQ-030: A **Graphical User Interface** shall be provided for the **CMCS** test software, offering convenient and configurable remote access via the **VCI**.
EC-REQ-031: All system processing and interconnect hardware shall be readily accessible for maintenance, repair, replacement, and reconfiguration.
EC-REQ-032: All systems and application source code shall be available to or on the systems that execute it.
EC-REQ-033: All software application modules shall be debuggable, with inputs and outputs capable of simulation.
EC-REQ-034: All software processes shall be killable, restartable, debuggable, and testable with minimal impact on normal system operations.
EC-REQ-035: Software tools without available source code shall come with a complete diagnostic package and customer support.
EC-REQ-036: Operating system software shall either have source code available or be accompanied by comprehensive diagnostic tools and customer support.

performanceRequirements: EC-REQ-037: The **CMCS** processors shall be capable of meeting all current data processing deadlines and anticipated future requirements. This is critical to ensure that astronomical data is processed and controlled in real-time, preventing latency that could compromise scientific objectives.

EC-REQ-038: The **CMCS** processors shall respond to correlator hardware inputs (interrupts) in a deterministic fashion, providing sufficient performance to avoid data loss, corruption, or overflows. Determinism is paramount for the integrity and accuracy of highly sensitive astronomical measurements.

EC-REQ-039: The communication network between **CMIB**, **MCCC**, and **CPCC** shall maintain a data rate of **100 Mbits/sec** or better, ensuring rapid and efficient data transfer for real-time control and monitoring. This throughput is necessary to handle the volume of state changes and data streams generated by the correlator.

EC-REQ-040: The software part of the system shall perform without total system restart due to internal failure between system maintenance windows. This requirement ensures high operational availability and minimizes interruptions to astronomical observations.

EC-REQ-041: The system shall be capable of resuming operations from an idle standby mode with minimal delay. This ensures quick recovery and responsiveness for unscheduled or scheduled operational changes, reducing overall operational downtime.
safetyRequirements: EC-REQ-042: The system shall implement robust error detection and reporting mechanisms for all critical hardware and software components, providing timely alerts for abnormal conditions. This is essential to prevent system failures that could lead to physical damage to correlator hardware or loss of invaluable astronomical data, safeguarding against operational hazards.

EC-REQ-043: The system shall coordinate a safe, system-wide shutdown of the correlator hardware in response to prolonged power outages, utilizing **UPS** signals. This requirement is a critical safeguard against equipment damage and data corruption during unexpected power supply interruptions, ensuring graceful degradation and system integrity.

EC-REQ-044: The system shall employ redundant communication paths and failover mechanisms for critical control components, such as between the **MCCC** and **CPCC**, to ensure continuity of operations even during primary communication failures. This redundancy acts as a safety net, mitigating the risk of uncontrolled hardware states due to communication loss and ensuring system stability.
securityRequirements: EC-REQ-045: Network routers and/or switches shall be employed at the **MCCC-EVLA M&C** interface level (or higher) to protect the **MCCC** from unauthorized external access and irrelevant network traffic. This is crucial for safeguarding the integrity and confidentiality of the correlator control system from potential cyber threats and ensuring operational security.

EC-REQ-046: All computers within the **CMCS** system shall provide secure remote access, requiring authentication for authorized users for maintenance and monitoring. This ensures that only verified personnel can interact with critical system components, preventing unauthorized configuration changes, data access, or system manipulation.

EC-REQ-047: Access to parts of the system typically considered restricted shall only be granted to a few authorized individuals (e.g., **Web Users**), with appropriate access control mechanisms in place. This enforces a principle of least privilege, minimizing the attack surface for sensitive functionalities and reducing potential security vulnerabilities.
softwareQualityAttributes: EC-REQ-048: The **CMCS** shall be self-monitoring, capable of detecting, reporting, and automatically taking action to remedy or lessen the impact of abnormal conditions. This attribute ensures the system's ability to operate continuously without failure for extended periods, maximizing uptime for astronomical observations by proactively addressing issues.

EC-REQ-049: The software shall perform without total system restart due to internal failure between system maintenance windows. This guarantees sustained operation, reducing the frequency of disruptive reboots and minimizing interruptions to scientific data acquisition.

EC-REQ-050: The hardware shall perform indefinitely without complete loss of service, barring total failure of primary and backup power. This underscores the system's inherent robustness against component failures, contributing to overall system availability.

EC-REQ-051: The system shall be capable of continuing to process all correlator configuration/control events until queues are exhausted and external communications are restored. This ensures resilience against temporary communication disruptions, preventing immediate data loss and maintaining operational continuity.

EC-REQ-052: The system shall be able to sit at idle and resume operations with minimal delay. This highlights the system's readiness for immediate operational resumption, improving responsiveness to scientific observation scheduling.

EC-REQ-053: All system processing and interconnect hardware shall be readily accessible for maintenance, repair, replacement, and reconfiguration. This facilitates efficient physical maintenance, minimizing downtime and accelerating fault resolution.

EC-REQ-054: All systems and application source code shall be available to or on the systems that execute it. This enables in-depth software debugging and modification, crucial for long-term system evolution and problem-solving.

EC-REQ-055: All software application modules shall be debuggable, with inputs and outputs capable of simulation. This supports isolated testing and fault diagnosis, improving repair efficiency and ensuring module reliability.

EC-REQ-056: All software processes shall be killable, restartable, debuggable, and testable with minimal impact on normal system operations. This ensures that maintenance operations do not disrupt ongoing correlator functions unnecessarily, preserving scientific data collection.

EC-REQ-057: Software tools and pre-built applications without source code shall include a complete diagnostic package and customer support. This ensures that even third-party components can be effectively diagnosed and supported, enhancing overall system maintainability.

EC-REQ-058: Operating system software shall either have source code available or be provided with robust diagnostic support. This facilitates long-term system health and upgradeability, protecting against vendor lock-in or unresolvable issues.

EC-REQ-059: The **CMCS** is designed to accommodate anticipated future requirements for data processing and control, ensuring that the system can adapt to evolving scientific demands. This proactive design principle prevents early obsolescence and supports future expansion of the correlator's capabilities without extensive re-architecture.

EC-REQ-060: The system shall provide comprehensive test software with a **GUI** to allow full access to messaging, monitoring, and control traffic for debugging and testing, even when the correlator is offline. This ensures that all components and interfaces can be thoroughly validated, reducing the risk of defects in operational environments.
businessRules: EC-REQ-061: All data output to the **Backend Data Processing System** and **EVLA M&C System** must adhere to strict astronomical data formatting and timing standards. This ensures compatibility and interpretability with downstream scientific analysis pipelines, preserving the scientific utility of the data.

EC-REQ-062: Any corrective actions taken by the **CMCS** in response to faults must prioritize the preservation of ongoing astronomical observations over immediate full system restoration, if a graceful degradation path is available. This reflects the high value of continuous data acquisition in scientific research.

EC-REQ-063: Configuration changes initiated through the **VCI** must be validated against a set of predefined rules to ensure they result in a convergent and physically achievable hardware configuration. This prevents invalid states that could lead to equipment damage or unreliable operation, maintaining the integrity of the correlator.

otherRequirements: EC-REQ-064: The system shall support straightforward installation and upgrade procedures for both hardware and software components, minimizing downtime and complexity. This ensures that the system can be efficiently deployed and evolve with future requirements without significant operational overhead.

EC-REQ-065: The **CMIB** operating system shall support the ability to access and upgrade correlator hardware **PLD/FPGA** personalities through its network connection. This allows for remote updates to embedded firmware, enhancing maintainability and reducing the need for physical access for critical updates.

glossary.definition: An individual with unrestricted access to all aspects of the system.
A computer system that performs final real-time post-correlation processing on data received from the correlator baseline boards.
Correlator Monitor and Control System, referring to the **evla corr** system.
Correlator Monitoring and Interface Board, an intelligent hardware control processor.
Commercial Off-The-Shelf, referring to commercially available hardware or software products.
Correlator Power Control Computer, a general-purpose computer for power monitoring and control.
Central Processing Unit, in this context referring to a single board computer or computer system.
End-to-End System (archive), encompassing the entire data path from acquisition to archive.
The VLA Expansion Project, a major upgrade to the Very Large Array radio telescope.
Monitor and Control System, the overarching system managing the EVLA array.
Master Correlator Control Computer, the primary control system for the correlator.
Radio Frequency Interference, unwanted radio frequency signals that can degrade astronomical observations.
Monitor and control operations which have hard deadlines that when missed result in data corruption or loss.
Software Requirements Specification document.
System Requirements document.
Uninterruptible Power Supply, a device providing emergency power when the input power source fails.
Coordinated Universal Time, the primary time standard by which the world regulates clocks and time.
Virtual Correlator Interface, a software entity on the **MCCC** acting as the gateway to the correlator.
Wideband Digital Acquisition Receiver, the advanced digital correlator hardware used in the EVLA project.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: FORMAL SPECIFICATION: Flowcharts visualize processes and decisions using nodes and directed edges. Nodes can have various shapes to indicate different types of steps (e.g., `([])` for start/end, `[]` for process, `{}` for decision). `TD` (Top-Down) or `LR` (Left-Right) defines the layout direction. Node IDs must be alphanumeric and unique, and labels with spaces or special characters must be double-quoted (e.g., `N1["Node Label"]`).
flowchartDiagram.caption: EC_FLOW: Correlator Configuration and Monitoring Flow.

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: FORMAL SPECIFICATION: Sequence Diagrams illustrate the temporal order of interactions between participants. Participants are declared using `participant` (e.g., `participant P as ParticipantName`). Messages are sent using various arrow types (`->`, `->>`, `-->`, `-->>`) which can indicate synchronous or asynchronous communication. Activation bars (`+`/`-` or `activate`/`deactivate`) represent the period an object is performing an action. `alt`, `opt`, `loop` blocks show conditional, optional, or repetitive interactions. Messages with spaces or special characters must be double-quoted.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant EVLA_MC as "EVLA M&C System"
    participant MCCC as "MCCC (VCI)"
    participant CMIB as "CMIB Node"
    participant Correlator_HW as "Correlator Hardware"

    EVLA_MC->>+MCCC: "Request Configuration (Config_ID)"
    MCCC->>MCCC: "Translate Config via VCI"
    MCCC->>+CMIB: "Send Hardware Config Table"
    CMIB->>+Correlator_HW: "Apply Configuration Data"
    Correlator_HW-->>-CMIB: "Configuration Acknowledged"
    CMIB->>CMIB: "Verify Register State"
    CMIB-->>-MCCC: "Config Applied (CMIB_ID)"
    MCCC->>MCCC: "Update System State"
    MCCC-->>-EVLA_MC: "Configuration Complete (Config_ID)"
    
    alt Fault Detection
        CMIB->>MCCC: "Report Hardware Fault"
        MCCC->>MCCC: "Initiate Recovery Protocol"
        MCCC->>+CPCC: "Request MCCC Redundancy Check"
        CPCC-->>-MCCC: "MCCC Health OK"
        MCCC->>+CMIB: "Attempt CMIB Reboot"
        CMIB-->>-MCCC: "CMIB Reboot Status"
    end
sequenceDiagram.caption: EC_SEQUENCE: Correlator Configuration and Fault Handling.

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: FORMAL SPECIFICATION: Entity Relationship Diagrams (ERDs) model data structures by showing entities (representing tables) and their relationships. Entities are defined with `entityName { attributeType attributeName keyType "comment" }`. Cardinality (`||--o{`, `}|--||`, etc.) indicates the numerical relationship between entities (e.g., one-to-many). Relationship labels must be double-quoted. Only `PK` (Primary Key), `FK` (Foreign Key), and `UK` (Unique Key) are allowed for attribute keys. Attribute names must be alphanumeric without spaces or quotes.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
    "EVLA M&C System" ||--o{ MCCC : "configures"
    MCCC }|--|| CPCC : "manages power"
    MCCC ||--o{ CMIB : "controls"
    CMIB ||--o{ "Correlator Hardware" : "interfaces with"
    CPCC }|--|| MCCC : "monitors"

    MCCC {
        int mccc_id PK
        string name
        string ip_address UK
        bool is_primary
        datetime last_heartbeat
    }
    CMIB {
        int cmib_id PK
        int mccc_id FK
        string ip_address UK
        int hardware_identifier UK
        string status
    }
    CPCC {
        int cpcc_id PK
        string name
        string ip_address UK
        string power_status
    }
    "Correlator Hardware" {
        int hardware_id PK
        int cmib_id FK
        string type
        string operational_status
    }
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: EC_ERD: Correlator System Core Entities.

tbdList: EC-TBD-001: Actions to be taken by external systems upon hard failures of the **Correlator Power Control Computer (CPCC)**. (Section 2.2.5)
EC-TBD-002: The exact amount of minimal delay allowed for the system to resume operations from an idle standby mode. (Section 3.4.5)

introduction.purpose: The primary purpose of this document is to propose and specify the **sce api**, a standard **C/C++** modeling interface for emulators and other verification platforms. This interface aims to facilitate high-performance, multichannel communication between software models executing on a host workstation and a hardware device under test (DUT) represented as a structural hardware netlist on a verification platform. The **sce api** addresses the critical industry need for a unified approach to connect advanced verification platforms into SoC modeling environments, thereby maximizing performance potential and overcoming limitations of existing proprietary solutions.
introduction.documentConventions: This document adheres to specific conventions to ensure clarity and consistency in its presentation. Any references to actual literal names found in source code, identifiers that form part of the API, or filenames are represented using a `courier font`. Key concepts, important words, or phrases that require emphasis are frequently *italicized*. Readers encountering *italicized* text are encouraged to consult the glossary for a more detailed definition and understanding of the specific term.
introduction.intendedAudience: This Software Requirements Specification for the **sce api** is primarily intended for members of the Standard Co-Emulation API technical committee, comprising representatives from **EDA** customers and tool suppliers. Additionally, it serves as a vital reference for end users seeking to integrate software testbenches with hardware designs, transactor implementors developing reusable models, and **sce api** infrastructure implementors responsible for platform-specific API implementations. The document aims to provide a clear and comprehensive understanding of the API's functionality and design principles for all stakeholders involved in its adoption and development.
introduction.productScope: The scope of this document is strictly confined to the **sce api**: Modeling Interface (**SCE-MI**), which constitutes a fundamental part of the broader **sce api** standard. This modeling interface is specifically designed to provide multiple communication channels that bridge untimed software models with **RTL** or gate-level hardware designs under test. While the full **sce api** standard may encompass additional features such as debug, control, and code coverage in future revisions, these aspects are outside the current document's scope. The **SCE-MI** focuses solely on establishing a robust and high-performance communication conduit between the software and hardware verification domains.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.
Functional Specification for SystemC 2.0 - Synopsys, Inc.; CoWare, Inc.; Frontier Design, Inc.
CoWare N2C User Manual - CoWare, Inc.
IEEE Standard Hardware Description Language Based on the Verilog Hardware Description Language (IEEE Std 1364-1995) - IEEE Computer Society
IEEE Standard VHDL Language Reference Manual (IEEE Std 1076-1993) - IEEE Computer Society

overallDescription.productPerspective:
The **sce api** is a novel, self-contained product designed to address the escalating verification requirements within the Electronic Design Automation (**EDA**) industry. It functions as a critical abstraction layer, bridging the gap between high-level, untimed software models and cycle-accurate or **RTL** (Register Transfer Level) hardware designs executing on specialized verification platforms. Unlike existing proprietary Application Programming Interfaces (**APIs**), the **sce api** offers a standardized, open solution, thereby mitigating the challenges associated with porting software-based verification products across disparate emulation environments and improving overall productivity for System-on-Chip (**SoC**) design teams. 

This system is not intended to replace comprehensive software modeling and simulation environments like **SystemC**, but rather to augment them by providing a high-performance communication fabric. It specifically focuses on connecting software proxy models to message port interfaces embedded within the hardware side of the design. The **sce api**'s architecture is explicitly engineered to prevent communication bottlenecks that could otherwise severely compromise the performance of intrinsically fast emulators, ensuring that the full potential of these verification platforms is realized without undue throttling. 

At its core, the **sce api** facilitates a multichannel communication interface. It translates abstract messages from the software domain into concrete, clocked events for the hardware Device Under Test (**DUT**) and vice versa, through dedicated transactor models. This seamless interaction enables the creation of highly integrated co-emulation environments where complex software testbenches can drive and observe detailed hardware implementations, accelerating the verification cycle for advanced **SoC** designs.
productFunctions: Provide a standardized, multichannel communication interface for co-emulation.
Enable untimed software models to interact with **RTL** or gate-level hardware designs.
Offer message port proxies for software-side API access and message port macros for hardware-side integration.
Support abstraction bridging through user-defined transactors that serialize/deserialize messages.
Provide clock and reset generation and control mechanisms for the hardware verification platform.
Facilitate seamless operation in both single-threaded and multi-threaded **C/C++** environments.
Optimize communication to prevent performance bottlenecks on high-speed emulators.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: End users are primarily interested in quickly and effortlessly integrating their high-level, untimed algorithmic software testbenches with **RTL** or cycle-accurate hardware designs under test. They typically prefer 'plug-and-play' solutions, relying on pre-defined transactor and proxy models rather than engaging with the intricate details of the **sce api** itself. Their focus is on achieving functional verification efficiently, with minimal learning curve or implementation overhead for the communication interface.

Transactor implementors possess a deep understanding of the **sce api**'s exposed interfaces and protocols, though they are not concerned with the API's internal implementation details. Their primary responsibility is to develop and provide specialized 'abstraction gasket' models, comprising both hardware-side transactors and corresponding software-side proxy models.
