Stimulus: User navigates to the file manager, clicks 'Browser', then 'Go to extraction layout'. Response: The system opens an empty extraction interface, allowing manual archive selection.

Stimulus: User right-clicks a selected archive(s) in the file manager and chooses 'Extract' or 'Extract (in new folder)'. Response: The system initiates the extraction process, potentially prompting for a destination or creating a new folder.

Stimulus: User right-clicks a suitable archive(s) outside the application's GUI (e.g., in Windows Explorer) and chooses 'Extract', 'Extract here', or 'Extract here (in new folder)' from the 'Send to' menu. Response: The system launches **peazip** and begins the extraction process, using the context-menu parameters.

Stimulus: In the Extraction Interface, user specifies a destination folder and filename, and enters a password/keyfile if required. Response: The system proceeds with the decompression and extraction, placing the contents in the specified location.
functionalRequirements: P-REQ-034: The peazip system shall decompress the contents of archives in supported formats.
P-REQ-035: The peazip system shall allow users to specify the destination folder for extracted content.
P-REQ-036: The peazip system shall allow users to name the output folder for extracted content.
P-REQ-037: The peazip system shall handle conflicts (e.g., existing files) during extraction based on user-defined rules.
P-REQ-038: The peazip system shall require a correct password for extracting encrypted archives.
P-REQ-039: The peazip system shall require a valid keyfile for extracting keyfile-protected archives.
P-REQ-040: The peazip system shall preserve the original archive file after extraction.

systemFeatures.name: Archive Creation and Update
systemFeatures.description: This feature enables users to compress one or more selected files or folders into new archive volumes, or to update existing archives by adding new objects. Users can select from a range of compression formats offered by **peazip**, ensuring compatibility and efficiency for their specific needs. The ability to update archives provides flexibility for incremental backups and ongoing data management, avoiding the need to re-archive entire datasets. Technically, this requires integration with various compression engines, efficient handling of file streams, and robust metadata management for maintaining archive integrity. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects files or folders in the file manager. Response: Contextual options to 'Add to archive' or 'Create new archive' become available.
Stimulus: User clicks a 'Create archive' or 'Add' button in the toolbar. Response: The system opens the archive creation interface, pre-populating with selected items.

Stimulus: In the archive creation interface, user selects output format, compression level, and specifies a destination path and archive name. Response: The system begins compressing the selected objects into the new archive.
Stimulus: In the archive creation interface, user chooses to add a password or keyfile for encryption. Response: The system applies the selected encryption method during archive creation.
Stimulus: User selects an existing archive and chooses 'Update archive'. Response: The system opens the archive modification interface, allowing users to add or remove files.
functionalRequirements: P-REQ-041: The peazip system shall allow users to select multiple files and folders for archive creation.
P-REQ-042: The peazip system shall support creation of archives in all listed compression formats.
P-REQ-043: The peazip system shall allow users to specify the compression level for new archives.
P-REQ-044: The peazip system shall allow users to define the output directory and filename for new archives.
P-REQ-045: The peazip system shall provide options to encrypt new archives with a password.
P-REQ-046: The peazip system shall provide options to protect new archives with a keyfile.
P-REQ-047: The peazip system shall allow users to add new files or folders to an existing archive.
P-REQ-048: The peazip system shall allow users to remove files or folders from an existing archive.
P-REQ-049: The peazip system shall ensure the integrity of existing archives during update operations.

systemFeatures.name: Archive Browsing and Content Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature allows users to browse the internal contents of compressed archives without fully extracting them. Users can view individual files, their properties, and perform selective operations within the archive structure. This significantly saves time and disk space by enabling access to specific files without a full extraction, especially useful for large archives. Technically, this requires efficient parsing of various archive formats to build a virtual file system representation and capabilities to read specific data blocks on demand. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User double-clicks an archive file in the file manager. Response: The system opens the archive, displaying its internal file and folder structure in a dedicated browsing interface.
Stimulus: User navigates within the archive browsing interface. Response: The system updates the display to show the contents of selected internal folders.
Stimulus: User selects an individual file within an archive. Response: The system displays the file's properties (size, date, permissions) within the interface.
Stimulus: User right-clicks a file inside an archive and chooses 'Extract selected'. Response: The system extracts only the selected file to a specified destination.
Stimulus: User right-clicks a file inside an archive and chooses 'Open'. Response: The system extracts the file to a temporary location and opens it with its associated application.
functionalRequirements: P-REQ-050: The peazip system shall display the internal file and folder structure of a selected archive.
P-REQ-051: The peazip system shall allow navigation through directories within an opened archive.
P-REQ-052: The peazip system shall display properties of individual files contained within an archive.
P-REQ-053: The peazip system shall support selective extraction of individual files or folders from an archive.
P-REQ-054: The peazip system shall allow opening files directly from an archive by extracting them to a temporary location.

systemFeatures.name: Checksum and Hash Calculation
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides users with the ability to calculate a wide range of checksums and hash values for selected files. This is critical for verifying file integrity, detecting data corruption, and ensuring the authenticity of downloaded or transferred files. Users can compare calculated hashes against known values to confirm that a file has not been altered. From a technical perspective, this involves integrating various cryptographic hash functions (e.g., MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) and performing efficient byte-stream processing on large files. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects one or more files in the file manager. Response: A context menu option or toolbar button for 'Calculate checksum/hash' becomes available.
Stimulus: User selects 'Calculate checksum/hash'. Response: The system presents a dialog to choose the desired hashing algorithm (e.g., MD5, SHA-256).
Stimulus: User selects an algorithm and initiates calculation. Response: The system computes the hash value(s) for the selected file(s) and displays the results.
Stimulus: User compares the displayed hash value with a reference value. Response: User confirms or denies the integrity of the file.
functionalRequirements: P-REQ-055: The peazip system shall calculate MD5 checksums for selected files.
P-REQ-056: The peazip system shall calculate SHA-1 hash values for selected files.
P-REQ-057: The peazip system shall calculate SHA-256 hash values for selected files.
P-REQ-058: The peazip system shall support calculation of additional common cryptographic hashes (TBD).
P-REQ-059: The peazip system shall display the calculated checksums/hashes to the user.

systemFeatures.name: Archive Conversion and Benchmarking
systemFeatures.description: This feature enables users to convert archives between different supported formats and to benchmark the performance of various compression algorithms. Archive conversion provides flexibility for interoperability with different systems or software, while benchmarking helps users choose optimal settings for speed and compression ratio. This enhances the utility of the application by allowing adaptation to diverse requirements and informed decision-making regarding archiving strategies. Technically, this involves a sequence of extraction followed by re-compression, and standardized tests for measuring algorithm efficiency on the host system. Priority: Medium.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects an archive file in the file manager. Response: A context menu option or toolbar button for 'Convert archive' becomes available.
Stimulus: User selects 'Convert archive'. Response: The system presents a dialog to choose the target output format and compression settings.
Stimulus: User initiates conversion. Response: The system extracts the contents of the source archive and re-compresses them into the new format.
Stimulus: User selects 'Tools' then 'Compression Benchmark'. Response: The system runs a series of tests using different algorithms and compression levels.
Stimulus: Benchmark completes. Response: The system displays performance metrics (e.g., time, compression ratio) for each tested algorithm.
functionalRequirements: P-REQ-060: The peazip system shall convert selected archives from one supported format to another.
P-REQ-061: The peazip system shall allow users to specify the target format and compression settings for conversion.
P-REQ-062: The peazip system shall provide a compression benchmark tool to evaluate algorithm performance.
P-REQ-063: The peazip system shall display detailed results of compression benchmarks, including speed and compression ratio.

systemFeatures.name: User Preferences and Settings Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides users with extensive control over **peazip's** behavior, appearance, and operational parameters through a dedicated settings menu. Customization options include default compression levels, output paths, security settings, and interface themes. This empowers users to tailor the application to their specific workflows and preferences, significantly improving usability and efficiency. Technically, this requires a persistent configuration storage mechanism, a dynamic settings UI, and methods to apply changes globally or contextually throughout the application. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects 'Settings' or 'Options' from the main menu. Response: The system displays the **peazip** settings window.

Stimulus: User navigates through different categories in the settings window (e.g., 'Compression', 'Extraction', 'Security', 'Interface'). Response: The system displays relevant configuration options for the selected category.
Stimulus: User modifies a setting (e.g., default compression level). Response: The system updates the internal configuration for that setting.
Stimulus: User clicks 'Apply' or 'OK' in the settings window. Response: The system saves the modified settings and applies them to subsequent operations.
Stimulus: User clicks 'Cancel' or closes the settings window without saving. Response: The system discards any unsaved changes to the settings.
functionalRequirements: P-REQ-064: The peazip system shall provide a comprehensive settings interface for user preferences.
P-REQ-065: The peazip system shall allow users to configure default compression parameters (e.g., format, level).
P-REQ-066: The peazip system shall allow users to configure default extraction parameters (e.g., output path, overwrite rules).
P-REQ-067: The peazip system shall allow users to manage security settings, including default encryption methods and keyfile locations.
P-REQ-068: The peazip system shall allow users to customize interface options, such as themes and display modes.
P-REQ-069: The peazip system shall persist user settings across application sessions.
P-REQ-070: The peazip system shall apply updated settings immediately upon confirmation by the user.

systemFeatures.name: Real-time Monitoring and Command Line Integration
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides users with real-time graphical monitoring of all ongoing archiving and file management operations, offering transparency into process progress and status. Additionally, it supports the execution of any **peazip** function directly via command prompt commands, with the ability to extract and save these commands for scripting or automation. This enhances user control, allows for background processing, and facilitates integration into automated workflows, catering to both interactive and scripting-oriented users. Technically, this requires inter-process communication for monitoring and robust command-line argument parsing for external execution. Priority: Medium.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User initiates a long-running operation (e.g., large archive creation/extraction). Response: The system displays a real-time graphical progress monitor showing current status, speed, and estimated time remaining.
Stimulus: User performs an operation via the GUI. Response: The system concurrently logs the equivalent command-line command.
Stimulus: User selects an option to 'Show command-line' or 'Save command-line'. Response: The system displays or saves the corresponding command-line arguments and syntax for the current operation.

Stimulus: User executes a **peazip** command from the operating system's command prompt. Response: The **peazip** system performs the specified operation without launching the full GUI, reporting status via console output.
functionalRequirements: P-REQ-071: The peazip system shall display a real-time graphical progress monitor for ongoing operations.
P-REQ-072: The peazip system shall show progress percentage, current speed, and estimated time remaining for active tasks.
P-REQ-073: The peazip system shall generate and display the equivalent command-line syntax for any GUI-initiated operation.
P-REQ-074: The peazip system shall allow users to save generated command-line commands to a text file.
P-REQ-075: The peazip system shall execute any core functionality via command-line interface without requiring the graphical user interface.

performanceRequirements: P-REQ-076: The peazip system shall achieve a minimum compression speed of 50 MB/s for typical file sets (e.g., documents, images) when using 'Fast' compression settings on a modern **x-86 CPU** with at least 4GB of RAM. Rationale: Users expect quick operation, especially for everyday archiving tasks, to maintain productivity. Slower speeds would lead to frustration.

P-REQ-077: The peazip system shall maintain a maximum **RAM** usage of 256 MB for archives up to 1 GB in size when performing standard compression or extraction operations. Rationale: Efficient memory usage is crucial to prevent system slowdowns or crashes, particularly on systems with limited resources, ensuring a smooth user experience.

P-REQ-078: The peazip system shall complete archive integrity checks for a 10 GB archive within 5 minutes on a standard **SSD**-equipped system. Rationale: Timely verification of archive integrity is essential for data reliability, especially for large backups, providing users with quick assurance of their data's health.
safetyRequirements: P-REQ-079: The peazip system shall implement robust error handling mechanisms to prevent data loss or corruption during file manipulation (e.g., secure deletion, splitting, merging). Rationale: Data integrity is paramount; any operation that could compromise user files must be safeguarded with explicit checks and recovery paths.

P-REQ-080: The peazip system shall ensure that during archive extraction, the original archive file remains unchanged and is not deleted. Rationale: This protects against accidental loss of the source archive, providing a fallback in case of issues with the extracted content or destination.

P-REQ-081: The peazip system shall provide clear warnings to the user before performing irreversible actions, such as secure file deletion or overwriting existing files. Rationale: Users must be fully aware of the consequences of their actions, allowing them to confirm or cancel potentially destructive operations, thus enhancing user safety and preventing unintended data removal.
securityRequirements: P-REQ-082: The peazip system shall encrypt archives using industry-standard algorithms (e.g., **AES-256**) when a password is provided, to protect sensitive data from unauthorized access. Rationale: Robust encryption is fundamental for protecting confidential information stored in archives, meeting user expectations for data privacy and security.

P-REQ-083: The peazip system shall enforce a minimum password strength policy for archive encryption, requiring a combination of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols. Rationale: Weak passwords are a common security vulnerability; enforcing strength minimizes the risk of brute-force attacks against encrypted archives.

P-REQ-084: The peazip system shall securely handle keyfiles for two-factor authentication, ensuring they are not stored in plaintext or easily accessible locations. Rationale: Keyfiles provide an additional layer of security, and their secure handling is critical to prevent compromise of the two-factor mechanism, protecting archives even if passwords are leaked.

P-REQ-085: The peazip system shall perform all operations within the user's defined file permissions, preventing unauthorized access or modification of system files. Rationale: Adhering to operating system security models prevents privilege escalation and unauthorized changes, maintaining system stability and security.
softwareQualityAttributes: P-REQ-086: **Usability**: The peazip system shall feature an intuitive graphical user interface (GUI) that enables new users to perform basic archiving and extraction tasks within 5 minutes of their first interaction, without consulting documentation. Rationale: A high level of usability reduces the learning curve and increases user adoption, making the application accessible to a broad audience.

P-REQ-087: **Reliability**: The peazip system shall successfully complete 99.9% of all archiving and extraction operations without error, assuming valid input files and sufficient system resources. Rationale: Reliability is crucial for a file management tool; users depend on it for accurate and consistent data handling, making data corruption or unexpected failures unacceptable.

P-REQ-088: **Maintainability**: The peazip system's codebase shall adhere to established **Delphi/Kylix** and **Object Pascal** coding standards, with comprehensive inline documentation, allowing a new developer to understand and modify a core module within 5 days. Rationale: High maintainability facilitates ongoing development, bug fixes, and feature enhancements, crucial for an open-source project with community contributions.

P-REQ-089: **Portability**: The peazip system shall be executable on **Microsoft Windows** (32-bit and 64-bit) and **POSIX**-compliant operating systems (e.g., **Linux**, **BSD**) without requiring recompilation or significant environmental adjustments. Rationale: Cross-platform compatibility broadens the user base and allows the application to be deployed in diverse computing environments, maximizing its utility.

P-REQ-090: **Flexibility**: The peazip system shall allow users to customize all primary operational parameters, including compression algorithms, encryption methods, and default output paths, through a centralized settings interface. Rationale: Flexibility empowers users to tailor the application to their specific needs and workflows, enhancing efficiency and user satisfaction across varied use cases.
businessRules: P-REQ-091: The peazip software shall be licensed under the **LGPL** (Lesser General Public License), ensuring it remains 100% Open Source and free for use, distribution, and modification. Rationale: Adherence to **LGPL** promotes community involvement, transparency, and broad accessibility, aligning with the project's open-source ethos.

P-REQ-092: All contributions to the peazip project, including code and documentation, shall be submitted under terms compatible with the **LGPL** license. Rationale: This ensures that the entire project ecosystem remains open and consistent, preventing licensing conflicts and maintaining the collaborative development model.

P-REQ-093: The peazip project shall maintain its primary development environment using **Lazarus IDE**. Rationale: Standardizing the development environment simplifies collaboration, toolchain management, and ensures consistency in the build process for all contributors.

otherRequirements: P-REQ-094: The peazip system shall support multi-core CPU architectures to enhance performance for computationally intensive tasks like high-level compression. Rationale: Leveraging modern hardware capabilities is essential for competitive performance in archiving, delivering faster processing times for users with multi-core systems.

P-REQ-095: The peazip system shall provide a mechanism for users to submit bug reports and feature requests directly from the application's help menu. Rationale: A direct feedback channel encourages user engagement and provides valuable input for continuous improvement and issue resolution.

glossary.definition: The general-purpose file and archive manager application this document specifies.
A file that contains one or more files and/or directories, usually in a compressed format, for storage or transfer.
The process of encoding information using fewer bits than the original representation, reducing file size.
The process of decompressing and retrieving files from an archive to their original uncompressed state.
An additional security measure, a small file used in conjunction with a password for two-factor authentication to access encrypted archives.
Lesser General Public License; a free software license that permits linking with proprietary software.
Graphical User Interface; a visual interface allowing users to interact with electronic devices through graphical icons and visual indicators.
A small-sized datum derived from a block of digital data for detecting errors that may have been introduced during its transmission or storage.
Application Programming Interface; a set of definitions and protocols for building and integrating application software.
The part of a software system that interacts with the user directly, translating user input into commands for the backend.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart visualizes the process of creating a new archive in peazip. Nodes are identified by alphanumeric IDs and display their labels in double quotes if they contain spaces. Decision nodes are represented by diamonds, start/end nodes by rounded rectangles, and process steps by rectangles. The flow direction is from top to bottom (TD), showing the logical sequence of user actions and system responses. All IDs are alphanumeric, and labels are correctly quoted. Reserved words are avoided.
flowchartDiagram.caption: peazip Archive Creation Process Flow

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram illustrates the workflow for archive extraction in peazip. Participants represent distinct entities in the system, with the user initiating the process. Messages indicate interactions and data flow between participants, showing the temporal order of operations. Standard synchronous (->>) and asynchronous (->) messages are used. An 'alt' block is used to represent conditional flows based on whether an archive is encrypted or not. Aliases are used for brevity without quoting their IDs.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
  participant User as "End User"
  participant GUI as "peazip GUI"
  participant Engine as "Archiving Engine"
  participant FS as "File System"

  User->>GUI: Select archive for extraction
  GUI->>FS: Request archive metadata (P-REQ-034)
  FS-->>GUI: Return archive metadata

  alt Archive is Encrypted
    GUI->>User: Prompt for Password/Keyfile
    User->>GUI: Provide Password/Keyfile (P-REQ-038, P-REQ-039)
    GUI->>Engine: Send archive & credentials for decryption
    Engine->>Engine: Decrypt archive contents
  else Archive is Not Encrypted
    GUI->>Engine: Send archive for extraction
  end

  Engine->>Engine: Decompress contents
  Engine->>GUI: Indicate extraction readiness
  GUI->>User: Prompt for destination path (P-REQ-035)
  User->>GUI: Specify destination path
  GUI->>FS: Request creation of destination folder
  FS-->>GUI: Confirm folder creation
  Engine->>FS: Write extracted files to path
  FS-->>Engine: Confirm file write completion
  Engine->>GUI: Notify extraction complete
  GUI->>User: Display success message
sequenceDiagram.caption: peazip Archive Extraction Workflow

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) models the core data entities within the peazip system. Entities are represented by rectangles with their attributes listed below. Each attribute specifies its type and key constraint (PK for Primary Key, FK for Foreign Key, UK for Unique Key). Relationships between entities are indicated by lines with crow's foot notation for cardinality and labels describing the nature of the relationship, enclosed in double quotes. All entity names are uppercase, attribute names are alphanumeric, and relationship labels are quoted.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
  USER ||--o{ ARCHIVE : "creates"
  ARCHIVE ||--|{ FILE : "contains"
  ARCHIVE }|--|| FORMAT : "uses"

  USER {
    int user_id PK
    string username
    string email UK
    datetime created_at
  }

  ARCHIVE {
    int archive_id PK
    int user_id FK
    string name
    string path
    decimal size
    datetime created_at
    string format_type FK
    boolean is_encrypted
  }

  FILE {
    int file_id PK
    int archive_id FK
    string filename
    string original_path
    decimal size
    datetime last_modified
  }

  FORMAT {
    string format_type PK
    string description
    boolean supports_compression
    boolean supports_encryption
  }
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: peazip Core Data Model

tbdList: P-REQ-058: The peazip system shall support calculation of additional common cryptographic hashes (TBD).

introduction.purpose: The primary objective of this Software Requirements Specification (SRS) is to precisely delineate the functional and non-functional requirements for the **inventory** system. This document serves as a foundational reference for all stakeholders, ensuring a unified understanding of the system's capabilities and constraints. It aims to integrate multiple faculty databases into a cohesive web-based interface, providing secure access and management functionalities for university assets. The **inventory** system will streamline asset tracking, transfer, and request processes across the academic institution.
introduction.documentConventions: This document adheres to the guidelines set forth by the IEEE 830-1998 standard for Software Requirements Specifications. All requirements are articulated using clear, unambiguous language, prioritizing business terminology where appropriate, with minimal use of highly technical jargon. Key terms, acronyms, and abbreviations are defined in the accompanying Glossary. This structured approach facilitates comprehension across diverse stakeholder groups, including technical developers, project managers, and end-users.
introduction.intendedAudience: This SRS is intended for a diverse audience, including software developers, quality assurance engineers, project managers, and client stakeholders. Developers will utilize it as a blueprint for system design and implementation, while QA engineers will base their test plans on the specified requirements. Project managers will rely on this document for project planning, scope management, and progress monitoring. Client representatives will leverage it to confirm that the developed **inventory** system aligns with their business needs and operational expectations.
introduction.productScope: The **inventory** system will provide a comprehensive web-based interface for managing university assets, including rooms and spaces, software licenses, and various physical items. Its scope encompasses core operations such as adding, editing, modifying, and transferring assets, along with managing requests for borrowing or reserving assets and spaces. The system will support a hierarchical university structure for asset ownership and approval workflows, featuring robust user authentication and a granular permission system. It will also facilitate the generation of various reports to aid in asset oversight and management.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.
Shari Lawrence Peeger and Joanne M. Atlee. Software Engineering: Theory and Practice. Prentice Hall, fourth edition, 2009. ISBN: 978-0-13-606169-4.
Object-Oriented Software Engineering: Practical Software Development using UML and Java, Timothy Lethbridge, ISBN: 0077109082 Publisher: Mcgraw-Hill Edition: 2
The Elements of UML 2.0 Style Cambridge University Press, 2005 ISBN: 0-521-61678-6 http://www.agilemodeling.com/style/useCaseDiagram.htm
Class diagram – Wikipedia http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Class_diagram
Sequence Diagram - Wikipedia http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sequence_diagram

overallDescription.productPerspective: The **inventory** system is a new, self-contained product designed to replace disparate data management practices across different university faculties. It integrates existing faculty databases into a unified platform, offering a centralized view and control over all university assets. This system serves as a critical component in the university's digital transformation strategy, aiming to standardize inventory management processes. The **inventory** system's primary goal is to enhance efficiency, reduce manual errors, and provide real-time insights into asset utilization and availability.
productFunctions: Facilitate secure user authentication and authorization.
Enable creation, modification, and editing of various asset types.
Manage asset transfer processes within and between university departments and faculties.
Allow users to create requests for borrowing assets or reserving spaces.
Implement a multi-level approval workflow for asset requests.
Support the creation and management of new university locations or spaces.
Provide functionalities for processing returned assets and updating inventory status.
Enable administrators to delegate and modify user permissions.
Generate comprehensive reports on assets, requests, and user permissions.
Provide robust search capabilities for inventory assets.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: These users possess the highest administrative privileges, having complete control over the entire **inventory** across all faculties and departments. They can approve any type of inventory transaction and manage permissions at all levels. Typically, they are senior management personnel requiring comprehensive oversight and strategic control over university resources.

Faculty Administrators have extensive control over the inventory within their specific faculty, including all associated departments. They can approve inter-departmental transfers within their faculty and manage permissions for department-level users. These users are typically faculty deans or directors, requiring a detailed understanding of their faculty's resource allocation and needs.

These users manage the **inventory** specifically within their assigned department. They can approve transfers within their department and modify assets belonging to it. Department Administrators are usually department heads or designated staff responsible for day-to-day asset management and immediate resource allocation. They have a more focused scope compared to higher-level administrators.

These users are delegated specific tasks and permissions by any administrative level (Level 1, 2, or 3). Their permissions can vary widely based on assigned tasks, allowing them to work on specific applications or perform particular actions. They may be staff members, graduate assistants, or other personnel assigned operational duties within the **inventory** system.

This class primarily includes students and professors. Their main interaction with the system involves creating requests to borrow assets or reserve spaces. They have limited privileges, primarily focused on initiating requests and viewing their status. These users typically have basic technical proficiency and require an intuitive interface for quick request submission.

These users possess the highest technical privileges, controlling all aspects of the **inventory** system, including data base servers locally. They can create new spaces, modify floor structures, and define/assign new permission groups. This class consists of highly technical personnel responsible for system maintenance, security, and infrastructure management.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The **inventory** system is designed to operate as a web-based application, accessible via standard web browsers. It will be hosted on servers that support both **Microsoft** and **Unix** platforms, ensuring flexibility in deployment. This multi-platform compatibility minimizes infrastructure-specific constraints and allows for broader adoption within the university's existing IT landscape. The system's architecture will be robust enough to handle simultaneous access from numerous users across various administrative and academic departments.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The system must integrate with existing databases from three distinct faculties, requiring careful consideration of data migration, synchronization, and schema mapping. Data integrity and consistency across these integrated sources are paramount. The design must accommodate diverse data structures while presenting a unified view to the end-users.

The application must maintain continuous availability during working hours, with any maintenance or backup operations scheduled outside these periods. This necessitates a resilient architecture capable of rapid recovery and fault tolerance. Database queries should be optimized to complete within one minute to prevent system slowdowns and ensure responsiveness.

The user interface must adhere to principles of clarity and consistency, employing terminology that is easily understood by users with basic internet and office application experience. This is crucial given that many tasks may be delegated to working students who require a minimal learning curve for efficient operation. The maximum learning time for basic functionalities should not exceed 2 to 4 hours.

The system must implement robust security measures, including user authentication via username and password for all users. Permissions must be assigned strictly according to predefined user roles and administrative levels. Direct local access to database servers is restricted solely to the IT team, ensuring data security and preventing unauthorized access.
userDocumentation: The **inventory** system will be accompanied by a comprehensive online help system accessible through the web interface, providing context-sensitive assistance. A user manual will be provided, detailing all functionalities, workflows, and administrative procedures for different user roles. Additionally, short tutorials or quick-start guides will be available for common tasks such as creating requests or modifying assets, aimed at reducing the learning curve for new users.
assumptionsAndDependencies: It is assumed that the underlying hosting system and servers will provide sufficient resources (CPU, memory, storage, network bandwidth) to support all contemplated operations of the **inventory** system without performance degradation. This includes handling peak loads, concurrent user access, and complex database queries efficiently. The stability and availability of the hosting environment are critical to the system's operational success.

The successful implementation of the **inventory** system is dependent on the cooperation and active participation of IT teams from all three faculties during the integration phase. Timely access to existing database schemas, data samples, and any relevant API documentation from these faculties is crucial. Any changes in the existing faculty database structures or access policies must be communicated promptly to the development team.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: The **inventory** system will feature a web-based graphical user interface designed for intuitive navigation and ease of use. The interface will adhere to a consistent visual style and layout across all modules, utilizing clear, unambiguous terminology suitable for users with varied technical backgrounds. Key interface elements will include a centralized dashboard providing an overview of pending requests and owned assets, dedicated forms for asset creation and modification, and a robust search bar supporting both simple and advanced queries. Standard features such as login/logout, help menus, and notification displays will be present on all relevant screens. Error messages will be descriptive and guide users toward resolution.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The **inventory** system will interact with standard client hardware components such as monitors, keyboards, and pointing devices through the web browser interface. For administrative and IT team members, the system may implicitly interface with dedicated administrative computers and local database servers for maintenance tasks, although direct hardware control is abstracted. There are no direct, custom hardware interfaces explicitly required by the software itself, relying instead on the operating system and network stack for hardware communication. Communication with any external hardware will be through standard network protocols.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: The **inventory** system will interface with various external software components. This includes existing faculty databases (e.g., **Oracle**, **MySQL**, **PostgreSQL**) for initial data ingestion and ongoing synchronization, necessitating robust data connectors and potential ETL processes. An external authentication system (e.g., **LDAP**, **OAuth 2.0**) will be used to manage user identities and authentication, ensuring secure access. The system will also rely on standard web servers (e.g., **Apache HTTP Server**, **Nginx**) and application servers (e.g., **Node.js**, **Java EE**) for deployment and execution. Integration with an email service will be required for user notifications regarding request statuses and approvals. All data exchanges between these components will utilize well-defined **REST API** endpoints to ensure interoperability and maintainability.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: The **inventory** system will primarily rely on **HTTP/S** for secure communication between client web browsers and the server. All sensitive data transmissions, including user credentials and asset information, will be encrypted using **TLS/SSL** protocols to ensure confidentiality and integrity. The system will also utilize **SMTP** for sending email notifications to users regarding various system events, such as request approvals or rejections. Network communication will need to be configured to support data transfer rates sufficient for responsive user interaction, even with bulk data operations. Mechanisms for handling network latency and potential disconnections will be implemented to enhance user experience.

systemFeatures.name: User Authentication
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides a secure mechanism for all users to log into the **inventory** system, ensuring that only authorized individuals can access system functionalities based on their assigned roles and permissions. From a user's perspective, this means a straightforward login process, while technically, it establishes a foundational security layer for all subsequent interactions and data access within the system. Its priority is High as it is a prerequisite for all other system operations.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User navigates to the login page. Response: The system displays the login form requesting username and password.
Stimulus: User enters credentials and clicks 'Login'. Response: The system verifies credentials with the authentication service and grants access or displays an error message for invalid attempts.
functionalRequirements: I-REQ-001: The system shall provide a user authentication interface requiring a username and password.
I-REQ-002: The system shall validate user credentials against the configured authentication service.
I-REQ-003: The system shall grant access to the main application page upon successful authentication.
I-REQ-004: The system shall display an informative error message for failed authentication attempts.

systemFeatures.name: Manage User Permissions
systemFeatures.description: This feature allows administrative users to view, edit, and delegate specific permissions to other users within their scope of authority. It is essential for maintaining granular control over who can perform what actions in the **inventory** system, enhancing security and operational flexibility. Users benefit from clearly defined roles that prevent unauthorized access while enabling necessary task delegation. Technically, this feature manages the complex relationships between users, roles, and individual permissions, ensuring that privilege assignments are consistently enforced across the system. Its priority is High due to its critical role in system security and administrative control.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Administrator navigates to the 'Change Permissions' section. Response: The system displays a list of users and their current permissions within the administrator's scope.
Stimulus: Administrator selects a user and modifies their assigned permissions. Response: The system updates the user's permissions and enforces them immediately.
Stimulus: Administrator attempts to assign permissions exceeding their own authority. Response: The system displays an error message and prevents the unauthorized modification.
functionalRequirements: I-REQ-005: The system shall allow University, Faculty, and Department Administrators to view users and their assigned permissions within their respective organizational scope.
I-REQ-006: The system shall enable administrators to modify the permissions of selected users, delegating specific authorized actions.
I-REQ-007: The system shall ensure that an administrator cannot assign permissions that they do not possess.
I-REQ-008: The system shall update user roles to 'Inventory Administrator' when permissions are delegated.
I-REQ-009: The system shall notify the modified user of their updated permissions via email.

systemFeatures.name: Manage Inventory Assets
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides comprehensive capabilities for adding, editing, and modifying assets within the **inventory** system. It supports various asset types—rooms, software licenses, and general assets—and allows for bulk operations, significantly enhancing efficiency for inventory managers. Users benefit from a streamlined process for maintaining accurate and up-to-date asset records. From a technical standpoint, this involves robust data management for asset properties, categorization, and ownership, ensuring data integrity across the integrated databases. Its priority is High, as it forms the core functionality for asset lifecycle management.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Administrator initiates adding a new asset. Response: The system presents a form to select asset type, location, owner, and fill in properties.
Stimulus: Administrator fills asset properties and submits the form. Response: The system adds the new asset to the inventory and updates the database.
Stimulus: Administrator selects an existing asset for editing. Response: The system displays all editable fields of the asset, excluding IDs.
Stimulus: Administrator modifies asset properties and saves changes. Response: The system updates the asset record in the database.
Stimulus: Administrator uploads a bulk entry file for assets. Response: The system processes the file, adding or modifying multiple assets as specified.
functionalRequirements: I-REQ-010: The system shall allow authorized users to add new inventory assets by specifying asset type, location, and ownership.
I-REQ-011: The system shall support three primary asset classifications: Rooms and spaces, Software licenses, and All other assets.
I-REQ-012: The system shall allow authorized users to group assets, such as computer parts.
I-REQ-013: The system shall allow authorized users to modify all fields of an existing asset, excluding its unique identifier.
I-REQ-014: The system shall support bulk entry of new assets and bulk modification of existing assets via a file upload.
I-REQ-015: The system shall ensure that Department Administrators can only add/modify assets owned by their department.
I-REQ-016: The system shall ensure that Faculty Administrators can add/modify assets for all related departments within their faculty.
I-REQ-017: The system shall ensure that University Administrators can add/modify all assets in the inventory.
I-REQ-018: The system shall update the inventory database immediately upon successful asset addition or modification.

systemFeatures.name: Manage Asset Transfers
systemFeatures.description: This feature facilitates the controlled movement of assets within and between different organizational units of the university. It includes specific workflows for inter-departmental, inter-faculty, and external transfers, each requiring appropriate levels of approval to maintain accountability and accurate asset tracking. Users benefit from a structured process for reallocating resources, while administrators maintain oversight through defined approval chains. Technically, this involves updating asset location and ownership records based on approved transfer requests. Its priority is High to ensure proper asset governance and tracking.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User initiates an asset transfer within the same department. Response: The system directly updates the asset's location without requiring an explicit request or approval.

Stimulus: User initiates an inter-departmental asset transfer. Response: The system creates a request requiring approval from a Department Administrator and a Faculty Administrator, unless initiated by a higher-level administrator.
Stimulus: User initiates an inter-faculty asset transfer. Response: The system creates a request requiring approval from a Faculty Administrator or a higher-level administrator.
Stimulus: User initiates an asset transfer outside the university. Response: The system creates a request requiring approval from a University Administrator.
functionalRequirements: I-REQ-019: The system shall allow direct update of asset location for transfers within the same department by authorized users.
I-REQ-020: The system shall require approval from a Department Administrator and a Faculty Administrator for inter-departmental asset transfers.
I-REQ-021: The system shall require approval from a Faculty Administrator or higher for inter-faculty asset transfers.
I-REQ-022: The system shall require approval from a University Administrator for asset transfers outside the university.
I-REQ-023: The system shall update the asset's location and ownership records upon approval of a transfer request.

systemFeatures.name: Manage Asset Requests
systemFeatures.description: This feature allows users to create requests for borrowing assets or reserving spaces, streamlining the process of resource acquisition. It supports various request forms, from basic to advanced and exception requests, catering to different user levels and scenarios. Users gain the ability to formally request needed resources, improving access and planning. Technically, this involves managing request lifecycles from creation to pending status, awaiting administrative approval. Its priority is High, as it enables the primary interaction for general users to obtain resources.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects a basic request form. Response: The system presents a simplified form for specifying an asset and location with a short text description.

Stimulus: User selects an advanced request form. Response: The system presents a form allowing specification of asset serial numbers, locations, detailed operation descriptions, and the ability to add multiple assets.
Stimulus: User selects an exception request form. Response: The system presents a form for submitting a text message describing an unusual request or scenario.
Stimulus: User submits a request. Response: The system creates a new request with 'pending' status, awaiting approval from an appropriate administrative level.
Stimulus: User searches for an asset to borrow that is unavailable. Response: The system displays an 'Asset not available' error message.
functionalRequirements: I-REQ-024: The system shall allow any authenticated user to create a request to borrow an asset or reserve a location.
I-REQ-025: The system shall provide a basic request form for Level 0 users.
I-REQ-026: The system shall provide advanced request forms with search facilities for users at levels 1, 2, and 3.
I-REQ-027: The system shall allow users to specify asset serial numbers and locations in advanced requests.
I-REQ-028: The system shall enable users to add multiple assets to a single advanced request.
I-REQ-029: The system shall provide an exception request form for users to submit special requests.
I-REQ-030: The system shall assign a 'pending' status to new requests upon creation.
I-REQ-031: The system shall notify the user via email when their request is treated (approved or rejected).
I-REQ-032: The system shall prevent users from requesting assets that are currently unavailable.

systemFeatures.name: Approve Requests
systemFeatures.description: This feature empowers administrative users to review and act upon pending asset requests, ensuring that resource allocations align with university policies and availability. It provides a clear interface for displaying requests relevant to an administrator's authority and supports both approval and rejection actions. This streamlines the administrative workflow for resource management. Technically, this involves updating request statuses, notifying users, and triggering inventory updates upon approval. Its priority is High, as it's critical for processing resource requests efficiently.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Administrator accesses the pending request list. Response: The system displays only requests awaiting approval within the administrator's privilege level.

Stimulus: Administrator selects a request and confirms approval. Response: The system updates the request status to 'approved', adds it to the waiting for execution list, notifies the user, and updates inventory upon asset receipt.
Stimulus: Administrator selects a request and confirms rejection. Response: The system updates the request status to 'rejected', notifies the user, and removes it from the pending list.
functionalRequirements: I-REQ-033: The system shall allow any administrative level user or authorized inventory group member to display pending requests awaiting approval within their authority level.
I-REQ-034: The system shall enable administrators to approve or reject displayed requests.
I-REQ-035: The system shall notify the requesting user via email upon the approval or rejection of their request.
I-REQ-036: The system shall add approved requests to a 'waiting for execution' list.
I-REQ-037: The system shall update the inventory to reflect asset allocation when the user receives the requested asset.

systemFeatures.name: Process Returned Assets
systemFeatures.description: This feature manages the process of receiving borrowed assets back into the **inventory**, ensuring that records are accurately updated to reflect their availability. It accounts for asset condition upon return, allowing for status changes such as 'available' or 'damaged'. Users benefit from clear asset return procedures, and inventory accuracy is maintained. Technically, this involves updating the asset's availability status and potentially triggering audit trails for condition changes. Its priority is Medium, as it completes the asset lifecycle for borrowed items.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Inventory Administrator initiates the asset return process. Response: The system prompts the administrator to select the asset and specify its returned state.
Stimulus: Inventory Administrator selects 'available' as the asset state. Response: The system updates the asset's status to available in the inventory.
Stimulus: Inventory Administrator selects 'damaged' as the asset state. Response: The system updates the asset's status to damaged in the inventory and may flag it for further action.
functionalRequirements: I-REQ-038: The system shall allow an Inventory Administrator to check returned assets and update their status in the inventory.
I-REQ-039: The system shall support updating an asset's state to 'available' upon return.
I-REQ-040: The system shall support updating an asset's state to 'damaged' upon return, necessitating potential follow-up actions.

systemFeatures.name: Manage Locations
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides the capability to create and modify physical locations or spaces within the university's organizational structure. It is managed by the IT group, ensuring that the spatial representation in the **inventory** system accurately reflects the physical environment. This is crucial for precise asset tracking and location-based reporting. Technically, it involves updating the system's spatial data model, often in response to administrative requests for new spaces or structural changes. Its priority is Low, as it is an infrastructure-level feature with less frequent use.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: IT Group member receives an exception request for a new space or floor structure modification. Response: The system allows the IT Group member to create a new space or modify existing location structures.
Stimulus: IT Group member creates a new space. Response: The system adds the new location to the inventory's location database, making it available for asset assignment.
functionalRequirements: I-REQ-041: The system shall allow IT Group members to create new locations or spaces within the university.
I-REQ-042: The system shall enable IT Group members to modify the floor structure associated with locations.
I-REQ-043: The system shall ensure that new locations are available for asset assignment upon creation.

systemFeatures.name: Generate Reports
systemFeatures.description: This feature enables authorized users to generate various reports, providing valuable insights into asset utilization, request statuses, and user permissions. It supports different report types—asset by location, request status, and user permissions—allowing for filtered and sorted views. Users benefit from enhanced visibility into **inventory** data, aiding decision-making and auditing. Technically, this involves querying the database and presenting aggregated data in a user-friendly, customizable format. Its priority is Medium, providing critical business intelligence and oversight.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Authorized user clicks on a report type (e.g., 'Asset by Location Report'). Response: The system generates and displays a general report of the selected type.
Stimulus: User applies filters or sorts fields within a displayed report. Response: The system dynamically updates the report to reflect the applied filtering and sorting criteria.
functionalRequirements: I-REQ-044: The system shall allow authorized users to generate asset reports filtered by location.
I-REQ-045: The system shall allow authorized users to generate reports on the status of requests.
I-REQ-046: The system shall allow authorized users to generate reports detailing user permissions.
I-REQ-047: The system shall enable filtering and sorting of data within all generated reports.

systemFeatures.name: Search Functionality
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides users with the ability to search for assets and other **inventory** items using both simple and advanced search criteria. It enhances usability by allowing quick retrieval of information, significantly reducing the time and effort required to locate specific assets or data. Users benefit from flexible search options that cater to different levels of detail and complexity. Technically, this involves efficient database indexing and query mechanisms to deliver fast and accurate search results. Its priority is High, as it's integral to daily operational efficiency.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User enters keywords into a basic search field and presses submit. Response: The system displays search results matching the keywords.
Stimulus: User selects advanced search and fills multiple criteria fields, then presses submit. Response: The system displays search results refined by the advanced criteria.
Stimulus: User performs a search that yields no results. Response: The system displays a 'No results found' message.
functionalRequirements: I-REQ-048: The system shall provide a simple search functionality for inventory assets.
I-REQ-049: The system shall provide an advanced search functionality with multiple filterable fields.
I-REQ-050: The system shall display relevant search results in a clear and organized manner.
I-REQ-051: The system shall inform the user if no results are found for a given search query.

performanceRequirements: I-REQ-052: The system shall respond to user interface actions (e.g., page loads, form submissions, search queries) within 3 seconds under normal load conditions (up to 100 concurrent users) to ensure a fluid user experience and maintain productivity. This rapid response time is critical for frequently accessed functionalities like asset search and request submission, preventing user frustration and ensuring efficient task completion.

I-REQ-053: Database queries, especially those for complex reports or large data sets, shall execute and return results within 60 seconds. This prevents timeouts and ensures that administrators can access critical business intelligence in a timely manner. Queries exceeding this limit shall be automatically terminated by the system to prevent resource exhaustion and maintain overall system responsiveness for other users.
safetyRequirements: I-REQ-054: The system shall implement robust data validation mechanisms on all input fields to prevent the entry of malicious or malformed data that could compromise system integrity or lead to incorrect asset tracking. This includes type checking, range validation, and protection against SQL injection and cross-site scripting vulnerabilities. Ensuring data integrity at the input level reduces the risk of operational errors and system failures.

I-REQ-055: The system shall include comprehensive error handling and logging for all critical operations, such as asset transfers, request approvals, and permission changes. In the event of a system failure or unexpected error, the system shall maintain a consistent state, and all actions leading up to the failure shall be logged for audit and recovery purposes. This safeguard ensures that no data is lost or corrupted, and system administrators can diagnose and rectify issues efficiently.
securityRequirements: I-REQ-056: All user authentication shall require a unique username and a strong password, with passwords securely stored using industry-standard hashing and salting techniques. The system shall enforce password complexity rules (e.g., minimum length, mix of character types) and prevent the reuse of old passwords. This foundational security measure protects user accounts from unauthorized access and brute-force attacks.

I-REQ-057: The system shall implement a role-based access control (RBAC) model, ensuring that user permissions are strictly assigned and enforced according to their defined roles and organizational levels. Access to specific functionalities and data will be restricted based on an authenticated user's privileges, preventing unauthorized operations and data breaches. This granular control is essential for managing sensitive asset information and administrative actions.

I-REQ-058: All communication between client browsers and the **inventory** server shall be encrypted using **TLS/SSL** protocols (e.g., HTTPS). This secures data in transit, protecting sensitive information like login credentials, asset details, and personal data from eavesdropping and tampering. The use of robust encryption ensures privacy and data integrity across network channels.

I-REQ-059: The system shall perform regular, scheduled backups of all **inventory** data to a secure, off-site location. These backups shall be verifiable and restorable to minimize data loss in the event of a catastrophic system failure or data corruption incident. Backup operations will be conducted outside of peak working hours to minimize impact on system availability.
softwareQualityAttributes: I-REQ-060: Usability: The system shall be designed with an intuitive and consistent web-based user interface, requiring a maximum learning time of 2 to 4 hours for users with basic internet and office application experience. Clear terminology and logical workflows will be used throughout to enhance user adoption and reduce training overhead. This ensures that even delegated working students can quickly become proficient in using the system.

I-REQ-061: Availability: The system shall be available 99.9% of the time during university working hours (e.g., 8:00 AM - 6:00 PM, Monday-Friday). Any scheduled maintenance or backup operations shall be performed outside these hours to minimize disruption to users. High availability is crucial for supporting continuous academic and administrative operations that rely on accurate and accessible inventory data.

I-REQ-062: Portability: The web application component shall be compatible with major modern web browsers, including **Google Chrome**, **Mozilla Firefox**, **Microsoft Edge**, **Apple Safari**, and **Opera**. The server-side components shall be deployable on both **Microsoft Windows Server** and **Linux (Unix-like)** operating system platforms. This portability ensures flexibility in deployment and broad accessibility for end-users, regardless of their preferred browser or the university's server infrastructure.

I-REQ-063: Maintainability: The system shall be developed using modular, well-documented code that adheres to established programming standards and design patterns. This will facilitate future enhancements, bug fixes, and system evolution with minimal effort. Clear separation of concerns and robust internal documentation are paramount to ensuring the long-term maintainability and adaptability of the **inventory** system as university needs change.

I-REQ-064: Testability: The system shall be designed with clear separation of concerns, making individual modules and components independently testable. It shall provide logging and diagnostic tools to assist in identifying and isolating defects during testing and operational phases. This ensures that the system can be thoroughly verified for correctness and reliability before deployment and efficiently debugged thereafter.
businessRules: I-REQ-065: All asset transfers outside the immediate department or faculty hierarchy must follow a defined approval chain involving higher administrative levels to ensure proper oversight and accountability. Transfers outside the university require the highest level of approval.

I-REQ-066: Permissions within the **inventory** system are hierarchical; a user cannot delegate permissions they do not possess. This rule maintains the integrity of the administrative structure and prevents unauthorized escalation of privileges.

I-REQ-067: All asset requests, once created, must enter a 'pending' state and await formal approval or rejection by an authorized administrator before any action is taken on the inventory. This ensures that resource allocation is a controlled and deliberate process.

glossary.definition: Unified University Inventory System, an alias for the **inventory** system.
The system's abbreviation (derived from 'IUfA's UUIS' in the source text), responsible for integrating faculty databases and managing inventory.
Any item managed by the **inventory** system, including rooms, spaces, software licenses, and physical equipment.
A user role with full control over the entire university inventory.
A user role with control over inventory within a specific faculty and its departments.
A user role with control over inventory within a specific department.
A user role with specific permissions delegated by higher administrative levels to perform specific inventory tasks.
A user role, typically students and professors, authorized to create requests for assets or spaces.
A user role with technical privileges to maintain the system, including database access and infrastructure management.
A formal submission by a user to borrow an asset, reserve a space, or initiate an asset transfer.
Software Requirements Specification, this document outlining the software's requirements.
Role-Based Access Control, a security model where access is granted based on user roles.
Transport Layer Security / Secure Sockets Layer, cryptographic protocols for securing network communications.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart utilizes standard Mermaid syntax to illustrate the high-level process flow for asset requests and their subsequent approval or rejection within the **inventory** system. Nodes represent distinct steps or decisions, while arrows indicate the direction of the flow. Decision points (rhombus shapes) have clear 'Yes' or 'No' branches. All node labels with spaces are enclosed in double quotes for strict compliance. Node IDs are alphanumeric, ensuring unique identification without using reserved keywords.
flowchartDiagram.caption: Asset Request and Approval Flow

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram illustrates the temporal interaction flow for the user authentication process in the **inventory** system. Participants represent system components or users, and messages depict the communication between them. Standard synchronous and asynchronous message arrows are used to denote request and response patterns. `alt` and `else` blocks are employed to show alternative flows based on the success or failure of authentication, ensuring all possible outcomes are represented clearly. Aliases for participants are used without quoting to maintain strict compliance.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant WebApp as "Web Application"
    participant AuthService as "Authentication Service"
    participant Database

    User->>WebApp: "Access Login Page"
    WebApp-->>User: "Display Login Form"
    User->>WebApp: "Submit Credentials(username, password)"
    WebApp->>AuthService: "Verify Credentials(username, password)"
    AuthService->>Database: "Query User Credentials"
    Database-->>AuthService: "Return User Data / Error"
    alt Valid Credentials
        AuthService-->>WebApp: "Authentication Successful"
        WebApp-->>User: "Redirect to Dashboard"
    else Invalid Credentials
        AuthService-->>WebApp: "Authentication Failed"
        WebApp-->>User: "Display Error Message"
    end
sequenceDiagram.caption: User Authentication Flow

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) models the core data entities and their relationships within the **inventory** system's database. Entities represent significant data structures, with attributes defining their properties. Primary Keys (PK), Foreign Keys (FK), and Unique Keys (UK) are explicitly identified for attribute keys. Relationships between entities are shown with their cardinality and a descriptive, double-quoted label. The diagram adheres to the rule that if Entity A has a Foreign Key to Entity B, the relationship is depicted as 'Entity A }|--|| Entity B', indicating a many-to-one relationship from A to B. Entity names and attribute names are alphanumeric.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
    USER ||--o{ USER_ROLE : "has role"
    ROLE ||--o{ USER_ROLE : "assigned to"

    ROLE ||--o{ ROLE_PERMISSION : "grants"
    PERMISSION ||--o{ ROLE_PERMISSION : "is granted"

    ORG_UNIT }|--o{ ORG_UNIT : "contains"
    ORG_UNIT ||--o{ LOCATION : "manages"
    ORG_UNIT ||--o{ ASSET : "owns"

    ASSET_TYPE ||--o{ ASSET : "categorizes"
    LOCATION ||--o{ ASSET : "is located at"

    USER ||--o{ REQUEST : "creates"
    USER ||--o{ REQUEST : "approves"
    REQUEST ||--o{ REQUEST_ITEM : "includes"
    ASSET ||--o{ REQUEST_ITEM : "is for"

    USER ||--o{ AUDIT_LOG : "performs action"

    USER {
        int id PK
        string username UK
        string email UK
        string password_hash
    }
    ROLE {
        int id PK
        string name UK
    }
    USER_ROLE {
        int user_id PK,FK
        int role_id PK,FK
    }
    PERMISSION {
        int id PK
        string name UK
        string description
    }
    ROLE_PERMISSION {
        int role_id PK,FK
        int permission_id PK,FK
    }
    ORG_UNIT {
        int id PK
        string name
        string type
        int parent_id FK
    }
    LOCATION {
        int id PK
        string name UK
        string description
        int org_unit_id FK
    }
    ASSET_TYPE {
        int id PK
        string name UK
        string description
    }
    ASSET {
        int id PK
        string name
        string serial_number UK
        string description
        int asset_type_id FK
        int location_id FK
        int org_unit_id FK
    }
    REQUEST {
        int id PK
        int user_id FK
        string request_type
        string status
        datetime created_at
        int approved_by_user_id FK
    }
    REQUEST_ITEM {
        int request_id PK,FK
        int asset_id PK,FK
        int quantity
        string status
    }
    AUDIT_LOG {
        int id PK
        int user_id FK
        string action
        string details
        datetime timestamp
    }
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: Inventory System Entity Relationship Diagram

revisionHistory.author: SRA System

introduction.purpose: This Software Requirements Specification (SRS) details the purpose and functionality of the **space fractions** project. This document serves as a comprehensive guide for the development of a web-based educational game designed to enhance fraction-solving skills. It includes specific requirements, interface considerations, and core components of the software product, as requested by Ms. Andrea Brooks of Pecan Springs Elementary School.
introduction.documentConventions: This document adheres to the IEEE Standard 830-1998 for Software Requirements Specifications, utilizing formal prose and structured sections. All technical terms, acronyms, and abbreviations are defined in the Glossary for clarity and consistency. Requirements are articulated with specific identifiers to facilitate traceability and management throughout the development lifecycle, ensuring a standardized approach to documentation.
introduction.intendedAudience: This SRS is primarily intended for both the client, Ms. Andrea Brooks, and the technical development team responsible for implementing the **space fractions** project. It provides the client with a clear understanding of the proposed system's capabilities and serves as a detailed blueprint for designers, developers, and testers. Additionally, it informs project managers and quality assurance personnel about the scope and detailed requirements of the system.
introduction.productScope: The **space fractions** project focuses on developing an interactive, web-based game aimed at sixth-grade students to improve their fraction arithmetic skills. The system will provide immediate feedback on student performance and will integrate an 'umbrella' feature. This umbrella will act as a centralized web-based menu, offering access to the **space fractions** game and other related educational tools, thereby expanding its utility as a learning platform.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.
Dr. Vicki L. Almstrum, professor at the University of Texas at Austin.
Ms. Andrea Brooks, client and teacher at Pecan Springs Elementary.
Class home page: http://www.cs.utexas.edu/users/almstrum/cs373/fa01/.
Mr. Keith Henning, team mentor.
Macromedia home page: http://www.macromedia.com.
S2S projects done in previous semesters: http://www.cs.utexas.edu/users/s2s/admin/s2s-by-semester.html.
Schach, Stephen R. Object-Oriented and Classical Software Engineering, Fifth Edition. McGraw-Hill, 2002.

overallDescription.productPerspective: The **space fractions** product is a new, self-contained educational game, not directly dependent on or a component of any other existing software system. It is designed to operate within standard web browser environments, specifically those capable of rendering **Macromedia Flash** content. While the user interface appearance may vary slightly across different browser configurations, the underlying behavior and core functionality of the game will remain consistent, ensuring a uniform learning experience.
productFunctions: Deliver an engaging introductory movie that establishes the game's narrative.
Provide a main menu for navigation, including access to game rules and team information.
Present a series of interactive, multiple-choice fraction questions structured into a storyline.
Calculate and display user scores, offering customized feedback and replay options at the game's conclusion.
Enable an administrator to remotely update and customize game questions via a dedicated web interface.
Integrate a 'Math Umbrella' component, providing categorized links to other S2S educational projects.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: These users are sixth-grade students actively learning arithmetic involving fractions. They are assumed to possess basic computer and Internet navigation skills. Alice represents students who may not inherently enjoy computer use but are motivated to learn fractions, benefiting from engaging, easy-to-use interfaces and collaborative learning elements. Bobby represents competitive students who enjoy computer games and excel in performance-driven environments, thriving on quick progression and scoring mechanisms.

Claire is a sixth-grade teacher with proficient computer skills, seeking innovative teaching methods for fractions. She requires the ability to easily manage and customize the game's content to suit specific learning objectives. Her role as an administrator necessitates secure and intuitive tools for updating questions, ensuring the educational material remains relevant and adaptable to student needs. She values educational effectiveness and ease of content management.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The **space fractions** software is designed to operate within a standard **Web**-based environment. It requires an **Internet**-accessible computer equipped with a **web browser** that fully supports **JavaScript** and the **Macromedia Flash 5** plug-in. The application will be hosted on a web server, serving content to client browsers without requiring permanent file installations on the user's local machine. This ensures broad accessibility across various computing platforms that meet the browser and plug-in requirements.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The **space fractions** application must be implemented using **Macromedia Flash** and **HTML** to ensure web-based accessibility and rich interactive content. It is constrained to run on any **Internet**-accessible computer with a **web browser** supporting **JavaScript** and **Macromedia Flash 5**. No new hardware development is required for the system's operation, and it must operate independently, without reliance on other specific software applications for its core functionality.
userDocumentation: The **space fractions** product will include an integrated help section accessible directly from the Main Menu, providing basic instructions for gameplay and navigation. This in-game documentation is designed to be intuitive and graphically oriented, catering specifically to the sixth-grade student user base. Further detailed user documentation, such as comprehensive user manuals or external tutorials, is not a required deliverable for this iteration of the project.
assumptionsAndDependencies: It is assumed that all target users, including sixth-grade students and their teachers, possess fundamental computer literacy and Internet navigation skills. A core assumption is the availability and proper configuration of a **web browser** that supports **Macromedia Flash 5** and **JavaScript** on the client machines. The project depends on the stability and continued functionality of the **Macromedia Flash** platform and the **World Wide Web** infrastructure for content delivery and interactivity. Any changes in browser compatibility or **Flash** support could impact the product's accessibility.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: The user interface for **space fractions** will be graphically rich and designed for ease of use by sixth-grade students, prioritizing visual appeal and intuitive interaction. All user input will be exclusively managed through **mouse clicks**, eliminating the need for keyboard interaction. This design choice aims to simplify navigation and engagement for the target demographic. The interface will adhere to general **Web**-based graphical user interface standards, ensuring a consistent and accessible experience across supported browsers. While the visual presentation may adapt slightly to different browser environments, the core interactive elements and functional behavior will remain uniform.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The **space fractions** software does not require any specialized or new hardware components beyond a standard **Internet**-accessible computer. It is designed to function with minimal hardware requirements, leveraging the capabilities of the host client machine's existing setup. There are no explicit interactions with specific client hardware devices, as the program primarily relies on the **web browser** and its associated plug-ins for rendering and input processing.
