externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: The **space fractions** product's primary software interface requirement is a **web browser** capable of executing **Macromedia Flash** content via its plug-in. The system interacts directly with the **Flash** Player embedded within the browser environment for rendering interactive graphics and managing game logic. The application is a self-contained unit and does not integrate with other external software components, specialized tools, or database systems on the client side, beyond what is necessary for web content delivery. Data for questions and scores is managed server-side or locally within the **Flash** application itself.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: The **space fractions** application relies heavily on standard **HTTP** protocol for delivering **Web** content, including **HTML** pages and **Macromedia Flash** movies, from a central web server to client browsers. Communication between the client and server for dynamic question updates (by administrators) will utilize standard web forms over **HTTP**. All data transfers, including game content and administrative updates, are handled through the standard **Internet** protocols. While no specific encryption or advanced security protocols are explicitly required for general gameplay, administrator login for content updates will necessitate secure communication practices appropriate for sensitive data, ensuring data integrity during question modification.

systemFeatures.name: Introductory Movie Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides an initial narrative introduction to the **space fractions** game, setting the storyline and providing context for the upcoming fraction questions. Its purpose is to immerse the student user in the game's world, thereby enhancing engagement and motivation. Technically, it manages the display of a media asset and controls the transition to the main game menu, optionally allowing users to bypass the introduction to expedite gameplay.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User initiates the **space fractions** application. Response: The introductory movie begins playing automatically.
Stimulus: User clicks the mouse button during the introductory movie. Response: The introductory movie terminates, and the system immediately transitions to the Main Menu component.
functionalRequirements: SF-REQ-001: The system shall automatically play the introductory movie upon application startup.
SF-REQ-002: The system shall detect a mouse click input during the introductory movie playback.
SF-REQ-003: The system shall terminate the introductory movie and navigate the user to the Main Menu component upon detecting a mouse click.
SF-REQ-004: The system shall automatically transition the user to the Main Menu component upon the completion of the introductory movie playback if no skip input is received.

systemFeatures.name: Main Menu Navigation
systemFeatures.description: The Main Menu feature serves as the central hub for the **space fractions** game, providing essential navigation options to the user. It offers access to the core game sequence, a brief help section, and information about the development team. This improves the user experience by centralizing access to game functionalities and support, ensuring both competitive players and those new to computers can easily begin the game or understand its rules.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User arrives at the Main Menu. Response: The Main Menu interface is displayed, showing options to start the game, view help, or access developer information.
Stimulus: User clicks the 'Start Game' button. Response: The system initiates the Game Sequence component.
Stimulus: User clicks the 'Help' button. Response: The system displays a brief screen detailing basic game instructions.
Stimulus: User clicks the 'Denominators' web page link. Response: The system opens the specified web page in a new browser window or tab.
functionalRequirements: SF-REQ-005: The system shall display the Main Menu interface upon transition from the introductory movie or game ending.
SF-REQ-006: The system shall provide a clear button to initiate the main Game Sequence.
SF-REQ-007: The system shall provide an accessible button to display a brief help section.
SF-REQ-008: The system shall provide a link to the Denominators' external web page.
SF-REQ-009: The system shall transition to the Game Sequence upon user selection of the 'Start Game' button.
SF-REQ-010: The system shall display the help content upon user selection of the 'Help' button.
SF-REQ-011: The system shall open the specified external web page in a new browser context upon user selection of the Denominators' link.

systemFeatures.name: Interactive Game Sequence
systemFeatures.description: This feature constitutes the core gameplay of **space fractions**, presenting a series of multiple-choice fraction questions embedded within a continuous storyline. It allows students to apply and test their fraction skills in an engaging context, providing immediate feedback on their answers. The system dynamically adapts the plot based on critical question responses, ensuring a personalized and stimulating learning journey while tracking performance for scoring.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User enters the Game Sequence. Response: The system displays the first question within the context of the game's storyline.
Stimulus: User selects an answer option for a question. Response: The system evaluates the answer, provides feedback, and either progresses to the next question or offers another attempt.
Stimulus: User answers a 'critical point' question correctly/incorrectly. Response: The system adjusts the narrative plot based on the outcome of the critical question.
Stimulus: User completes a set number of questions. Response: The system transitions the user to the Ending Scene component.
functionalRequirements: SF-REQ-012: The system shall display sequential multiple-choice fraction questions integrated into a storyline.
SF-REQ-013: The system shall accept user input via mouse clicks on selected answer options.
SF-REQ-014: The system shall provide immediate visual and auditory feedback indicating whether an answer is correct or incorrect.
SF-REQ-015: The system shall advance to the next question upon a correct answer.
SF-REQ-016: The system shall offer a second attempt for incorrect answers, without counting the question as correctly answered for scoring.
SF-REQ-017: The system shall dynamically alter the game's narrative plot based on user responses to designated 'critical point' questions.
SF-REQ-018: The system shall direct the user to the Ending Scene component after a predefined number of questions have been presented.

systemFeatures.name: Game Ending and Scoring
systemFeatures.description: The Ending Scene feature provides a conclusion to the **space fractions** game, summarizing the user's performance and offering choices for replay or exit. This motivates competitive students by displaying their score and encourages all users to revisit the game to further improve their skills or experience alternative storylines. It provides a clear resolution to the game's narrative, fostering a sense of accomplishment or a challenge for improvement.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User completes the Game Sequence. Response: The system displays the Ending Scene, including the user's score and a plot conclusion related to gameplay decisions.
Stimulus: User clicks the 'Try Again' button. Response: The system returns the user to the Main Menu or restarts the Game Sequence.
Stimulus: User clicks the 'Exit Game' button. Response: The system terminates the active game session.
functionalRequirements: SF-REQ-019: The system shall display the user's overall score at the conclusion of the game.
SF-REQ-020: The system shall present a plot conclusion dynamically generated based on user performance at critical points in the Game Sequence.
SF-REQ-021: The system shall provide an option for the user to return to the Main Menu to replay the game.
SF-REQ-022: The system shall provide an option for the user to exit the game.
SF-REQ-023: The system shall process user selections to either restart the game or terminate the application gracefully.

systemFeatures.name: Question Updater Administration
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides an exclusive web-accessible tool for game administrators to modify and update the fraction questions used in the **space fractions** game. It allows teachers like Claire to tailor educational content to specific curriculum needs, ensuring the game remains relevant and effective. The system secures access with a password and provides a user-friendly interface for content management, directly impacting the quality and adaptability of the learning experience.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Administrator navigates to the Question Updater page. Response: The system prompts the administrator for a password.
Stimulus: Administrator submits correct password. Response: The system grants access to the intuitive web forms interface for question editing.
Stimulus: Administrator inputs new question data via fields and menus. Response: The system validates the input data for completeness and contextual correctness.
Stimulus: Administrator clicks the submission button for an updated question. Response: The system saves the new question data to a text file on the game server.
functionalRequirements: SF-REQ-024: The system shall require password authentication for access to the Question Updater tool.
SF-REQ-025: The system shall provide a web-based interface for administrators to input new question data using pulldown menus and text fields.
SF-REQ-026: The system shall allow administrators to update questions individually across separate pages.
SF-REQ-027: The system shall validate inputted question data for completeness and logical consistency before saving.
SF-REQ-028: The system shall save updated question data to a designated text file on the game server.
SF-REQ-029: The system shall ensure the game sequence dynamically loads question data from the updated server-side text file.

systemFeatures.name: Math Umbrella Integration
systemFeatures.description: The Math Umbrella feature acts as a centralized repository, providing curated links to other S2S educational projects focused on mathematics for sixth graders, categorized by topic. This enhances the value of the **space fractions** application by offering students and teachers a broader range of supplementary learning resources. It simplifies access to related content, promoting continuous learning and exploration beyond the immediate game.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User navigates to the Math Umbrella component. Response: The system displays a categorized list of links to S2S mathematics projects.
Stimulus: User clicks a link to an S2S product. Response: The system opens the specified S2S product in a new, separate browser window or tab.
functionalRequirements: SF-REQ-030: The system shall display a categorized list of links to S2S mathematics projects.
SF-REQ-031: The system shall organize links by mathematical topic (e.g., fractions, decimals, percents).
SF-REQ-032: The system shall open the selected S2S product in a new browser window or tab upon user click.
SF-REQ-033: The system shall ensure that external S2S products load independently of the **space fractions** application.

performanceRequirements: SF-REQ-034: The system shall load the introductory movie (~200KB) and main menu movie (~100KB) within approximately one minute over a standard modem connection (e.g., 56kbps), ensuring quick initial access for users. This rapid loading enhances user engagement by minimizing wait times at the start of the application. The **Flash** streaming capabilities are crucial for meeting this requirement, as full download is not necessary for playback initiation.

SF-REQ-035: The system shall enable the main game movie (1-2MB) to be playable within a few minutes of initial download over a standard modem connection, leveraging **Flash**'s progressive download capabilities. This ensures that users can begin interacting with the core game without waiting for the entire asset to be fully retrieved. The user experience is optimized by allowing gameplay to commence as soon as sufficient data is buffered.

SF-REQ-036: The system shall support multiple concurrent users accessing individual instances of the product from different client machines via the **Internet**. Each user's game instance will operate independently on their local browser, minimizing server-side load for active gameplay. The **web server** must be capable of handling simultaneous content delivery requests to accommodate a classroom or broader student audience efficiently.
safetyRequirements: SF-REQ-037: The system shall prevent the display of inappropriate or offensive content to sixth-grade students. All questions, images, and narrative elements within the **space fractions** game must be age-appropriate and educationally sound. This requirement is critical to ensure a safe and conducive learning environment, preventing any material that could cause distress or be deemed unsuitable for the target demographic. Regular content reviews are necessary to uphold this standard.

SF-REQ-038: The system shall not pose any physical safety risks through its operation, as it is purely a software application. It will not interact with or control any physical hardware components beyond the user's standard computer peripherals (mouse, display). This ensures that the application operates strictly within the confines of a digital environment, posing no direct hazards to the user's physical well-being or equipment.
securityRequirements: SF-REQ-039: The system shall ensure administrator access to the Question Updater is protected by a password, preventing unauthorized modification of game content. This is a critical security measure to maintain the integrity and educational accuracy of the fraction questions. The password mechanism must be robust enough to deter common unauthorized access attempts, safeguarding the curriculum content.

SF-REQ-040: The system shall inherit the security posture of the underlying **web browser** running the product, relying on browser-level protections against common web vulnerabilities. Since the product operates within the browser sandbox, it is assumed that the browser provides adequate isolation and security. This implies that the **space fractions** application itself will not introduce new, significant security vulnerabilities beyond those inherent in the browser and **Flash** plug-in environment.

SF-REQ-041: The system shall not collect or store personally identifiable information (PII) of student users, except for local session-based scoring data that is not transmitted to a server. This privacy requirement ensures compliance with educational data protection principles, minimizing risks associated with data breaches or misuse. User scores will be confined to the local game instance, providing privacy and avoiding external data retention concerns.
softwareQualityAttributes: SF-REQ-042: Reliability: The system shall be extensively tested by team members and mentors to ensure consistent and error-free operation throughout gameplay and administrative functions. This commitment to rigorous testing aims to minimize crashes, freezes, or incorrect feedback, ensuring a dependable learning experience for students. The goal is to maximize uptime and reduce unexpected disruptions during use.

SF-REQ-043: Maintainability: The system shall be designed with clear modularity, particularly within the main **Flash** game, using appropriate sub-scenes to segment code. This approach will facilitate easy alteration and updates to game content and logic at a later date. This attribute is paramount for long-term project viability, allowing for future curriculum adjustments or feature enhancements without extensive re-engineering.

SF-REQ-044: Usability: The system's user interface shall be highly intuitive and graphically engaging, designed specifically for sixth-grade students, requiring only mouse-click input for all interactions. This ensures that students with varying levels of computer familiarity, including those less inclined to use computers, can easily navigate and enjoy the game. Clear feedback mechanisms and a simple interaction model contribute to high usability.

SF-REQ-045: Availability: The system shall be available over the **Internet** via the S2S website, ensuring continuous access for students and administrators during specified operational hours. This requires robust hosting infrastructure and proper deployment procedures to guarantee that the web application and its components are consistently accessible whenever needed for educational purposes. Downtime must be minimized to support learning continuity.
businessRules: SF-REQ-046: All fractional arithmetic problems presented in the game must adhere to standard mathematical principles suitable for sixth-grade curriculum. This rule ensures the educational integrity and accuracy of the learning content. The system must validate that all questions and their associated correct answers are mathematically sound and align with pedagogical objectives.

SF-REQ-047: The game storyline and feedback mechanisms shall be designed to be encouraging and supportive, promoting a positive learning attitude regardless of a student's performance. This rule underpins the educational philosophy, ensuring that even incorrect answers are presented constructively, fostering perseverance and engagement rather than frustration.

SF-REQ-048: Game administrators (teachers) are the sole authorized individuals for updating game questions. No other user class shall possess the privileges to modify the educational content. This rule enforces content control and quality, ensuring that only approved educational material is presented within the **space fractions** game.

otherRequirements: SF-REQ-049: There are no additional requirements for the **space fractions** project beyond those detailed in this document at this time. All defined functionalities and quality attributes comprehensively cover the current scope and objectives of the software product as specified by the client.

glossary.definition: Hypertext Markup Language: the formatting language used to describe web pages.
A type of computer made by Apple Computers.
Kilobytes: a measure of the amount of space available on a computer.
An application for creating dynamic graphics and sound, generally intended to produce artifacts for display over the World Wide Web.
Megabytes: a measure of the amount of space available on a computer. One MB is equal to 1,024KB.
A downloadable component for a web browser that provides additional functionality.
Student to Student: the designation representing the collective efforts of several hundred University of Texas Computer Science students taking Software Engineering with Dr. Vicki Almstrum.
Software Requirements Specification: this document.
A software product that consists of several independent programs that are unified under a single interface.
Able to be run over the web, without any permanent files on the user's computer.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart utilizes standard flowchart symbols to illustrate the main workflow of the **space fractions** game. Nodes are represented by IDs with quoted labels for clarity, and arrows indicate transitions between states or processes. Decisions are shown with diamond shapes, directing the flow based on user input or system evaluation. The chart provides a high-level overview of the user journey from starting the application to game completion or exit.
flowchartDiagram.caption: SF_FLOW_GameFlow - Overall **space fractions** game flow

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram illustrates the interaction flow for an Administrator updating questions within the **space fractions** system. Participants represent distinct entities or components, and messages show the synchronous or asynchronous communication between them over time. Activations indicate when a participant is performing an action, and alternative blocks ('alt') depict conditional flows, such as successful authentication or data validation.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Admin as "SF_Administrator"
    participant QA as "SF_QuestionUpdater"
    participant GS as "SF_GameServer"
    participant DB as "SF_QuestionDatabase"

    Admin->>QA: Access "SF_QuestionUpdater" Page
    activate QA
    QA->>Admin: Request "SF_Password"
    Admin->>QA: Submit "SF_Password"
    alt Successful Authentication
        QA->>Admin: Display "SF_UpdateInterface"
        Admin->>QA: Enter "SF_NewQuestionData"
        QA->>QA: Validate "SF_InputData"
        alt Data Valid
            QA->>GS: Send "SF_UpdateQuestionFile"
            activate GS
            GS->>DB: Write "SF_QuestionRecord"
            activate DB
            DB-->>GS: Confirmation "SF_WriteSuccess"
            deactivate DB
            GS-->>QA: Confirmation "SF_FileUpdated"
            deactivate GS
            QA->>Admin: Display "SF_UpdateSuccess"
        else Data Invalid
            QA->>Admin: Display "SF_ValidationError"
        end
    else Authentication Failed
        QA->>Admin: Display "SF_AuthError"
    end
    deactivate QA
sequenceDiagram.caption: SF_SEQ_QuestionUpdate - Administrator question update process

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) models the data structure for the **space fractions** system. Entities represent distinct data objects, and relationships define how these entities are associated. Cardinality symbols indicate the number of instances of one entity that can be associated with the number of instances of another. Primary Keys (PK) uniquely identify records, Foreign Keys (FK) establish links between tables, and Unique Keys (UK) ensure uniqueness.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
    USER ||--o{ SCORE : "achieves"
    GAME ||--o{ QUESTION : "contains"
    GAME ||--o{ SCORE : "records"
    ADMIN ||--o{ QUESTION : "manages"
    USER }|--|| ADMIN : "is a system user"

    USER {
        int user_id PK
        string email UK
        string username
        datetime created_at
    }

    ADMIN {
        int admin_id PK
        int user_id FK
        string password_hash
    }

    GAME {
        int game_id PK
        string game_name UK
        string description
    }

    QUESTION {
        int question_id PK
        int game_id FK
        string question_text
        string question_type
        string options
        string correct_option
        boolean is_critical_point
    }

    SCORE {
        int score_id PK
        int user_id FK
        int game_id FK
        int score_value
        datetime played_at
    }
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: SF_ERD_Schema - **space fractions** database schema

introduction.purpose: The primary purpose of the **grid 3D** application is to provide a robust method for visualizing complex biological data within a three-dimensional environment. This system addresses a critical need for researchers to interactively explore data, enabling them to quickly identify and analyze various attributes at a glance. It aims to offer advanced visualization capabilities that are currently unavailable in existing tools, thereby enhancing scientific investigation and discovery. The scope of this document is to define the functional and non-functional requirements for the development of this novel visualization tool.
introduction.documentConventions: This Software Requirements Specification (SRS) adheres to the guidelines set forth by IEEE Standard 830-1998, ensuring a comprehensive and structured documentation of requirements for the **grid 3D** application. All technical terms, acronyms, and abbreviations are defined in the Glossary for clarity and consistency across the document. Functional requirements are presented in a standardized format, beginning with 'The system shall...', to facilitate unambiguous interpretation and traceability. The document focuses on specifying 'what' the system should do, rather than 'how' it will be implemented.
introduction.intendedAudience: This document is intended for a diverse audience, including project stakeholders, development teams, quality assurance personnel, and future maintainers of the **grid 3D** application. Researchers and scientists within the parasitology department at NIMR, along with external research organizations, will find this SRS valuable for understanding the product's capabilities and limitations. Technical personnel, such as designers, developers, and testers, will use this document as a foundational reference for implementation, verification, and validation activities. Project management will utilize this specification for scope management, resource allocation, and progress tracking.
introduction.productScope: The **grid 3D** application is designed as a standalone scientific visualization tool, offering users the ability to plot and interact with biological data in a three-dimensional space. It will enable users to visualize data points based on multiple parameters, including position (X, Y, Z) and color-coded attributes (Cluster_Title). The product's scope encompasses data parsing, 3D rendering, interactive grid manipulation (rotation, zoom, move), and detailed data point inspection functionalities. While it will integrate with existing operating systems and data generation tools like **Matlab**, it will not encompass data generation or complex analytical processing capabilities, focusing solely on advanced visualization.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.

overallDescription.productPerspective: The **grid 3D** application is conceived as a new, self-contained product aimed at fulfilling a unique visualization need within biological research. It is not intended as a replacement for existing systems but rather as a complementary tool to address the identified shortfalls in current data visualization offerings. The project emerged from a recognized demand for a dedicated 3D environment that facilitates nuanced data exploration, moving beyond conventional tabular or basic graphical representations. This product is expected to enhance the analytical capabilities of researchers by providing an intuitive interactive platform for complex biological datasets.
productFunctions: The **grid 3D** application shall enable users to upload and parse tab-delimited biological data files.
The system shall accurately plot data points in a three-dimensional grid, dynamically labeling axes based on input data.
The application shall allow interactive manipulation of the 3D grid, including rotation, zooming, and panning.
The system shall facilitate detailed interaction with individual data points, allowing users to display names and full descriptions on demand.
The application shall support the visualization of up to 2000 data points while maintaining responsive performance.
The system shall ensure that data points are color-coded according to a specified cluster attribute for immediate visual differentiation.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: These users are actively engaged in investigating proteins, biological data, and conducting data-mining activities. They possess strong subject matter experience in biology and research methodologies but may have varying levels of technological expertise, ranging from basic computer operation to advanced computational skills. They are primarily interested in analyzing their data efficiently and require a tool that provides detailed information and visual comparisons. They are expected to be proficient with basic operating system functions, internet browsers, keyboard, mouse, and command-line input for file handling.

Demonstrators are a specialized subset of users who utilize the **grid 3D** application to present and showcase properties of biological data to other individuals. Their primary need is for clear, unambiguous visualizations that effectively communicate complex data relationships. They may prioritize ease of presentation and visual clarity over in-depth analytical functions, requiring responsive controls for grid manipulation and data point highlighting. They share the general computing skills assumed for research scientists and students, with a focus on visual communication.

These users actively use the **grid 3D** application to identify specific 'points of interest' within the visualized data, such as outliers or clusters. Investigators then delve deeper to find detailed information about these specific points. They require precise interaction capabilities, such as single and double-click functionalities, to extract and display relevant data point attributes. Their technological experience is similar to other research users, with an emphasis on data exploration and detailed information retrieval, often progressing from identification to in-depth investigation.

This class includes individuals like Ben Tagger, who are responsible for designing, developing, testing, and maintaining the **grid 3D** application. They possess high technical expertise, including programming, system administration, and quality assurance skills. Their primary interest is in the system's architecture, code quality, and performance, ensuring its stability, scalability, and adherence to design specifications. They also handle deployment and troubleshooting, often interacting with the backend and configuration settings, and managing various operating system environments.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The **grid 3D** application is designed to operate across multiple mainstream desktop computing environments, ensuring broad accessibility for its user base. It shall support both **Microsoft Windows** (versions 98, 2000, NT, XP) and **Linux** and **Solaris** operating systems. The application is intended to run on a minimum of a mid-range personal computer, necessitating compatibility with standard hardware configurations prevalent in research settings.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The **grid 3D** application must be available on both **Windows** and **Linux** operating platforms, requiring a cross-platform development approach or separate builds. The core execution of the application shall be kept on the server side, implying a client-server architecture. The solution must be produced within a constrained timeframe, specifically by March 2005, to align with the publication schedule of a scientific paper, demanding efficient development methodologies and resource management.

The application will interface with external data generation tools such as **Matlab**, which is used for pre-processing biological data into the required tab-delimited format. Deployment considerations may involve technologies like **Tomcat**, **Apache**, and **Ant**, particularly if the server-side component relies on web server infrastructure. Development must adhere to established design conventions and programming standards to ensure maintainability by the client's organization.
userDocumentation: The **grid 3D** application shall be delivered with comprehensive user documentation that includes an intuitive online help system. This documentation will cover installation guides for supported operating systems, a detailed user manual explaining all features and interaction methods, and troubleshooting FAQs. It will also include tutorials demonstrating common use cases, such as loading data, manipulating the 3D grid, and extracting point-specific information, all designed to ensure ease of use and minimize the need for specialist training.
assumptionsAndDependencies: Users of the **grid 3D** application are assumed to possess basic computing skills, including the ability to start and terminate operating system sessions, competently use a keyboard and mouse, and perform basic file handling operations. They are also expected to have command-line input skills and access to a **Java**-enabled internet browser if the application utilizes web-based components. These foundational skills are critical for effective interaction with the application and its associated data files.

The project depends on the availability of **Matlab** for pre-processing and formatting biological data into the required tab-delimited text files, serving as a critical upstream component. Furthermore, it is assumed that users will have access to a workstation meeting the minimum hardware specifications necessary to run the application effectively. Any deviation from these assumptions could impact the user experience or necessitate additional training and support.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: The **grid 3D** application shall provide a graphical user interface (GUI) optimized for scientific visualization and interactive data exploration. The GUI will feature a central 3D rendering area for plotting data points, complemented by clear axis labels that dynamically adjust with grid orientation. Standard input tools, specifically a mouse and keyboard, will be the primary means of interaction for navigation and data point selection. Interactive elements, such as buttons for rotation, zoom, and move functions, will be prominently displayed and intuitively accessible.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The **grid 3D** application shall interface with standard input devices, primarily a mouse and a keyboard, for all user interactions. The mouse will enable precise point selection, grid manipulation (rotation, zoom, pan), and contextual menu interactions. The keyboard will support additional input functionalities, such as shortcuts for view adjustments or data entry for search queries. The system will leverage the graphics processing unit (GPU) of the host computer to render the 3D environment efficiently, ensuring smooth animations and responsiveness.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: The **grid 3D** application will interface with various operating systems, including **Microsoft Windows** (98, 2000, NT, XP), **Linux**, and **Solaris**, necessitating a compatible runtime environment, likely **Java** given the era. It will consume tab-delimited text files generated by external tools, such as **Matlab**, which serve as the primary source of biological data. The application may integrate with web servers like **Tomcat** or **Apache** for deployment, especially if it operates in a client-server mode, and potentially utilizes **Ant** for build automation. Data exchange with these systems will primarily involve file-based input and output of visualization states or exported images.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: Given that the execution of the **grid 3D** application is to be kept on the server side, significant communication interfaces are implied for interaction with client applications. This suggests the use of standard network protocols such as **HTTP** or **HTTPS** for transmitting the rendered 3D view and user interaction commands between the client and server. Data transfer rates must be optimized to ensure responsive real-time interaction, particularly during grid manipulation (rotation, zoom, move). Any sensitive biological data transmitted will require appropriate encryption and secure communication channels to maintain data integrity and confidentiality.

systemFeatures.name: Data Visualization and Plotting
systemFeatures.description: This feature allows users to load biological data from tab-delimited files and visualize it in a scientifically accurate three-dimensional grid. It empowers researchers to gain immediate insights into their datasets by automatically plotting points, labeling axes, and color-coding points based on specific attributes. The ability to handle up to 2000 data points ensures broad applicability for various research scales. This directly addresses the user's need to quickly comprehend complex data structures and patterns, improving the efficiency of data analysis. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects a tab-delimited data file for loading. Response: The system parses the file, validates its format, and prepares the data for plotting.

Stimulus: The data file contains column headings for Name, X_Title, Y_Title, Z_Title, Cluster_Title, and Description. Response: The system dynamically labels the grid axes and assigns the cluster number for color-coding.
Stimulus: The loaded data set contains up to 2000 data points. Response: The system plots all data points within the 3D grid in a scientifically correct manner, maintaining responsiveness.
functionalRequirements: The system shall parse tab-delimited text files, expecting specific column headings for data point attributes. G3-REQ-001
The system shall accurately plot data points in a three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system. G3-REQ-002
The system shall dynamically label the X, Y, and Z axes according to the 'X_Title', 'Y_Title', and 'Z_Title' provided in the data file. G3-REQ-003
The system shall color-code data points based on the 'Cluster_Title' attribute value specified in the input data file. G3-REQ-004
The system shall support the visualization of up to 2000 individual data points without significant performance degradation. G3-REQ-005
The system shall ensure that the plotted data points accurately reflect the numerical values and scientific context provided in the input file. G3-REQ-006

systemFeatures.name: Interactive 3D Grid Manipulation
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides users with robust tools to interactively adjust the orientation and perspective of the 3D data grid. It includes functionalities for rotation, zooming, and panning the grid, allowing researchers to explore data from multiple angles and levels of detail. The responsiveness of these manipulations is crucial for maintaining the user's flow of thought during analysis, directly contributing to an intuitive and engaging user experience. This empowers users to uncover hidden patterns and relationships by dynamically altering their view of the data. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User initiates a drag gesture (e.g., mouse click and drag) on the 3D grid area. Response: The system rotates the grid in real-time according to the drag direction, updating the view continuously.

Stimulus: User activates the zoom function (e.g., mouse scroll wheel or dedicated button). Response: The system adjusts the magnification level of the 3D grid, zooming in or out centered on the current view.

Stimulus: User activates the pan/move function (e.g., specific mouse button and drag). Response: The system translates the grid across the display area, allowing users to shift the center of their focus.
Stimulus: The grid is reoriented. Response: The system ensures that all axis labels remain clear, legible, and correctly aligned with their respective axes regardless of the grid's orientation.
functionalRequirements: The system shall allow users to rotate the 3D grid interactively along all three axes using mouse input. G3-REQ-007
The system shall provide a zoom function, enabling users to magnify or reduce the view of the 3D grid and its data points. G3-REQ-008
The system shall implement a pan or move function, allowing users to translate the entire 3D grid within the display area. G3-REQ-009
The system shall update the 3D view of the grid and data points in real-time, providing immediate visual feedback during manipulation. G3-REQ-010
The system shall ensure that axis labels remain clearly visible and accurately positioned relative to their axes after any grid reorientation. G3-REQ-011

systemFeatures.name: Data Point Interaction and Information Retrieval
systemFeatures.description: This feature enables users to directly interact with individual data points displayed in the 3D grid to retrieve detailed information. It provides immediate feedback upon selection, allowing for a single-click display of a point's name and a double-click for its full descriptive details. The capability to select and retrieve information for multiple points simultaneously enhances comparative analysis, providing researchers with granular control over data exploration. This granular interaction capability allows for in-depth investigation and comparison of specific data points, enriching the overall analysis process. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User performs a single mouse click over a data point. Response: The system highlights the selected data point and displays its name in a temporary overlay or tooltip.

Stimulus: User performs a double mouse click over a data point. Response: The system highlights the selected data point and displays all its associated details (e.g., X, Y, Z, Cluster, Description) in a dedicated information panel.
Stimulus: User clicks on multiple distinct data points sequentially. Response: The system displays the names of all selected data points, either cumulatively or in a list format.
Stimulus: A data point is selected. Response: The system ensures the selected point is clearly distinguishable from unselected points, even within dense clusters.
functionalRequirements: The system shall display the name of a data point upon a single mouse click. G3-REQ-012
The system shall display all detailed attributes (e.g., name, X_Value, Y_Value, Z_Value, Cluster_Title, Description) of a data point upon a double mouse click. G3-REQ-013
The system shall support the selection of multiple data points, displaying the names of all currently selected points. G3-REQ-014
The system shall provide visual feedback (e.g., distinct highlighting) for selected data points to differentiate them from unselected points. G3-REQ-015
The system shall ensure that data points are sufficiently sized to allow for easy selection without inadvertently selecting adjacent points. G3-REQ-016

performanceRequirements: The program shall initialize and fully load a data file containing up to 2000 points and render the initial 3D view within 30 seconds. This is critical for user engagement, particularly when dealing with moderately sized datasets, ensuring researchers can begin their analysis without undue delay. This timeframe directly supports the project's goal of providing a timely and responsive tool for scientific investigation.

The interaction with individual data points (e.g., single-click to display name, double-click to display details) shall have a perceptible delay of no longer than 2 seconds. This ensures a fluid and responsive user experience, preventing frustration during detailed data exploration. Rapid feedback is essential for maintaining user focus and efficiency when investigating specific points of interest.

The response time for any change in the grid's orientation (rotation, zoom, move) shall be fast enough to avoid interrupting the user's flow of thought, targeting sub-second latency for visual updates. This requirement is paramount for interactive exploration, allowing users to intuitively navigate the 3D space without disruptive pauses. The responsiveness of visual manipulation directly impacts the usability and effectiveness of the visualization tool.
safetyRequirements: The system shall ensure that any errors during data parsing or plotting do not lead to misrepresentation of scientific data, which could result in incorrect research conclusions. This is vital for maintaining the integrity of scientific inquiry and preventing erroneous interpretations. Mechanisms shall be in place to validate input data and alert the user to any inconsistencies that might affect visualization accuracy.

The application shall protect against data loss or corruption of input files during its operation, ensuring that the original biological datasets remain unaltered. This safeguard is critical as biological data often represents significant effort and resources. The system will primarily perform read-only operations on user-supplied data files, thereby minimizing any risk of unintentional modification or damage to the source material.
securityRequirements: The system shall enforce appropriate access controls if deployed in a multi-user, server-side environment, ensuring that only authorized users can load, view, or interact with specific datasets. This protects sensitive research data from unauthorized disclosure or manipulation, maintaining confidentiality within a collaborative research institution. Authentication mechanisms, potentially integrating with existing institutional directories, shall be implemented to verify user identities.

All communication channels between client and server components, particularly when transmitting user interactions or rendered views, shall be secured using industry-standard encryption protocols (e.g., **TLS/SSL**). This prevents eavesdropping and tampering with data in transit, safeguarding both the integrity of user commands and the privacy of visualized information. Data privacy is crucial in a research context to protect intellectual property and personal research findings.
softwareQualityAttributes: **Usability**: The **grid 3D** application shall be intuitive to operate, requiring no specialist training for users already proficient with basic computing tasks. This enhances user adoption and reduces the learning curve, allowing researchers to focus on their data rather than the tool. An intuitive interface is paramount for a research tool designed for broad scientific use.

**Reliability**: The system shall consistently plot data points and render the 3D grid in a scientifically correct manner, ensuring the accuracy of all visualizations. This is critical for the credibility of research findings derived from the application. Consistent performance and accurate data representation build trust in the tool's output.

**Portability**: The application shall be executable on diverse operating systems, including **Microsoft Windows** and **Linux** and **Solaris**, without requiring significant platform-specific modifications. This broad compatibility ensures that a wider range of research environments can utilize the tool, maximizing its reach and impact across scientific communities.

**Maintainability**: The codebase of the **grid 3D** application shall be structured, documented, and modularized to facilitate easy diagnosis and correction of defects, and to accommodate future enhancements. This ensures the long-term viability and adaptability of the software, particularly as it may be maintained by internal personnel like Ben Tagger. Well-structured code reduces maintenance costs and time.

**Scalability**: The application shall efficiently handle datasets of up to 2000 data points, maintaining interactive performance without noticeable degradation. While this is the current upper bound, the underlying architecture should be extensible to allow for larger datasets with future optimizations. This ensures the tool remains relevant as data collection methods evolve and dataset sizes increase.

**Testability**: The design of the **grid 3D** application shall allow for comprehensive and efficient testing of all functional and non-functional requirements. This includes the ability to easily simulate data inputs, verify rendering accuracy, and measure performance metrics. High testability reduces the effort required for quality assurance and ensures that all specified behaviors are met.
businessRules: All data visualizations produced by the **grid 3D** application must adhere to recognized scientific standards for accuracy and clarity in graphical representation. This ensures that any interpretations or conclusions drawn from the visualizations are scientifically sound. The application must support the specific data formatting requirements (tab-delimited with defined headers) used by the National Institute for Medical Research (NIMR) parasitology department, facilitating seamless integration into existing research workflows. Data ownership remains with the user and their respective organizations; the system does not claim ownership or rights to user-supplied biological data.

glossary.definition: Data of a biological scientific nature generated by an external product or process. To be used with the **grid 3D** application, it must be formatted in a specified tab-delimited manner.
A user of the **grid 3D** application who utilizes the three-dimensional view to visually present or explain properties of the data to other individuals.
The name of the application for which this document specifies requirements, designed for visualizing biological data in a three-dimensional environment.

A user of the **grid 3D** application who employs its features to pinpoint different 'points of interest' within the visualized data, such as outliers or clusters. An Identifier may subsequently become an Investigator.

A user of the **grid 3D** application who uses the tool to obtain detailed information about specific data points previously identified as points of interest. An Investigator may originate as an Identifier.
National Institute for Medical Research, the institution where the client and customer of the **grid 3D** application are based.
A plain text file where data values within each row are separated by a tab character, used as the input format for biological data in the **grid 3D** application.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: Flowcharts visualize processes and workflows using nodes and directed edges. 'flowchart TD' specifies a top-down direction. Nodes are defined by an alphanumeric ID followed by quoted text for labels. Decision nodes are represented by diamond shapes, processes by rectangles, and start/end points by rounded rectangles or circles. Arrows indicate the flow of control or information. Conditional branches from decision nodes are labeled to indicate the path taken.
flowchartDiagram.caption: G3_FLOWCHART: Application startup and interaction.

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: Sequence diagrams illustrate the temporal order of messages exchanged between participants. 'participant' defines actors or system components. Messages are shown as arrows, with '->>' for synchronous and '->>' for asynchronous calls, and '-->>' for replies. 'activate' and 'deactivate' blocks indicate the lifespan of an object's activation. Aliases can be used for participant IDs to improve readability.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as "User (Identifier/Investigator/Demonstrator)"
    participant G3App as "grid 3D Application"
    participant DataFile as "Data File"

    User->>DataFile: Prepares tab-delimited data
    DataFile-->>User: Provides data file path
    User->>G3App: "Load Data (file_path)"
    activate G3App
    G3App->>DataFile: "Read Data"
    DataFile-->>G3App: "Return Raw Data (2000 points)"
    G3App->>G3App: "Process & Plot 3D Points"
    G3App-->>User: "Display Initial 3D View"
    deactivate G3App

    User->>G3App: "Rotate Grid"
    activate G3App
    G3App->>G3App: "Recalculate View"
    G3App-->>User: "Update 3D View"
    deactivate G3App

    User->>G3App: "Single Click (point_id)"
    activate G3App
    G3App->>G3App: "Retrieve Point Name"
    G3App-->>User: "Display Point Name (popup)"
    deactivate G3App

    User->>G3App: "Double Click (point_id)"
    activate G3App
    G3App->>G3App: "Retrieve Full Point Details"
    G3App-->>User: "Display Point Details (panel)"
    deactivate G3App
sequenceDiagram.caption: G3_SEQUENCE: User data loading and interaction flow.

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: Entity Relationship Diagrams (ERDs) model the logical structure of a database or data entities and their relationships. Entities are defined by a block containing their name and attributes, specifying primary keys (PK), foreign keys (FK), and unique keys (UK). Relationships are indicated by cardinality symbols (e.g., '||' for one, 'o{' for zero-to-many, '}|' for one-to-many) and labels describing the nature of the association. Relationship labels must be enclosed in double quotes.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
    DATA_FILE ||--o{ DATA_POINT : "contains"

    DATA_FILE {
        string file_name PK
        datetime created_at
        string format_type "tab-delimited"
    }

    DATA_POINT {
        string point_id PK
        string file_name FK
        string point_name
        float x_value
        float y_value
        float z_value
        int cluster_number
        string description
    }
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: G3_ERD: Data file and point structure.

introduction.purpose: This document rigorously defines the software requirements for **qheadache**, a computerized game. It outlines the foundational functionalities, performance criteria, and design constraints necessary for its successful development. The primary objective is to enable players to engage with a visual puzzle, track their progress, and manage game states effectively.
introduction.documentConventions: This Software Requirements Specification (SRS) adheres to the guidelines established by IEEE Standard 830-1998, 'Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications'. All requirements are expressed in a precise and unambiguous manner to facilitate clear communication among all project stakeholders. Diagrams are generated using Mermaid syntax, conforming to industry-standard visual modeling practices.
introduction.intendedAudience: The target audience for this document includes software analysts responsible for refining requirements, developers tasked with implementing the system, and quality assurance engineers charged with verifying its correct operation. It also serves as a foundational reference for project managers and other stakeholders interested in the technical aspects of the **qheadache** project.
introduction.productScope: **qheadache** is envisioned as a standalone desktop application designed to provide a single-player puzzle gaming experience. Its core functionality involves manipulating graphical blocks on a virtual board to achieve a predefined winning state. The product encompasses game mechanics, user interface interactions, game state persistence, and player statistics management, operating independently without reliance on external network services or complex integrations.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.

overallDescription.productPerspective: **qheadache** is a new, self-contained product designed to offer a unique puzzle-solving experience. It operates as a standalone application, implying no direct integration with larger systems or existing software families at its inception. The product's design focuses on delivering a complete and immersive gaming experience directly on the user's desktop environment without external dependencies beyond core operating system services.
productFunctions: Board Presentation and Block Interaction: Display a dynamic game board and enable users to select, move, and deselect blocks within defined constraints.
Action Management: Provide functionalities for undoing and redoing previous block movements, enhancing user control and error recovery.
Game Completion and Scoring: Automatically detect the end of a game based on specific conditions, present appropriate finish windows, and record player statistics.
Statistics Management: Track in-game metrics such as block movements and elapsed time, store aggregated game statistics, and display high scores to the player.
File Management: Allow users to save the current game state, open previously saved games, and manage game files, ensuring progress persistence.
Menu Navigation: Offer a structured menu bar for intuitive access to game operations, action controls, statistics display, and application information.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: This user class encompasses individuals who enjoy simple, engaging puzzle games without requiring extensive technical expertise. They may range from beginners, potentially as young as 8 years old, to adults seeking a relaxing mental challenge. They expect an intuitive user interface, clear visual feedback, and straightforward gameplay mechanics without steep learning curves. Their primary motivation is entertainment and light mental stimulation.

This user class includes players who are more dedicated to puzzle games, seeking to improve their scores, optimize their gameplay, and complete challenges efficiently. They are likely familiar with basic computer operations and expect robust game state management, including undo/redo capabilities and detailed performance statistics. They will interact frequently with save/load functions and the statistics display to track their progress and compare against their best attempts.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The **qheadache** software is designed to operate within a standard desktop computing environment. It requires a personal computer equipped with a **keyboard** and a **mouse** for user input, facilitating block selection and movement. A graphical display with a minimum resolution of 800x600 pixels is essential for rendering the game board and user interface elements correctly.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The application must be developed using the **Qt** graphical library, dictating the framework for its user interface and underlying architecture. This mandates adherence to **Qt**'s programming paradigms and ensures compatibility across multiple operating systems that **Qt** supports. Furthermore, the system is constrained to a single-user model, precluding any multi-user or networked gameplay functionalities. All game data, including player statistics and saved games, must be managed through local file storage, without relying on external databases or cloud services. The resolution of the game display is strictly maintained at a minimum of 800x600 pixels, ensuring a consistent visual experience.
userDocumentation: The **qheadache** product will be delivered with minimal embedded user documentation, primarily consisting of an 'About' window accessible via the Help menu. This 'About' window will provide basic application information, including version details and developer credits. Given the game's simplicity, comprehensive external user manuals or online help systems are not anticipated, with the intuitive user interface expected to guide players through gameplay.
assumptionsAndDependencies: It is assumed that the underlying operating system provides a stable and compatible environment for the **Qt** graphical library, ensuring proper rendering and input handling. The existence and reliable operation of a local file system are assumed for saving and loading game data and player statistics. Furthermore, it is assumed that users possess basic computer literacy, including the ability to operate a mouse and keyboard and navigate standard graphical user interfaces. The game's functionality relies solely on these local system capabilities, with no dependencies on network connectivity or external services.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: The **qheadache** user interface will predominantly feature a main window that hosts the interactive game board and a comprehensive menu bar. The visual presentation will be clean and functional, utilizing menus, distinct graphics for game elements, and optional sound cues. Standard GUI elements such as dialog boxes for file operations and message displays for errors will conform to the conventions of the chosen **Qt** framework. User interaction is primarily driven by mouse clicks and movements.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The **qheadache** software requires a **keyboard** and a **mouse** for all user input interactions, including block selection, movement, and menu navigation. While other pointing devices are permissible, they must replicate the core functionalities of a **mouse**, such as cursor control and click events. The display device must support a minimum screen resolution of 800x600 pixels to render the game board and associated interfaces accurately, ensuring all graphical elements are visible and properly scaled.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: **qheadache**'s primary software interface is the **Qt** graphical library, which provides the framework for its entire user interface and core application logic. This dependency ensures cross-platform compatibility with operating systems supported by **Qt**. The system will also interface with the underlying operating system's file system for all data persistence operations, including saving and loading game states, as well as managing player statistics files. No other explicit third-party software integrations or complex API interactions are required.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: The **qheadache** application operates as a fully standalone, offline game. Consequently, there are no specific requirements for communications interfaces such as email, web browser, or network server protocols. The product does not implement any form of inter-process communication, remote data exchange, or network-based multiplayer features. All data storage and retrieval are handled locally on the user's machine.

systemFeatures.name: Game Board Interaction
systemFeatures.description: This feature defines the fundamental gameplay mechanics of **qheadache**, allowing users to visually interact with the game board and manipulate its constituent blocks. It significantly enhances user engagement by providing a direct and intuitive method for solving the puzzle. Technically, this involves rendering the board, detecting user input for block selection, enforcing movement constraints, and updating the visual representation of blocks in real-time. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User left-clicks down on a visible block. Response: The system changes the game state to 'Block Deplacement'.
Stimulus: User moves the mouse while in 'Block Deplacement' state. Response: The selected block follows the mouse movement, adhering to overlap and boundary constraints.
Stimulus: User left-clicks up on a selected block. Response: The system changes the game state to 'Block Selection'.
functionalRequirements: Q-REQ-001: The system shall display a rectangular game board with a height of 5x and a width of 4x, where 'x' is a measure unit between 50 and 100 pixels.
Q-REQ-002: The system shall render four square blocks of side 'x' on the board.
Q-REQ-003: The system shall render four rectangular blocks of dimensions 2x (height) by x (width) on the board.
Q-REQ-004: The system shall render one rectangular block of dimensions x (height) by 2x (width) on the board.
Q-REQ-005: The system shall render one square block of side 2x on the board.
Q-REQ-006: The system shall ensure all blocks are separated by a margin of 0.1x.
Q-REQ-007: The system shall color the game board black and all blocks yellow.
Q-REQ-008: The system shall allow users to select a block by left-clicking down on it.
Q-REQ-009: The system shall allow users to deselect a block by left-clicking up on it.
Q-REQ-010: The system shall move a selected block in real-time to follow the mouse cursor during the 'Block Deplacement' state.
Q-REQ-011: The system shall prevent a selected block from overlapping other blocks.
Q-REQ-012: The system shall prevent a selected block from exiting the defined game board zone.
Q-REQ-013: The system shall maintain a minimum clearance of 0.05x between the selected block and all other blocks during movement.

systemFeatures.name: Action Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides users with the ability to undo and redo previous game actions, offering crucial flexibility and error recovery during gameplay. It significantly enhances user experience by mitigating frustration from accidental moves and encouraging experimentation with different strategies. From a technical perspective, the system must maintain a history of block positions and efficiently revert or reapply these states. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects 'Undo' from the Action menu. Response: The system reverts the game board to the state before the last movement.
Stimulus: User selects 'Redo' from the Action menu. Response: The system reapplies a previously undone movement, displaying the board state after that action.
functionalRequirements: Q-REQ-014: The system shall allow users to undo their last block movement.
Q-REQ-015: The system shall display the game board with block positions as they were prior to the undone movement.
Q-REQ-016: The system shall consider an 'Undo' action as a movement for purposes of redoing.
Q-REQ-017: The system shall make the 'Undo' action unavailable if no previous movements have occurred.
Q-REQ-018: The system shall allow users to redo a block movement that was previously undone.
Q-REQ-019: The system shall display the game board with block positions as they were after the redone movement.
Q-REQ-020: The system shall consider a 'Redo' action as a movement for purposes of undoing.
Q-REQ-021: The system shall make the 'Redo' action unavailable if the last action was not an 'Undo' action.

systemFeatures.name: Game Completion & Scoring
systemFeatures.description: This feature manages the detection of game completion, presentation of results, and recording of player statistics, providing a sense of accomplishment and a mechanism for players to track their performance. It motivates continuous engagement by offering recognition for efficient play. Technically, the system must monitor the position of specific blocks, trigger end-game events, and interact with the statistics management component to store and display results. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: The large 2x2 square block is moved to the bottom of the board. Response: The system freezes player statistics and initiates the end-game sequence.
Stimulus: Game ends, and the player's current block movement count is lower than the highest recorded in statistics. Response: The system displays the 'Finish Window with Statistics'.

Stimulus: Player enters a name in the 'Finish Window with Statistics' and clicks 'OK'. Response: The system records player statistics in the statistic file and closes the window, then displays the Statistic Window.
Stimulus: Game ends, and the player's current block movement count is not lower than the highest recorded. Response: The system displays the 'Simple Finish Window'.
Stimulus: Player clicks 'OK' in the 'Simple Finish Window'. Response: The system closes the window and displays the Statistic Window.
functionalRequirements: Q-REQ-022: The system shall detect game completion when the 2x2 square block reaches the bottom of the board.
Q-REQ-023: The system shall freeze current player statistics (movements and time) upon game completion.
Q-REQ-024: The system shall display the 'Finish Window with Statistics' if the current player's block movements are fewer than the highest recorded.
Q-REQ-025: The 'Finish Window with Statistics' shall contain the text 'You win! Enter your name:', an edit box for up to 20 characters, and an 'OK' pushbutton.
Q-REQ-026: The system shall record the player's name, block movement count, and time taken into the game's statistic file when 'OK' is clicked in the 'Finish Window with Statistics'.
Q-REQ-027: The system shall close the 'Finish Window with Statistics' upon successful recording and display the 'Statistic Window'.
Q-REQ-028: The system shall display the 'Simple Finish Window' if the current player's block movements are not fewer than the highest recorded.
Q-REQ-029: The 'Simple Finish Window' shall contain the text 'You win!' and an 'OK' pushbutton.
Q-REQ-030: The system shall close the 'Simple Finish Window' when 'OK' is clicked and display the 'Statistic Window'.
Q-REQ-031: The system shall increment the number of block movements by 1 for each valid block movement during a game.
Q-REQ-032: The system shall record the time elapsed since the start of the game.
Q-REQ-033: The system shall store game statistics as a list of up to 10 player statistics.
Q-REQ-034: The system shall, upon recording a new player statistic, replace the existing statistic with the greatest number of block movements if 10 player statistics are already recorded.
Q-REQ-035: The system shall display an error message if the statistic file cannot be updated due to permissions or disk space issues.
Q-REQ-036: The system shall allow users to erase all recorded statistics via menu selection.
Q-REQ-037: The system shall, upon erasing statistics, clear all data from the statistic file.
Q-REQ-038: The system shall display the 'Statistic Window' upon game completion or menu selection.
Q-REQ-039: The 'Statistic Window' shall display a listbox with up to 10 lines, each showing a player's name, block movement count, and time used.

systemFeatures.name: Game Data Persistence
systemFeatures.description: This feature enables users to save their current game progress and load previously saved games, providing essential continuity and flexibility in their gameplay experience. It ensures that players can resume their challenges at their convenience without losing progress. From a technical standpoint, this involves managing file input/output operations and serializing/deserializing the complex game state, including block positions and action history. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects 'Open game...' from the Game menu. Response: The system opens a dialog box for file selection.
Stimulus: User selects a game file in the dialog box. Response: The system loads the game data and redraws the board, updating player statistics.
Stimulus: User selects 'Save Game...' from the Game menu for a previously unsaved game. Response: The system processes this as a 'Save game as' action, opening a file selection dialog.
Stimulus: User selects 'Save Game...' from the Game menu for a previously saved game. Response: The system saves the current game state to the previously designated file.
Stimulus: User selects 'Save Game As...' from the Game menu. Response: The system opens a dialog box for file selection and saves the current game data to the chosen file.
Stimulus: User selects 'Exit' from the Game menu while the current game is unsaved. Response: The system displays a dialog box asking if the player wishes to save the game.
Stimulus: User selects 'Yes' in the 'Save on Exit' dialog. Response: The system processes a 'Save' action and then closes the main window.
Stimulus: User selects 'No' in the 'Save on Exit' dialog. Response: The system closes the main window without saving.
functionalRequirements: Q-REQ-040: The system shall allow users to open a previously saved game via menu selection.
Q-REQ-041: The system shall present a file dialog for users to choose a saved game file.
Q-REQ-042: The system shall redraw the game board according to the loaded file data.
Q-REQ-043: The system shall set player statistics to those contained within the loaded game file.
Q-REQ-044: The system shall allow users to save the current game via menu selection.
Q-REQ-045: The system shall, if the game has not been previously saved, perform a 'Save game as' action.

Q-REQ-046: The system shall, if the game has been previously saved, overwrite the existing save file with current block positions, last 10000 previous positions, previous movement count, and elapsed time.
Q-REQ-047: The system shall allow users to save the current game to a new file via menu selection ('Save Game As...').
Q-REQ-048: The system shall present a file dialog for users to choose a new save file path.
Q-REQ-049: The system shall save current block positions, previous positions, movement count, and elapsed time to the chosen file.
Q-REQ-050: The system shall allow users to exit the game via menu selection.
Q-REQ-051: The system shall, if the game is unsaved, display a dialog box prompting the user to save with 'Yes' and 'No' options.
Q-REQ-052: The system shall perform the 'Save Game' action if 'Yes' is selected in the exit save prompt, then close the main window.
Q-REQ-053: The system shall close the main window immediately if 'No' is selected in the exit save prompt.

systemFeatures.name: Menu Navigation
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides a centralized and intuitive menu bar for accessing all primary functionalities of the **qheadache** application. It streamlines user interaction by grouping related commands and making critical actions readily available. Technically, this involves rendering a consistent menu structure and dispatching appropriate commands to the game logic based on user selections. Priority: Medium.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User clicks on the 'Game' menu item. Response: The system displays a dropdown menu with 'Open game...', 'Save Game...', 'Save Game As...', and 'Exit' options.
Stimulus: User selects an item from the 'Game' menu. Response: The system performs the corresponding file management action and closes the menu.
Stimulus: User clicks on the 'Action' menu item. Response: The system displays a dropdown menu with 'Undo' and 'Redo' options.
Stimulus: User selects an item from the 'Action' menu. Response: The system performs the corresponding action management function, ensuring availability constraints are met, and closes the menu.
Stimulus: User clicks on the 'Statistics' menu item. Response: The system displays a dropdown menu with 'Display' and 'Erase' options.
Stimulus: User selects an item from the 'Statistics' menu. Response: The system either displays the 'Statistic Window' or erases statistics, then closes the menu.
Stimulus: User clicks on the 'Help' menu item. Response: The system displays a dropdown menu with an 'About' option.
Stimulus: User selects 'About' from the 'Help' menu. Response: The system displays the 'About Window' with application information and closes the menu.
functionalRequirements: Q-REQ-054: The system shall provide a 'Game' menu containing 'Open game...', 'Save Game...', 'Save Game As...', and 'Exit' options in that specific order.
Q-REQ-055: The system shall, upon selection of 'Open game...', invoke the Open Game functionality (Q-REQ-040).
Q-REQ-056: The system shall, upon selection of 'Save Game...', invoke the Save Game functionality (Q-REQ-044).
Q-REQ-057: The system shall, upon selection of 'Save Game As...', invoke the Save Game As functionality (Q-REQ-047).
Q-REQ-058: The system shall, upon selection of 'Exit', invoke the Exit functionality (Q-REQ-050).
Q-REQ-059: The system shall provide an 'Action' menu containing 'Undo' and 'Redo' options in that specific order.
Q-REQ-060: The system shall dynamically enable or disable 'Undo' and 'Redo' menu items based on their availability.
Q-REQ-061: The system shall, upon selection of 'Undo', invoke the Undo Action functionality (Q-REQ-014).
Q-REQ-062: The system shall, upon selection of 'Redo', invoke the Redo Action functionality (Q-REQ-018).
Q-REQ-063: The system shall provide a 'Statistics' menu containing 'Display' and 'Erase' options in that specific order.
Q-REQ-064: The system shall, upon selection of 'Display', display the 'Statistic Window' (Q-REQ-038).
Q-REQ-065: The system shall, upon selection of 'Erase', invoke the Statistics Erasing functionality (Q-REQ-036).
Q-REQ-066: The system shall provide a 'Help' menu containing an 'About' option.
Q-REQ-067: The system shall, upon selection of 'About', display an 'About Window'.
Q-REQ-068: The 'About Window' shall display the text 'Qheadache 1.0 by Jean-Philippe Brossat jp_brossat@yahoo.fr'.

performanceRequirements: Q-REQ-069: The system shall render block movements on the game board with no perceptible lag, maintaining a smooth visual experience, within 50 milliseconds of user input. This is critical for an enjoyable interactive puzzle experience and directly impacts user satisfaction.

Q-REQ-070: The system shall load saved game states and display the reconstructed board within 500 milliseconds of file selection. Rapid loading minimizes user waiting times and improves overall responsiveness for resuming gameplay.

Q-REQ-071: The system shall execute 'Undo' and 'Redo' actions, updating the board state within 100 milliseconds. Quick state transitions are essential for maintaining user flow and confidence in action recovery features.
safetyRequirements: Q-REQ-072: The system shall prevent data corruption of saved game files and statistics files during read/write operations by implementing robust error handling and rollback mechanisms. This ensures the integrity of user progress and historical performance data.

Q-REQ-073: The system shall display clear, user-friendly error messages if file operations (save, load, erase statistics) fail due to system-level issues such as insufficient disk space or incorrect file permissions. This provides crucial feedback to the user and guides them in resolving potential problems.
securityRequirements: Q-REQ-074: The system shall store all game-related data (saved games, statistics) locally on the user's machine, relying on the underlying operating system's file access controls for data protection. Since it's a standalone game without network features, this approach is sufficient and avoids complex internal security mechanisms.

Q-REQ-075: The system shall not collect or transmit any personal user information, nor shall it require user authentication beyond what the local operating system provides for file access. This ensures user privacy and simplifies the security footprint of the application.
softwareQualityAttributes: Q-REQ-076: Portability: The system shall be portable to all operating systems supported by the **Qt** graphical library, specifically targeting **Windows OS** as a primary platform. This broadens the user base and maximizes market reach for the game.

Q-REQ-077: Usability: The system shall provide an intuitive and easy-to-learn user interface, allowing new users to understand game mechanics and navigate menus without external guidance within 5 minutes of first use. High usability reduces user frustration and enhances initial adoption.

Q-REQ-078: Maintainability: The system's codebase shall be well-structured and documented, leveraging the modularity of the **Qt** framework to facilitate future updates, bug fixes, and feature enhancements by development teams. Good maintainability reduces long-term development costs.

Q-REQ-079: Reliability: The system shall operate without crashes or unexpected behavior for at least 8 hours of continuous gameplay under normal usage conditions. High reliability ensures a consistent and positive user experience, preventing loss of progress or system instability.

Q-REQ-080: Testability: The system shall be designed to allow for comprehensive automated and manual testing of all functional requirements, particularly for game logic, UI interactions, and file management. Strong testability ensures the quality and correctness of the software prior to release.
businessRules: Q-REQ-081: The game is strictly a single-player experience; no multiplayer or collaborative modes are supported.
Q-REQ-082: Player statistics are limited to the top 10 scores based on the lowest number of block movements, with ties potentially resolved by elapsed time.
