introduction.purpose: This document specifies the functional requirements for the **fishing** Electronic Logbook Software System (ELSS) used by UK fishing vessels. It outlines the capabilities necessary for accurate record-keeping and data submission to the UK fisheries administrations' Electronic Recording and Reporting System (ERS). Furthermore, the document includes a Product Profile and Self-Declaration Form, which provides a structured format for suppliers to document their product's features for the ELSS Approval Programme. The primary goal is to ensure that proposed ELSS products conform to regulatory standards and can be formally validated.
introduction.documentConventions: This document adheres to the IEEE 830-1998 Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications. Key terms and abbreviations are defined in Appendix A: Glossary to ensure clarity and consistency. Analysis models are presented using Mermaid syntax for enhanced visualization of system architecture and interaction, as detailed in Appendix B. All requirement identifiers are prefixed with F- to signify their origin from the **fishing** project.
introduction.intendedAudience: The primary audience for this document includes software developers and testers responsible for implementing and validating the **fishing** ELSS. Additionally, project managers, technical leads, and quality assurance personnel within supplier organizations will utilize this specification to guide development efforts. Representatives from the UK Fisheries Administration will use this document to evaluate and approve ELSS products for compliance with regulatory standards.
introduction.productScope: The **fishing** ELSS is a specialized software application designed for onboard use on UK fishing vessels. Its scope encompasses the capture, validation, storage, and transmission of electronic logbook data, including fishing activities, transhipments, and landing declarations. The system is specifically engineered to interface with the UK Fisheries Administrations' ERS system and generate reports compliant with European Council and Commission Regulations. This specification excludes onshore data entry methods or direct user interaction with the ERS website.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.
Council Regulation (EC) No. 1966/2006 concerning electronic recording and reporting of fishing activities.
Commission Regulation (EC) No. 1077/2008 laying down detailed rules for the application of Council Regulation (EC) No. 1966/2006.

overallDescription.productPerspective:
The **fishing** Electronic Logbook Software System is a new, self-contained product designed to replace traditional paper logbooks currently used on UK fishing vessels. This initiative is mandated by Council Regulation (EC) No. 1966/2006, which requires electronic logbooks for vessels exceeding 15 meters in overall length. The system addresses the staged implementation, initially for vessels over 24 meters by January 2010 and subsequently for those over 15 meters by July 2011. 

The ELSS serves as a critical component within the larger UK Fisheries Administrations' Electronic Recording and Reporting System (ERS) ecosystem. It acts as the primary data generation point on vessels, providing validated and encrypted logbook data to the central ERS for regulatory compliance and monitoring. This integration ensures seamless data flow and adherence to European regulatory frameworks for fisheries management.
productFunctions: Capture and validate fishing activity data according to regulatory requirements.
Generate various types of electronic logbook reports (e.g., Departure, Fishing Activity, Landing Declaration).
Facilitate the correction and deletion of previously transmitted data.
Transmit encrypted logbook data to the UK fisheries administrations' ERS system via email.
Receive and process acknowledgments from the ERS system for transmitted data.
Provide onboard printing capabilities for logbook data and electronic print files.
Manage user access and security for vessel owners and masters.
Store logbook reports and corrections onboard the vessel until the end of each fishing trip.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: The Vessel Master is the primary operator of the **fishing** ELSS, responsible for accurately entering daily fishing activities, managing report submissions, and handling data corrections or deletions. They require training in the system's operation and an understanding of fishing regulations. The Master possesses specific security credentials assigned by the vessel owner, enabling them to “sign” electronic logbook data and commit to associated responsibilities.

The Vessel Owner typically oversees the administration of the **fishing** ELSS and is responsible for setting up and managing subsidiary user accounts, such as those for vessel masters. The owner is also concerned with ensuring the system's compliance with regulatory requirements and managing software updates. They are the initial recipient of the ELSS and hold the primary administrative credentials.

This user class represents the automated systems and human operators within the UK Fisheries Administration responsible for receiving, processing, and validating data transmitted by the **fishing** ELSS. They issue acknowledgments or error messages back to the ELSS. This class primarily interacts with the ELSS through established communication protocols and data formats, ensuring regulatory oversight and data collection efficiency.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment:
The **fishing** ELSS is designed to operate exclusively on onboard computer systems within UK fishing vessels. It supports various **operating systems**, including **Windows XP Service Pack 3**, **Windows Vista Service Pack 2**, and **Linux Redhat** distributions, as specified for product identification. The system must coexist peacefully with other onboard electronic systems, such as **GPS** devices for location and time data, onboard **weighing systems**, and existing onboard **databases** used for pre-populating certain data fields.

The system relies on the vessel’s onboard communication infrastructure, particularly its **email service**, for transmitting data to the UK Fisheries Administration's ERS system. An onboard **printer** is required for generating hard copies of logbook data. The software must function reliably in a marine environment, which may present challenges related to power stability, network connectivity, and hardware durability.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The **fishing** ELSS must adhere strictly to the UK XML/XSD definitions for all data input and output formats, ensuring compatibility with the UK Fisheries Administrations' ERS system.
All transmitted data must be encapsulated in an **XML** file and attached to an email, with the email subject header formatted precisely according to the GBRRN attribute.
All outgoing **XML** documents must be secured using **PGP encryption** to maintain data confidentiality and integrity during transmission.
The system is constrained to provide English (UK) localizations for all user interface elements and must record all dates and times in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC).

Data retention policies mandate that all logbook reports and corrections remain on the system until the completion of each fishing trip, specifically until the submission of the electronic landing declaration or a transhipment report.
Software updates to the ELSS require re-approval if they impact the system's ability to meet specified requirements, ensuring continuous regulatory compliance and validation.
userDocumentation:
The **fishing** ELSS will be accompanied by comprehensive user documentation to assist both Vessel Masters and Owners in its effective operation. This documentation will include a detailed user manual providing step-by-step instructions for data entry, report generation, transmission procedures, and troubleshooting. Additionally, an online help system will be integrated into the ELSS, offering context-sensitive assistance for various features.

Tutorials will be provided to guide new users through initial setup and common workflows, such as completing a fishing activity report or initiating a data correction. These materials will also incorporate guidance on completing the Product Profile and Self Declaration Form as part of the ELSS Approval Programme. All documentation will adhere to a consistent format and language, utilizing clear and concise terminology.
assumptionsAndDependencies:
It is assumed that UK fishing vessels operating the **fishing** ELSS possess a reliable and operational onboard communication system, including a stable internet connection and an active email service. The accuracy of transmitted data is dependent on the availability and correct functioning of integrated onboard systems such as **GPS** for positional data and **weighing systems** for catch details. It is further assumed that the UK XML/XSD definitions and associated lists of valid codes, provided by the UK Fisheries Monitoring Centre (FMC), will remain stable or that any changes will be communicated promptly and clearly to ELSS suppliers.

The successful operation of the **fishing** ELSS is critically dependent on the continuous availability and responsiveness of the UK Fisheries Administrations' ERS system for receiving data and returning acknowledgments. Any delays or failures in the ERS system could disrupt the ELSS's ability to transmit data and receive confirmations. The project also assumes the availability of compatible **PGP encryption** libraries and tools for integration into the ELSS, ensuring secure data transmission.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces:
The **fishing** ELSS will feature intuitive data capture screens designed for onboard use, facilitating the entry of logbook, transhipment, and landing declaration data. These interfaces will utilize English (UK) localizations, ensuring familiarity and ease of use for UK vessel masters. All dates and times displayed and entered will be in UTC to maintain consistency across regulatory reporting.

The user interface will clearly distinguish between original, corrected, and deleted data, providing visual cues to help users manage their reports effectively. Standard navigation elements and consistent screen layouts will be employed, adhering to principles of good user experience in a potentially challenging maritime environment. Error messages will be clear, concise, and actionable, guiding users in resolving data input issues.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces:
The **fishing** ELSS must interface with several hardware components common on fishing vessels. It requires connectivity to an onboard **printer** to generate hard copies of logbook data, including landing declarations and various regulatory returns. The system will integrate with **GPS** devices to automatically populate date, time, and location information for fishing activities and transmissions, reducing manual entry errors.

Integration with onboard **weighing systems** is essential for accurately recording catch weights, minimizing discrepancies between captured data and actual catch. Furthermore, the ELSS should be designed to support integration with other existing onboard electronic systems and **databases**, allowing for the pre-population of data and preventing redundant data entry where possible.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces:
The **fishing** ELSS is designed to interact primarily with the UK Fisheries Administrations' **ERS system**. This interface involves sending **XML** documents containing fishing logbook data and receiving **acknowledgment (RET)** messages in return. The communication protocol relies heavily on an onboard **email service**, which acts as the transport layer for encrypted **XML** attachments.

Internally, the ELSS will interface with its own local **database** to store all logbook reports, corrections, and transmission statuses throughout a fishing trip. The system may also integrate with other existing onboard software applications or **databases** to retrieve vessel information, crew details, or fishing gear specifications, thereby streamlining the data entry process. These interfaces will utilize defined **XML** schemas and potentially **REST API** calls for data exchange with internal components.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces:
The **fishing** ELSS requires robust communication capabilities for secure and reliable data transmission. The primary communication mechanism is **email**, where **XML** documents are sent as attachments. These emails must adhere to a strict format, including a standard subject header and specific naming conventions for the **XML** file, ensuring proper processing by the ERS system.

All **XML** attachments transmitted via email must be protected using **PGP encryption**, maintaining the confidentiality and integrity of sensitive fishing data. While email is the default, the system acknowledges the potential for alternative communication methods (e.g., **FTP**, **HTTP** over satellite links) if approved by the Validation Authority. Requirements also extend to establishing communication standards for data transfer rates and synchronization mechanisms to handle potentially intermittent satellite connectivity.

systemFeatures.name: Data Capture and Validation
systemFeatures.description: This feature enables the **fishing** ELSS to collect all necessary data pertaining to a UK fishing vessel's activities and ensure its accuracy and compliance with regulatory standards. It provides user-friendly screens for data input and employs rigorous validation against predefined XML schemas. The goal is to minimize entry errors and facilitate the creation of valid reports for submission to the UK fisheries administrations. This feature is critical for maintaining data integrity and ensuring the system's foundational compliance with regulatory requirements.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: The Master of the vessel enters fishing activity data into a capture screen. Response: The system immediately validates the entered data against the UK XML/XSD definitions and provides real-time feedback on any discrepancies.
Stimulus: The Master attempts to save an incomplete or invalid report. Response: The system prevents the save operation and highlights all mandatory or erroneous fields requiring correction.

Stimulus: The ELSS automatically populates data fields from onboard GPS or weighing systems. Response: The system displays the automatically populated data and allows the Master to review and manually adjust if necessary.
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide data capture screens for all required logbook, transhipment, and landing declaration information.
The system shall capture all data necessary for recording fishing activities undertaken by a UK fishing vessel.
The system shall validate all entered data against the UK XML/XSD definitions prior to transmission.
The system shall ensure that all dates and times captured are recorded in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC).
The system shall allow data fields to be automatically populated from other onboard electronic systems (e.g., GPS, weighing systems, existing databases).

systemFeatures.name: Report Generation and Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature encompasses the **fishing** ELSS's ability to generate various types of electronic logbook reports, including Departure, Fishing Activity, and Landing Declarations. It also provides the functionality for Masters to correct or delete previously submitted reports, ensuring that the records accurately reflect the vessel's operations. The system manages the lifecycle of these reports, from creation to transmission and subsequent amendments. This feature is vital for the dynamic nature of fishing operations, where reports may require updates due to changing circumstances or errors.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: The Master selects a specific report type (e.g., "Fishing Activity Report") to generate. Response: The system presents a formatted report ready for data entry or review, pre-populated with available information.

Stimulus: The Master initiates a correction for a previously transmitted report. Response: The system loads the original report data, marks it as "correction in progress," and allows the Master to modify only the necessary fields, then generates a new complete report for transmission.

Stimulus: The Master requests the deletion of a previously transmitted report. Response: The system flags the original report for deletion, generates a formatted deletion request, and prevents further modifications to the original report.
functionalRequirements: The system shall support the generation of Departure (DEP) reports.
The system shall support the generation of Fishing Activity (FAR) reports.
The system shall support the generation of Relocation of Catch (RLC) reports.
The system shall support the generation of Transhipment (TRA) reports.
The system shall support the generation of Entry into Zone (COE) and Exit from Zone (COX) reports.
The system shall support the generation of Discard (DIS) reports.
The system shall support the generation of Prior Notification of Arrival to Port (PNO) reports.
The system shall support the generation of End of Fishing (EOF) reports.
The system shall support the generation of Return to Port (RTP) reports.
The system shall support the generation of Landing Declaration (LAN) reports.
The system shall provide facilities to capture and deliver for transmission corrections to previously successfully transmitted data, sending the entire corrected report.
The system shall provide facilities to transmit deletions to previously successfully transmitted data.

systemFeatures.name: Data Transmission
systemFeatures.description: This feature manages the secure and timely transmission of electronic logbook data from the **fishing** ELSS to the UK Fisheries Administrations' ERS system. It supports both automatic and Master-controlled transmission triggers, ensuring compliance with reporting frequencies. The system is responsible for encrypting the data and formatting the transmission email. This feature is paramount for fulfilling the regulatory requirement for electronic reporting and ensuring that administrative bodies receive timely and accurate information.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Daily transmission deadline (24:00 UTC) is reached, and the vessel is at sea. Response: The system automatically generates, encrypts, and transmits any outstanding logbook data for the day.

Stimulus: The Master completes a Landing Declaration and manually initiates transmission. Response: The system processes the declaration, encrypts it, and sends it to the ERS via the onboard email service.

Stimulus: The ERS system sends an acknowledgment message for a transmitted report. Response: The system receives the acknowledgment, matches it to the original transmission, and updates the report's status (success or error) in the user interface.
functionalRequirements: The system shall output all data as an XML file for transmission to the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system.
The system shall transmit data automatically at least daily by 24:00 UTC, immediately after the last fishing operation, immediately on departing port, and immediately after a transhipment.

The system shall permit the Master of the vessel to generate and transmit data immediately upon completion of a Landing Declaration, before entering port, at the time of inspection at sea, or at the request of the UK fisheries administrations.
The system shall encrypt all XML documents attached to emails to the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system using PGP.
The system shall include the XML document as an attachment to an email with a standard Subject header.
The system shall format the email subject header to consist of "ERS – " prefixed to the contents of the GBRRN attribute (e.g., ERS – A12345YYYYMMDDXXXXXX).
The system shall name the XML document file based on the GBRRN attribute, with an ".xml" suffix (e.g., A12345YYYYMMDDXXXXXX.xml).
The system shall pad the RSS Number with trailing "X" characters to ensure it is always 6 characters in length within the GBRRN attribute.
The system shall be able to receive and match acknowledgment (RET) messages from the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system to appropriate transmitted data.
The system shall display confirmation of successful transmission acknowledgment or any error messages from negative acknowledgments.

systemFeatures.name: Printing Capabilities
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides the **fishing** ELSS with the ability to generate both hard copies and electronic print files of logbook data. It caters to various onboard and onshore needs, from local record-keeping to providing transport documents and regulatory returns. The system ensures the integrity of electronic print files by protecting them against modification. This feature supports offline documentation and compliance requirements, complementing the electronic transmission processes.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: The Master requests a printout of the current logbook data. Response: The system sends the formatted data to the onboard printer, producing a physical copy.

Stimulus: The Master requests an electronic print file of a landing declaration. Response: The system generates a protected, non-modifiable electronic file (e.g., PDF) and makes it available for distribution (e.g., via email).
Stimulus: The Master requests a specific hard copy for third-country fishing regulations. Response: The system formats and prints the required logsheet or declaration.
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide the ability to print out ELSS logbook data, including landing declarations, using an onboard printer.
The system shall be capable of generating formatted electronic print files that cannot be modified once created and distributed.
The system shall support the generation of hard copies required when fishing in third-country or Regional Fisheries waters (e.g., NAFO, Faroese, Norwegian waters).
The system shall support the generation of hard copy logsheets or landing declarations to act as transport or takeover documents.

systemFeatures.name: Security and User Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature establishes the security framework and user access controls for the **fishing** ELSS, ensuring that only authorized individuals can access and manipulate sensitive fishing data. It provides mechanisms for vessel owners to manage user accounts for masters and ensures that all data entry and transmissions are traceable to a specific user. The system also embeds a unique identifier for each ELSS instance in transmitted reports, enhancing accountability and traceability. This feature is fundamental to regulatory compliance, data integrity, and overall system security.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: The Vessel Owner logs into the ELSS for the first time. Response: The system prompts the owner to set up their primary username and password and then provides an interface to create subsidiary user accounts.
Stimulus: The Master attempts to access the ELSS. Response: The system authenticates the Master using their unique username and password.
Stimulus: A user enters data into a logbook report. Response: The system automatically records the username of the person who entered the data into the report and prepares it for "signing."
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide security and access controls for its operation.
The system shall provide one unique username and password for the owner of each vessel.
The system shall allow the vessel owner to set up subsidiary users (e.g., the Master) with their own unique usernames and passwords.
The system shall record the username of the person who entered the data in each completed report and transmission.
The system shall provide a mechanism for the person entering data to "sign" the Electronic Logbook data, acknowledging their responsibilities.
The system shall automatically enter a unique internal identifier for its instance into each transmission to identify the source ELSS.

performanceRequirements: The **fishing** ELSS shall generate all XML report files within 5 seconds of a user initiating the action or an automatic trigger, ensuring minimal delay in data preparation. Automatic daily transmissions shall commence no later than 23:59 UTC, with the entire process (generation, encryption, email queueing) completing within 1 minute, to meet regulatory deadlines. The system shall process acknowledgment messages from the ERS system and update report statuses within 10 seconds of receipt, providing timely feedback to the Master.
safetyRequirements: The **fishing** ELSS shall implement robust data validation mechanisms to prevent the entry of invalid or malformed data that could lead to regulatory non-compliance. All electronic print files generated by the system shall be designed with inherent protections against modification, such as read-only formats, to ensure their integrity for legal and auditing purposes. The system shall maintain internal data consistency, particularly when handling corrections and deletions, to prevent accidental data loss or corruption of logbook records.
securityRequirements:
The **fishing** ELSS shall secure all outgoing XML data transmissions using **PGP encryption** to protect sensitive fishing activity data from unauthorized access during transit. The system shall enforce strict user authentication through unique usernames and passwords for both vessel owners and masters, preventing unauthorized access to the application and its data. Access controls shall restrict specific functions (e.g., user account creation) to the vessel owner role.

The system shall incorporate a unique internal software identifier into every transmitted report, enabling forensic traceability of the data's origin. It shall also log all significant user actions, such as report creation, modification, deletion, and transmission, for audit purposes, associating each action with the performing user.
softwareQualityAttributes:
Adaptability: The system shall be adaptable to minor changes in UK XML/XSD definitions with minimal software recompilation, potentially through configuration updates. This is crucial for evolving regulatory landscapes and maintaining compliance with updated standards.

Reliability: The system shall consistently generate accurate and valid XML reports and successfully transmit them, with a transmission success rate exceeding 99.5% under normal operating conditions. It shall be resilient to temporary network outages, queueing transmissions until connectivity is restored.

Usability: The user interface shall be designed with a clear, intuitive layout and adhere to English (UK) localization standards, minimizing the learning curve for vessel masters. Key operations, such as report entry and transmission, shall be completable within a few clicks or keystrokes.

Maintainability: The software architecture shall be modular and well-documented, allowing for straightforward updates and bug fixes by development teams. Adherence to defined coding standards and API specifications will facilitate future enhancements and integrations.

Portability: The **fishing** ELSS shall be deployable across a range of **Windows** (XP SP3, Vista SP2) and **Linux Redhat** operating systems without requiring significant code modifications, ensuring broad compatibility with existing onboard vessel systems.

Testability: The system shall provide a dedicated test transmission facility that allows communication with a test ERS environment without storing data, enabling comprehensive testing of communication pathways. All functional requirements shall be designed to be independently verifiable through automated or manual test cases.
businessRules: F-BR-001: All UK fishing vessels exceeding 15 meters in overall length are required to use electronic logbooks, as per Council Regulation (EC) No. 1966/2006.
F-BR-002: The **fishing** ELSS shall not be provided for onshore use by agents or representatives; onshore data entry must occur through the ERS website or other specified offline submission methods.
F-BR-003: ELSS software updates that impact documented requirements must undergo re-approval by the UK Fisheries Administration before deployment.

F-BR-004: All logbook reports and corrections must be retained on the ELSS until at least the end of the current fishing trip, specifically until the submission of the electronic landing declaration or a transhipment report.

glossary.definition: A message sent by the UK Fisheries Administration's ERS system to the ELSS confirming the receipt and processing status of a transmitted report.
An operation type used in the ELSS to transmit a formatted correction request for previously sent data.
An operation type used in the ELSS to capture and deliver formatted ELSS data for initial transmission to the ERS system.
An operation type used in the ELSS to capture and deliver a formatted deletion request for previously sent data.
Electronic Logbook Software System. The software product specified in this document for use on UK fishing vessels.
Electronic Recording and Reporting System. The central system of the UK Fisheries Administrations that receives and processes electronic logbook data.
Fisheries Monitoring Centre. The body responsible for providing data definitions and valid codes for ELSS suppliers.
Great Britain Registration Reference Number. A unique attribute defined in the UK XML/XSD, consisting of the vessel's RSS Number, current date, and a sequence number.
Pretty Good Privacy. An encryption program used for encrypting and decrypting data, required for securing XML transmissions from the ELSS.
Registration of Shipping System Number. A unique identifier for a vessel, used in generating the GBRRN.
The XML Schema Definition provided by the UK Fisheries Administrations that defines the structure and valid content for all electronic logbook data.
Coordinated Universal Time. The primary time standard by which the world regulates clocks and time, required for all dates and times in the ELSS.
Extensible Markup Language. A markup language that defines a set of rules for encoding documents in a format that is both human-readable and machine-readable.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart utilizes standard Mermaid syntax for defining processes and decisions. Nodes represent steps or states, and arrows indicate transitions. Rectangular nodes signify a process, rounded rectangles denote start/end points, and diamonds represent decision points. Labels with spaces are enclosed in double quotes for clarity. All node IDs are alphanumeric, ensuring strict compliance with naming conventions. The diagram illustrates a top-down (TD) flow for clear progression.
flowchartDiagram.caption: F_FLOWCHART: Data transmission process flow.

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram describes the temporal interactions between participants in the **fishing** ELSS. Participants are actors or system components, and arrows represent messages exchanged in chronological order. Solid arrows indicate synchronous calls, while dashed arrows denote replies. This diagram adheres to the principle of using standard message arrows, avoiding explicit activation/deactivation blocks unless simulating complex recursive scenarios not present here. Aliases for participants are not quoted.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant VesselMaster as "Vessel Master"
    participant ELSS as "fishing ELSS"
    participant OnboardEmail as "Onboard Email Service"
    participant ERS as "UK Fisheries ERS"
    participant Database as "ELSS Database"

    VesselMaster->>ELSS: Create/Edit Logbook Report
    ELSS->>ELSS: Validate data against XML/XSD
    ELSS->>ELSS: Generate encrypted XML file
    ELSS->>OnboardEmail: Send XML attachment (PGP)
    OnboardEmail->>ERS: Transmit Encrypted XML (Email)
    ERS->>ERS: Process Logbook Data
    ERS-->>OnboardEmail: Send Acknowledgment (RET)
    OnboardEmail-->>ELSS: Deliver Acknowledgment (RET)
    ELSS->>ELSS: Match RET to original message
    ELSS->>ELSS: Update transmission status
    ELSS->>Database: Store Report and Status
    Database-->>ELSS: Confirmation
sequenceDiagram.caption: F_SEQUENCE: Logbook report transmission workflow.

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) uses Mermaid syntax to model data entities and their relationships. Entities are defined with attributes, and primary (PK), foreign (FK), and unique (UK) keys are clearly indicated. Relationship lines specify cardinality (e.g., one-to-many, one-to-one) and are labeled with descriptive, quoted text. Entity names with spaces are enclosed in double quotes. Attribute names are alphanumeric, adhering to strict naming conventions without spaces or quotes.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
    VESSEL ||--o{ USER : "has_access"
    USER ||--o{ REPORT : "creates"
    VESSEL ||--o{ REPORT : "generates"
    REPORT ||--o{ LOG_ENTRY : "contains"
    REPORT ||--|| TRANSMISSION : "results_in"
    TRANSMISSION ||--o| ACKNOWLEDGEMENT : "expects"

    VESSEL {
        int vessel_id PK
        string rss_number UK
        string name
        string registration_number
    }

    USER {
        int user_id PK
        int vessel_id FK
        string username UK
        string password_hash
        string role
        string email
    }

    REPORT {
        int report_id PK
        int vessel_id FK
        int user_id FK
        string report_type
        string gbrrn UK
        datetime created_at
        datetime last_modified_at
        bool is_correction
        bool is_deletion
    }

    LOG_ENTRY {
        int log_entry_id PK
        int report_id FK
        datetime entry_timestamp
        string location_gps
        string activity_details
        decimal catch_weight
        string species
    }

    TRANSMISSION {
        int transmission_id PK
        int report_id FK
        datetime transmitted_at
        string file_name
        string subject_header
        bool is_encrypted
        string transmission_status
    }

    ACKNOWLEDGEMENT {
        int ack_id PK
        int transmission_id FK
        datetime received_at
        string ack_status
        string error_message
    }

introduction.purpose: The primary objective of this Software Requirements Specification is to establish a comprehensive repository for the high-level requirements guiding the architectural design of the **clarus high** system. These requirements systematically capture the fundamental needs articulated within the **clarus high** Concept of Operations and during consultative engagements with prospective users and participants. This document serves as the foundational blueprint for future design verification and validation activities, facilitating the progressive refinement and expansion of system requirements throughout the project lifecycle. Its content is crucial for ensuring a shared understanding among all project stakeholders regarding the system's core functionalities and constraints.
introduction.documentConventions: This document adheres to the structural and content guidelines stipulated by the IEEE 830-1998 Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications. All requirements, features, and analytical models are systematically identified using a unique project-specific prefix, CH-, to ensure comprehensive traceability throughout the development process. Technical terms, acronyms, and abbreviations are defined within Appendix A, promoting clarity and consistency in communication across all project phases and stakeholders. The aim is to provide a clear, unambiguous, and verifiable specification for the **clarus high** system.
introduction.intendedAudience: The primary audience for this document encompasses a broad spectrum of project stakeholders, including executive decision-makers responsible for strategic direction, key stakeholders invested in the system's success, system designers tasked with technical implementation, and quality assurance testers ensuring system integrity. This specification provides critical information for understanding the system's objectives, functional capabilities, and performance criteria. It facilitates alignment on scope and expectations, serving as a reference point for all technical and managerial discussions related to the **clarus high** development.
introduction.productScope: The **clarus high** initiative, sponsored by the Federal Highway Administration (**FHWA**), aims to establish a North American resource for collecting, quality-controlling, and disseminating surface transportation weather and road condition observations. This system is designed to enhance safety and mobility on roads by providing critical environmental data to State Departments of Transportation (**DOTs**), improving general weather forecasting, and supporting real-time operational responses to weather events. It seeks to integrate diverse observational data to enable better predictions in the atmospheric boundary layer, thereby advancing meteorological support services and data coverage.
introduction.productScope2: The initiative fundamentally demonstrates an open and integrated approach to observational data management, consolidating information from a multitude of independent data collection systems. The **clarus high** system specifically focuses on collecting atmospheric, pavement, and hydrologic data relevant to surface transportation. Its architecture is envisioned to include a 'one-stop Internet portal' for all relevant observations, continuous quality control with operator feedback, and support for emerging technologies like vehicle-based sensors and remote sensing. This platform acts as a 'network of networks' for environmental data, enabling autonomous data sharing while providing a unified view.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.
Clarus – The Nationwide Surface Transportation Weather Observing and Forecast System; Pisano, Pol, Stern, and Goodwin; TRB 2005.
National ITS Architecture, Version 5.0; FHWA, U.S. DOT; October 2003.
Weather Information in the National ITS Architecture Version 5.0; Meridian Environmental Technology, Inc.; August 2004.
Clarus Initiative Coordinating Committee (ICC) Management Plan (Revision 1); James Pol, U.S. DOT; September 2004.
Surface Transportation Decision Support Requirements, Version 1.0; Mitretek Systems, Inc.; January 2000.
Weather Information for Surface Transportation: National Needs Assessment Report; Office of the Federal Coordinator for Meteorology; FCM-R18-2002; December 2002.
Weather and Environmental Content on 511 Services; 511 Deployment Coalition; June 2003.

NTCIP 1204:1998 NTCIP Object Definitions for Environmental Sensor Stations; National Electrical Manufacturers’ Association, American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials, and Institute of Transportation Engineers; 1998.
Where the Weather Meets the Road: A Research Agenda for Improving Road Weather; The National Academies; BASC-U-02-06-A; 2004.
Road Weather Information Systems (RWIS) Data Integration Guidelines; Castle Rock Consultants; October 2002.
Draft Report: Joint TMC/TOC System Integration Study for Emergency Transportation Operations and Weather: Baseline Conditions; Battelle; 2004 (in review).
Clarus Final Draft Concept of Operations; Iteris and Meridian Environmental Technology, Inc.; May 16, 2005.
Security of Federal Automated Information Resources; Appendix III to OMB Circular No. A 130; Office of Management and Budget; February 8, 1996.

overallDescription.productPerspective: The **clarus high** initiative is conceptualized as a 'network of networks,' designed to interconnect disparate environmental data collection systems, analogous to the structure of the **Internet** for computer networks. This system will link various autonomous weather networks, enabling them to share collected environmental data and receive data disseminated by **clarus high**. The primary beneficiaries of this data sharing mechanism are weather service providers, though any participating entity can opt to receive data, fostering a collaborative environment for meteorological information exchange.
overallDescription.productPerspective2: This decentralized data sharing model allows participating organizations to retain ownership of their data while leveraging **clarus high** as a centralized 'meta-librarian' service. The **clarus high** system is tasked with collecting, organizing, and qualifying environmental data by adding quality flags, subsequently publishing this processed data to service providers and other consumers. This structured approach aims to consolidate diverse data streams, enhance data quality, and make vital surface transportation environmental information readily accessible to a broad array of stakeholders, all while appearing as a unified 'one-stop portal'.
overallDescription.productPerspective3: While the system is envisioned as a unified portal, the underlying architecture may be either centralized or decentralized, providing flexibility for designers to meet the 'one-stop' appearance requirement. The current development phase of **clarus high** does not explicitly include a direct, large-scale archiving solution for historical environmental data, recognizing that data value diminishes over time for real-time applications. Archiving capabilities may be externalized or deferred to later stages, focusing initial efforts on establishing an operational and proven data exchange network.
productFunctions: Collect environmental data from a multitude of autonomous contributing networks and sensors.
Organize and qualify raw environmental data, adding quality flags to ensure data reliability.
Publish processed and quality-controlled environmental data to various service providers and participants.
Provide a 'one-stop Internet portal' for users to access surface transportation environmental observations.
Manage user rights for contributing data and retrieving specific data components.
Support the integration of new and emerging data collection technologies, such as vehicle-based sensors and remote sensing.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: This class includes federal, state, local, and private institutions that own and operate environmental observing systems. They are primary data contributors to **clarus high**, focusing on atmospheric, pavement, and hydrologic data. Their main characteristic is the need to share their collected data while potentially receiving quality feedback and other environmental data from the **clarus high** system.

These stakeholders provide the physical sensors and platforms used for environmental data collection. Their interest lies in understanding data quality and performance metrics of their equipment within the **clarus high** ecosystem. They typically do not directly interact with the system but benefit from feedback on sensor data quality.

This group comprises entities such as system owners and their contractors who directly access and utilize the raw or quality-flagged data provided by the **clarus high** system. They require tools for data retrieval and integration into their own value-added products, making immediate operational decisions based on the environmental information.

These public and private entities assimilate **clarus high** data with other meteorological information to generate value-added road and rail weather services for the transportation community. They are critical consumers, requiring timely, quality-controlled data to enhance their forecasting and decision support offerings, often serving as intermediaries to end-user customers.

This category includes organizations like the **National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)** and the **National Weather Service (NWS)**, as well as private sector weather services. They are primarily interested in the meteorological and hydrologic components of **clarus high** data to support public forecasting and provide specialized services to sectors like agriculture, power utilities, and construction.

Comprising federal, academic, and private sector researchers, this group leverages **clarus high** data to advance scientific understanding and practice in surface transportation weather. They require access to comprehensive and quality-controlled environmental datasets for modeling, analysis, and developing innovative solutions.

These interests, both operational and non-operational, seek to incorporate **clarus high** data into their larger meteorological archives or data warehouses. They require robust data transfer capabilities and clear metadata, recognizing that comprehensive archiving efforts may extend beyond the direct scope of **clarus high** but benefit from its data streams.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The **clarus high** system is designed to operate within a highly distributed and interconnected digital infrastructure, functioning as a 'network of networks' for environmental data. It will interface with a multitude of diverse data collection platforms, including Environmental Sensor Stations (**ESS**), mobile data acquisition platforms, and various camera systems. The system must support heterogeneous hardware and software environments from participating data providers, necessitating flexible and standards-based communication protocols.
overallDescription.operatingEnvironment2: The backend infrastructure for **clarus high** must be robust and scalable, capable of retrieving and disseminating large volumes of data from numerous sources at potentially high rates. This implies a server-based architecture, possibly distributed across multiple servers and communication channels, to ensure timely data processing and delivery. The chosen operating systems and development environments will significantly impact system reliability, necessitating careful selection to maximize uptime and potentially incorporating redundancies at both hardware and software levels.
overallDescription.operatingEnvironment3: Data within the **clarus high** system and its published datasets will consistently include geo-reference coordinates (decimal longitude, latitude, and elevation) and time-date stamps referenced to **Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)**. The system must accommodate variations in data formats and precision from contributing systems, including challenges related to Daylight Savings Time and time zone differences. The underlying database tools will need to be capable of efficiently managing and querying large volumes of geo-temporal data.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The **clarus high** system must prioritize timeliness of information dissemination and overall system reliability as fundamental design constraints. The architecture shall be inherently scalable, designed to retrieve and disseminate large volumes of data from various sources at high rates, potentially utilizing distributed processing across multiple servers and communication channels.

The system is mandated to be 'open,' employing an architecture and communication interfaces that are non-proprietary and broadly supported within the information technology industry. It shall strictly adhere to applicable national standards, with particular emphasis on **Intelligent Transportation System (ITS)** standards, to ensure broad interoperability and future extensibility.

Design and implementation must address the trade-off between the desire for high reliability and the cost associated with achieving it, potentially through hardware and software redundancies. The system's criticality is considered non-essential as it is not replacing existing applications or supporting national security missions, influencing the acceptable level of investment in redundancy.
userDocumentation: A comprehensive online help system will be integrated into the **clarus high** Internet portal, providing users with context-sensitive guidance on data retrieval, system functionalities, and interface navigation.

Detailed user manuals will be provided for data contributors, outlining the procedures for submitting environmental data, understanding data quality feedback, and managing their data sharing agreements within the **clarus high** system.

Technical guides and API documentation will be made available for service providers and research communities, detailing data formats, communication protocols, and methods for integrating **clarus high** data into their existing applications and models.

Disclaimer information regarding data accuracy and limitations will be prominently displayed within the portal and accompanying documentation, in line with data sharing agreements, to ensure transparency for all data consumers.
assumptionsAndDependencies: The foundational assumption is that the usefulness and justification for the **clarus high** system are critically dependent on the active participation of multiple environmental data providers and a substantial number of environmental data consumers. Limited participation would significantly diminish the system's value proposition.

It is assumed that environmental data contributors will collect, process, and publish their data in a timely manner, and that the collected data will possess adequate accuracy. Contributing systems are also assumed to provide sufficient location, time/date, and data qualification tags, as a lack thereof could significantly complicate data acquisition interfaces.

The **clarus high** system will check data and apply quality flags; however, an explicit assumption is that neither the **FHWA** nor contributing data providers will accept responsibility for the absolute accuracy of any data. Specific limitations and conditions will be governed by formal data sharing agreements and transparent disclaimers.

Geographic coordinates for data presentation will be in decimal longitude, latitude, and elevation, assuming contributing systems can provide this format. Timestamps will be referenced to **Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)**, requiring contributing systems to handle time zone and Daylight Savings Time complexities accurately.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: The **clarus high** system will provide a cohesive 'one-stop Internet portal' as its primary user interface, designed to facilitate seamless access to all surface transportation environmental observations. This portal will feature intuitive navigation, standardized interface components, and clear presentation of complex meteorological and road condition data. Graphical User Interface (**GUI**) standards for consistent layouts, standard buttons, and unified error message displays will be rigorously applied to ensure a consistent and user-friendly experience across the platform.
externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces2: The portal will offer various data retrieval tools, allowing users to query, filter, and visualize environmental data based on criteria such as geographic location, time horizon, and data type. Input forms will be provided for observation system owners to manage their data contributions and review quality feedback. Although detailed UI design specifications will be externalized, the conceptual design prioritizes ease of use for diverse user classes, from field operators to research scientists, ensuring efficient interaction with the system's extensive data resources.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The **clarus high** system will interface with a wide array of environmental data collection hardware, including fixed and mobile Environmental Sensor Stations (**ESS**), various camera systems, and emerging vehicle-based sensors. These interfaces must support standard data acquisition protocols, with a strong emphasis on compliance with the **NTCIP 1204:1998 NTCIP Object Definitions for Environmental Sensor Stations** standard to ensure interoperability. The system needs to accommodate varying hardware capabilities and data transmission methods from diverse observation platforms.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces2: The nature of data and control interactions between the software and hardware will involve real-time streaming of sensor readings, metadata updates, and potentially remote configuration feedback mechanisms. The system must be robust enough to handle intermittent connectivity and varying data transfer rates inherent in field deployments. Support for geographical positioning systems (**GPS**) will be crucial for accurate geo-referencing of mobile sensor data, with precise coordinate formats (decimal longitude, latitude, elevation) as a requirement for all incoming hardware data feeds.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: The **clarus high** system will establish critical software interfaces with numerous external data collection systems, internal database management systems, and a variety of service provider applications. It will ingest data from autonomous weather networks, requiring flexible interfaces capable of integrating diverse data formats and proprietary systems, ideally transitioning to a common protocol internally. The system's internal software components will interface with data quality control modules, data storage, and data publication services.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces2: Outbound software interfaces will enable service providers and researchers to retrieve environmental data through defined **Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)**, ensuring programmatic access to quality-controlled information. The system will interact with underlying operating systems and potentially third-party libraries for data processing, spatial indexing, and time-series analysis. Data items exchanged will include raw observations, sensor metadata, quality flags, and aggregated environmental reports, all adhering to specified message formatting standards to ensure seamless integration and data consistency across disparate software components.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: The **clarus high** system will manage extensive communication functions to facilitate its role as a 'network of networks' for environmental data. This includes robust network server communications protocols for both data ingestion from contributing organizations and data dissemination to service providers. The system will support a common protocol for data transfer, with strong consideration given to established internet standards like **HTTP** and potentially **FTP** for bulk data transfers, ensuring broad compatibility.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces2: Communication security is paramount, requiring secure data transmission channels to protect sensitive environmental data in transit. Encryption mechanisms will be implemented using industry-standard protocols such as **TLS 1.2+** to safeguard data integrity and confidentiality between **clarus high** and its interconnected partners. Requirements will also address data transfer rates and synchronization mechanisms to ensure timely and consistent data flow, accommodating the high volumes and potentially rapid updates characteristic of real-time environmental observations. This includes handling data age minimization across various operational layers.

systemFeatures.name: Data Collection and Ingestion
systemFeatures.description: This feature enables the **clarus high** system to efficiently collect diverse environmental data from a multitude of autonomous sensor networks and platforms. It offers significant user value by centralizing raw data streams from various sources, reducing the complexity for individual users to aggregate information from disparate systems. Technically, this impacts the system architecture by requiring robust and flexible data acquisition interfaces capable of handling varying data formats, transmission protocols, and intermittent connectivity from contributing partners. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: An autonomous sensor network transmits new environmental observation data to **clarus high** through its designated interface. Response: The **clarus high** system receives the data, initiates parsing, and logs the reception timestamp.
functionalRequirements: CH-REQ-001: The system shall provide standardized interfaces for ingesting atmospheric, pavement, and hydrologic data from participating collection systems.
CH-REQ-002: The system shall support data ingestion from fixed Environmental Sensor Stations (**ESS**), mobile platforms, and camera imagery sources.
CH-REQ-003: The system shall parse incoming data to extract environmental values, geo-reference coordinates (latitude, longitude, elevation), and timestamps.
CH-REQ-004: The system shall log all incoming data, including source identification and reception time, for auditing and traceability purposes.

systemFeatures.name: Data Quality Control and Processing
systemFeatures.description: This feature ensures the integrity and reliability of all environmental data ingested into the **clarus high** system by applying continuous quality control mechanisms. It provides user value by enhancing the trustworthiness of the data, allowing decision-makers to rely on the information for critical surface transportation operations. Architecturally, this necessitates dedicated processing modules for data validation, anomaly detection, and the generation of quality flags, impacting storage requirements for metadata and processed data. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Raw environmental data is successfully ingested into the **clarus high** system. Response: The system automatically initiates a series of quality control checks against the data.

Stimulus: Quality control checks identify data elements that do not meet predefined thresholds. Response: The system applies specific quality flags to the affected data elements and stores them alongside the raw data.
functionalRequirements: CH-REQ-005: The system shall perform automated quality control checks on all ingested atmospheric, pavement, and hydrologic data.
CH-REQ-006: The system shall identify and flag data elements that fall outside predefined acceptable ranges or patterns.
CH-REQ-007: The system shall associate quality flags with individual data observations, indicating the nature of any detected anomalies.
CH-REQ-008: The system shall provide feedback mechanisms to originating sensor station operators regarding the quality of their submitted data.

systemFeatures.name: Data Publication and Dissemination
systemFeatures.description: This feature facilitates the efficient and timely distribution of quality-controlled environmental data to authorized service providers and other participants within the **clarus high** network. It delivers significant user value by making critical surface transportation weather and road condition observations readily available for downstream applications and decision support systems. From a technical perspective, this impacts the system by requiring robust data export interfaces, potentially involving various data formats and communication protocols, alongside a mechanism for managing user access rights to specific data components. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Quality-controlled environmental data becomes available within the **clarus high** system. Response: The system publishes the data, making it accessible to authorized service providers and direct data users.

Stimulus: A service provider requests a specific subset of environmental data from the **clarus high** system. Response: The system validates the provider's access rights and delivers the requested data in a predefined format.
functionalRequirements: CH-REQ-009: The system shall publish quality-controlled environmental data without post-processing, suitable for incorporation into value-added products and models.
CH-REQ-010: The system shall support data transfer using one common, non-proprietary communication protocol with full metadata.
CH-REQ-011: The system shall manage user rights to input or extract specific data components based on established data sharing agreements.
CH-REQ-012: The system shall disseminate data to authorized entities, including service providers, research communities, and archives, in a timely manner.

systemFeatures.name: User Rights Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides a robust framework for controlling access to and contributions of data within the **clarus high** system, ensuring data governance and adherence to agreements. Its user value lies in protecting data ownership, maintaining confidentiality, and enabling tailored data sharing policies for various participants. Technically, this requires an authorization and authentication subsystem integrated with data access layers, impacting security architecture and database schema design for user permissions. Priority: Medium.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A system administrator defines specific data access permissions for a new user or organization. Response: The system updates the user's profile with the specified data input and extraction rights.
Stimulus: A user attempts to access or modify data for which they lack authorization. Response: The system denies the access request and logs the attempted unauthorized action.
functionalRequirements: CH-REQ-013: The system shall allow administrators to define and modify user roles and permissions for data input and extraction.
CH-REQ-014: The system shall enforce data access policies based on the user's role and organization affiliation.
CH-REQ-015: The system shall restrict dissemination of data based on provider-defined data sharing agreements.
CH-REQ-016: The system shall audit all data access and modification attempts for security monitoring.

systemFeatures.name: Internet Portal and Data Retrieval Tools
systemFeatures.description: This feature delivers a central, easy-to-use 'one-stop Internet portal' that allows diverse users to effectively discover, retrieve, and potentially visualize surface transportation environmental observations. The user value is significantly enhanced by providing a unified access point, simplifying data acquisition from the complex network of networks. Architecturally, this involves web development, search and indexing capabilities, and integration with the data publication services, requiring careful consideration for user experience and scalability. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A user navigates to the **clarus high** Internet portal. Response: The portal displays an overview of available data and provides tools for searching and filtering.

Stimulus: A user requests to download environmental data for a specific geographic area and time range. Response: The system retrieves the relevant quality-controlled data and presents it to the user in a downloadable format, respecting their access rights.
functionalRequirements: CH-REQ-017: The system shall provide a web-based Internet portal as a central access point for environmental observations.
CH-REQ-018: The system shall offer data retrieval tools that allow users to search and filter data by location, time, data type, and quality flags.
CH-REQ-019: The system shall display environmental data in user-friendly formats, potentially including maps and charts.
CH-REQ-020: The system shall support the download of queried data sets in common, machine-readable formats.

performanceRequirements: The **clarus high** system shall be capable of ingesting environmental data from hundreds of concurrent sensor stations at a rate of at least 10,000 observations per second, with a rationale to support high-volume, real-time data collection across a vast network.
The system shall process and apply quality flags to 95% of ingested raw data within 5 seconds of reception, ensuring minimal latency in data qualification for timely dissemination.

The system shall disseminate quality-controlled environmental data to authorized service providers with an average data age not exceeding 30 seconds from sensor observation to availability, to support real-time operational responses and forecasting.

The Internet portal shall load interactive data visualizations within 3 seconds for queries covering typical geographical areas and time horizons (e.g., one state, 24 hours), maintaining a responsive user experience for critical decision-making.
safetyRequirements: The **clarus high** system shall implement robust data validation routines to prevent erroneous or corrupted environmental data from impacting public safety decisions, with the rationale that inaccurate weather and road conditions could lead to hazardous transportation situations.

The system shall incorporate fault-tolerant mechanisms for critical data ingestion and processing components, ensuring continuity of service and data flow even in the event of partial system failures. This is critical for maintaining situational awareness for surface transportation entities.

The system shall ensure that quality flags are clearly and unambiguously associated with all published data, providing users with the necessary context to interpret data reliability and mitigate potential risks from unverified observations.

All system components involved in data collection, quality control, and dissemination shall undergo rigorous testing to ensure their operational integrity and minimize the risk of providing misleading information that could compromise life and property.
securityRequirements: The **clarus high** system shall implement strong authentication mechanisms for all users accessing its services, including multi-factor authentication for administrative and data contribution roles, to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive environmental data and system functionalities.

The system shall employ robust authorization controls to manage user rights for data input, extraction, and modification based on predefined roles and data sharing agreements, ensuring data integrity and adherence to governance policies.

All data transmitted between participating networks and the **clarus high** system, as well as data disseminated to consumers, shall be encrypted using industry-standard protocols (e.g., **TLS 1.2+**) to protect against eavesdropping and tampering. This is crucial for maintaining data confidentiality and integrity.

The system shall maintain comprehensive audit logs of all data transactions, including ingestion, processing, access, and dissemination events, providing a clear trail for forensic analysis and compliance auditing. Regular security audits and vulnerability assessments shall be conducted to identify and mitigate potential weaknesses.
softwareQualityAttributes: **Reliability**: The **clarus high** system shall exhibit an uptime of 99.9% for its core data ingestion, processing, and publication services, excluding scheduled maintenance, ensuring continuous availability of critical environmental data. This is essential given the system's role in supporting real-time transportation decisions.

**Scalability**: The system shall be capable of horizontally scaling to accommodate a 50% increase in data contributors and a 100% increase in data consumption over a two-year period without requiring significant re-architecture, demonstrating its adaptability to growing demands.

**Openness/Interoperability**: The system shall utilize non-proprietary, standards-based architectures and communication protocols (**ITS standards**, common web standards) for all external interfaces, maximizing compatibility with diverse contributing and consuming systems and facilitating broad adoption.

**Maintainability**: The system's codebase shall adhere to established coding standards and be modular in design, allowing for efficient identification, diagnosis, and resolution of defects, as well as straightforward implementation of future enhancements, with minimal impact on existing functionalities.

**Flexibility**: The system shall support the addition of new environmental data types (e.g., air quality, bio-hazards) and new sensor technologies (e.g., vehicle-based, remote sensing) with configuration-driven updates rather than requiring core code changes, enabling future-proofing and adaptability.

**Usability**: The **clarus high** Internet portal shall achieve a System Usability Scale (**SUS**) score of at least 75 when evaluated by target users, indicating an intuitive and efficient interface for data discovery and retrieval, thereby maximizing user adoption and satisfaction.
businessRules: Ownership of environmental data submitted to the **clarus high** system shall remain with the originating organization, with usage governed by formal data sharing agreements.
The **clarus high** program will not assume responsibility for the absolute accuracy of ingested environmental data; disclaimers will be provided to all data consumers.

Environmental data within the **clarus high** system will be presented primarily by geographic coordinates (latitude, longitude, elevation) rather than regional political boundaries, to ensure universal applicability.
Data coverage across the **clarus high** network will not be uniform and will depend entirely on the sensor placement and operational status of contributing organizations.

glossary.definition: A prefix used for all identifiers (requirements, features, use cases, diagrams) within the **clarus high** Software Requirements Specification, ensuring project-specific traceability.
Federal Highway Administration, the sponsor of the **clarus high** initiative.
Department of Transportation, state-level entities that are key users and beneficiaries of **clarus high** data.
Environmental Sensor Station, a device or network of devices collecting environmental data, often relating to road conditions and weather.
National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, a federal agency focused on weather, climate, oceans, and coasts, identified as a key stakeholder and data consumer.
National Weather Service, part of NOAA, responsible for public weather forecasting and warnings.
Coordinated Universal Time, the primary time standard by which the world regulates clocks and time, used for all **clarus high** data timestamps.
Daylight Savings Time, a temporal adjustment that **clarus high** must account for when processing timestamps from diverse sources.

Intelligent Transportation System, which encompasses advanced applications aimed at providing innovative services relating to different modes of transport and traffic management. **clarus high** adheres to ITS standards.
Application Programming Interface, a set of defined interactions between multiple software intermediaries, used for programmatic data access from **clarus high**.
Road Weather Information Systems, systems that collect, process, and disseminate road weather and pavement condition information, often providing data to **clarus high**.
Closed Circuit Television, referred to as a potential source of surface visibility information for **clarus high**.
JSON Web Token, a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties. Inferred for secure API communication within **clarus high**.
Transport Layer Security, a cryptographic protocol designed to provide communications security over a computer network. Inferred for secure data transmission within **clarus high**.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart illustrates the high-level data acquisition, processing, and publication workflow within the **clarus high** system. Nodes represent distinct steps or states in the process, using alphanumeric identifiers. Labels with spaces are enclosed in double quotes. Arrows indicate the direction of data flow and process progression.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
  CH_A["Start Process: Data Acquisition"]
  CH_B("Collect Raw Environmental Data")
  CH_C["Raw Data Staging Area"]
  CH_D{"Data Validated?"}
  CH_E["Apply Quality Flags"]
  CH_F["Store Quality-Controlled Data"]
  CH_G("Publish Data to Service Providers")
  CH_H["End Process: Data Disseminated"]

  CH_A --> CH_B
  CH_B --> CH_C
  CH_C --> CH_D
  CH_D -- "Yes" --> CH_E
  CH_D -- "No" --> CH_Error["Log Data Error & Notify Collector"]
  CH_Error --> CH_B
  CH_E --> CH_F
  CH_F --> CH_G
  CH_G --> CH_H
flowchartDiagram.caption: CH_FLOWCHART: Data Acquisition, Processing, and Publication Flow.

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram models the interaction flow for environmental data collection, quality control, and dissemination through the **clarus high** system. Participants are represented by their roles or system components. Messages show the flow of information and actions over time. Standard synchronous and asynchronous message arrows are used, demonstrating the temporal order of operations. Alias IDs for participants are not quoted.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
  participant C as Collector
  participant S as Sensor
  participant CH as clarus_high
  participant SP as Service_Provider

  C->>S: "Configure Sensor (Metadata)"
  loop Continuous Data Collection
    S->>CH: "Send Observation Data (Raw)"
    activate CH
    CH->>CH: "Perform Quality Control Checks"
    alt Data Valid
      CH-->>S: "Send Quality Feedback (OK)"
      CH->>SP: "Publish Quality-Controlled Data"
    else Data Invalid
      CH-->>S: "Send Quality Feedback (Flags)"
      CH->>SP: "Publish Quality-Controlled Data (with Flags)"
    end
    deactivate CH
  end
  SP->>CH: "Request Data Subscription"
  activate CH
  CH-->>SP: "Confirm Subscription & Start Stream"
  deactivate CH
  SP->>SP: "Integrate clarus_high Data"
sequenceDiagram.caption: CH_SEQUENCE: Environmental Data Lifecycle Interaction.

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) illustrates the core entities and their relationships within the **clarus high** data model. Entities represent key data concepts, with attributes detailing their properties. Primary Keys (PK), Foreign Keys (FK), and Unique Keys (UK) are explicitly identified. Cardinality notations (e.g., }|--|| for many-to-one, ||--o{ for one-to-many) describe the relationships between entities. Relationship labels are strictly quoted.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
  USER }|--|| ORGANIZATION : "is_employed_by"
  SENSOR }|--|| ORGANIZATION : "belongs_to"
  OBSERVATION }|--|| SENSOR : "recorded_by"
  OBSERVATION }|--|| DATA_TYPE_ELEMENT : "is_classified_as"

  ORGANIZATION {
    int organization_id PK
    string name
    string type "e.g., Collector, Service_Provider"
    string contact_email UK
  }
  USER {
    int user_id PK
    int organization_id FK
    string email UK
    string password_hash
    string role "e.g., Admin, Data_Contributor, Consumer"
  }
  SENSOR {
    int sensor_id PK
    int organization_id FK
    string location_identifier
    string sensor_type "e.g., Atmospheric, Pavement"
    string configuration_details "JSON string of config params"
    datetime installation_date
  }
  OBSERVATION {
    int observation_id PK
    int sensor_id FK
    int data_type_id FK
    datetime timestamp
    float numeric_value "optional: for numeric sensor data"
    string string_value "optional: for categorical/text data"
    string unit
    string quality_flags_json "JSON string of flags"
    float latitude
    float longitude
    float elevation
  }
  DATA_TYPE_ELEMENT {
    int data_type_id PK
    string name UK "e.g., Air_Temperature, Humidity"
    string description
    string expected_unit
  }
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: CH_ERD: Core Environmental Data Model.

revisionHistory.description: Initial Release based on GParted 0.6.0-1 documentation.
revisionHistory.author: SRA System

introduction.purpose: This document precisely defines the functional and non-functional requirements for the **gparted** application, adhering to IEEE 830-1998 standards. **gparted** functions as a graphical partition editor, leveraging the **libparted** library to manage disk partitions by creating, reorganizing, and deleting them. This specification focuses on the LiveCD variant of version 0.6.0-1, which operates from RAM at system boot without requiring installation. The primary objective is to facilitate robust disk management operations through an intuitive graphical user interface.
introduction.documentConventions: This document is structured according to the IEEE 830-1998 standard for Software Requirements Specifications. The **gparted** software described herein has already been developed, with its functions and characteristics implemented at the time of this specification's creation. Future updates and new features will necessitate revisions to maintain the document's accuracy and relevance. System Features in Section 3 are presented sequentially, mirroring the application's operational flow from boot to main window interactions.
introduction.intendedAudience: This document serves a diverse audience including casual users, software developers, quality assurance testers, and technical documentation writers. Each group can extract relevant information to fulfill their specific needs concerning the **gparted** application. Developers and testers will find detailed functional requirements and interface specifications particularly useful for understanding implementation and validation criteria. Casual users and documentation writers can leverage the high-level overviews and feature descriptions to comprehend the system's capabilities and user interactions effectively.
introduction.productScope: **gparted** is a versatile disk utility designed for comprehensive partition management, including creation, deletion, resizing, moving, checking, and copying disk partitions and their respective file systems. This functionality is critical for tasks such as preparing disks for new operating system installations, optimizing storage space, and data migration. The project encompasses two primary distributions: an installable version for **Linux** operating systems and **gparted** Live, a bootable medium provided as a .zip or .iso file for direct execution from CD/DVD or USB. The Live version enables flexible disk operations independent of the host operating system, emphasizing a user-friendly graphical interface built upon the **GNU Parted** backend.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.
Official GParted page – Home of GParted: http://gparted.sourceforge.net/index.php
Project GParted page on Sourceforge: https://sourceforge.net/projects/gparted/
Wikipedia GParted page: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GParted
Wikipedia GNU Parted page: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNU_Parted
GParted Forum: http://gparted-forum.surf4.info/index.php
How to save files and take screenshots in GParted: http://gparted.sourceforge.net/larry/tips/save_details.htm

overallDescription.productPerspective: The **gparted** application serves as a leading graphical utility for disk partition manipulation, surpassing many contemporaries in its support for diverse file systems and comprehensive feature set.
