Diagrams are generated using Mermaid syntax, conforming to specified structural and visual conventions for enhanced readability and maintainability. Terminology and abbreviations are consistently applied throughout the document for unambiguous communication.
introduction.intendedAudience: This document is primarily intended for the **warc III** development team at Hanzo Archives, project managers, and the International Internet Preservation Consortium (IIPC) member institutions. It serves as a foundational reference for developers implementing the system components and for testers validating its functionalities. Additionally, web archivists, crawl engineers, and researchers who will be utilizing the tools are part of the target audience. Stakeholders involved in the project's funding and strategic direction will find this SRS crucial for understanding the project's scope and expected deliverables.
introduction.productScope: The **warc III** project extends the core `libwarc` library and existing **WARC Tools** to introduce a full migration application, a repackaging tool, a reporting application, a quality assurance tool, and an enhanced WARC browser. Its scope includes the specification, development, testing, and deployment of these new tools and applications. The system will primarily focus on converting ARC files to the WARC format, verifying migrations, and enabling detailed analysis and comparison of web archive collections. Maintenance of the `libwarc` library and collaborative engagement with the web archiving community are also integral parts of the project scope.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.

overallDescription.productPerspective: The **warc III** project represents the third phase in a strategic initiative to standardize and streamline web archive management. Building upon the foundational `libwarc` library and command-line tools developed in Phases I and II, this iteration introduces a comprehensive migration application and enhances existing functionalities. It integrates seamlessly with previously developed components, including the **Search Tools** project for full-text and metadata search, creating a unified and robust ecosystem for WARC file manipulation. The new tools are designed to extend the capabilities of the core library, offering higher-level applications for complex archiving workflows.
productFunctions: Facilitate and promote the adoption of the WARC file format.
Provide an open-source software library (`libwarc`) for WARC file manipulation.
Offer a suite of command-line tools and web applications for managing web archives.
Implement a full migration application for converting ARC files to WARC format.
Enable validation of migrated WARC content against original ARC files.
Provide a repackaging tool to extract and create new WARC files based on specific criteria.
Develop a reporting application for summarizing and analyzing WARC file collections.
Integrate an enhanced WARC browser with reporting and search capabilities.
Deliver a quality assurance tool for comparing and identifying differences in web crawls.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: These users are technical experts responsible for collecting and processing web content. They require powerful, flexible tools, often command-line driven, to manipulate and explore large collections of web archive content. Their primary goal is efficient data handling and scripting capabilities for automated workflows. They will utilize the migration, repackaging, and QA tools for their daily operations.

Web archivists are responsible for the long-term preservation and accessibility of digital cultural heritage. They need reliable tools for format migration, content validation, and comprehensive reporting to ensure data integrity and compliance with archiving standards. They interact with both command-line tools and web-based applications for managing archive collections effectively. Their focus is on verifiable and robust migration processes.

Researchers utilize archived web content for academic study and analysis. They require tools that enable easy exploration, searching, and summarization of WARC files without needing deep technical knowledge of the underlying format. The enhanced WARC browser and reporting application will be their primary interfaces for accessing and understanding archived data.

Staff from participating institutions are key collaborators in the project. They contribute to requirements specification, provide real-world test data, and participate in acceptance testing. These users have varying technical expertise but are deeply invested in the practical application and deployment of the tools within their institutional environments. Their feedback is crucial for validating the tools' effectiveness and scalability.

This internal user group is responsible for the design, implementation, and maintenance of the **warc III** software. Developers require clear API documentation and a robust development environment, while project managers need high-level oversight of project progress, resource allocation, and adherence to established timelines. They ensure the technical execution aligns with project goals and community requirements.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The **warc III** system is designed to operate across diverse environments, primarily as a set of command-line tools and web applications. It leverages a **Java** development environment, utilizing **web services** and **RESTful APIs** for interoperability and scalability. The tools are built upon the existing `libwarc` library, ensuring compatibility and leveraging established functionalities. Deployment is anticipated on standard server hardware running various operating systems, with an emphasis on **UNIX-style** principles for modularity and efficiency.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The system shall leverage the existing `libwarc` core software library, ensuring backward compatibility and consistency across the **WARC Tools** suite. Implementation will adhere to **UNIX-style** principles, emphasizing small, atomic tools for efficient and modular processing. The architecture must support distributed processing and data transport, enabling scalability to process large collections of web archives across multiple machines.

The software components shall be designed to avoid unnecessary technology dependencies, promoting maintainability and reducing external integration complexities. Development will be compliant with **Java** development environments, utilizing established communication protocols such as **web services** and **RESTful APIs**. This approach ensures broad interoperability and ease of integration within existing institutional infrastructures.

The tools are constrained to perform operations with minimal memory usage, optimizing resource consumption during large-scale processing. Functionality for dealing with hardware failures will not be provided by the tools themselves, placing responsibility for underlying infrastructure resilience on the deployment environment. All external tool integrations, such as for file identification or malware scanning, must be flexible and configurable, avoiding partner-specific hardcoded solutions.
userDocumentation: The **warc III** system will be delivered with comprehensive technical documentation, including API references for the `libwarc` library and tool integrations. User manuals will be provided for the web-based applications and graphical user interfaces, detailing configuration, operation, and troubleshooting. Command-line tools will include detailed usage guides and examples, complemented by easy-to-adapt shell script wrappers to facilitate automation. Tutorials and online help resources will be developed to assist users in understanding advanced features and best practices for web archive management.
assumptionsAndDependencies: It is assumed that the `libwarc` library, developed in **WARC Tools** Phases I and II, provides a stable and performant foundation for the **warc III** project. The project assumes continued collaborative engagement from IIPC member institutions for requirements refinement, provision of test data (e.g., ARC files), and participation in acceptance testing. The availability of external tools and services, such as persistent identifier generators (e.g., Noid, ARK, UUID), file identification services (e.g., DROID, FILE, JHOVE), and anti-malware scanners (e.g., ClamAV), is assumed for integration into the migration and validation workflows.

The project depends on a robust underlying network infrastructure to support distributed processing and data transport across multiple machines. It is also assumed that users will have the necessary technical proficiency to configure and operate command-line tools and interpret logging output. Any specific institutional requirements that extend beyond the project's baseline scope are assumed to be accompanied by additional financial contributions or mutually agreed-upon de-scoping to ensure project viability.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: The **warc III** system will offer several user interfaces tailored to different operational needs. A web-based user interface will be provided for configuring migration settings, monitoring active migration processes, and displaying comprehensive reports. This **Web UI** shall adhere to modern accessibility standards and provide intuitive navigation for complex workflows. It will include standard UI components such as forms, dashboards, and interactive graphs to visualize data and process status. Additionally, a console application will be available for advanced users to manage active migrations, view real-time logs, and restart processes directly.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The **warc III** software interfaces with standard computing hardware, including servers for hosting the **Migration Application** and **Reporting Application**. The system is designed to leverage multicore processors and high-speed I/O devices for efficient processing of large archive collections. While the software does not provide explicit functionality for dealing with hardware failures, it is optimized for concurrent execution on multiple machines to maximize throughput. Network interface cards are critical for distributed processing and data transport between system components.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: The **warc III** system interacts with several internal and external software components. Internally, it relies heavily on the `libwarc` library for core WARC file manipulation. It also integrates with the existing **Search Tools** project for full-text and metadata indexing and searching. Externally, the system is designed to interface with various third-party tools such as **JHOVE**, **DROID** for file format identification, and **ClamAV** for malware scanning. It will also connect to persistent data stores, likely **relational databases**, for configuration persistence, migration progress logging, and deduplication records. The system provides **APIs** for integration with dynamic language bindings and **Java** packages.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: Communication within the **warc III** system and with external components will primarily occur over network protocols. **RESTful APIs** and **web services** will be utilized for interactions between the **Web UI**, **Migration Application**, and other backend services. A simple messaging infrastructure will facilitate distributed processing across multiple machines for tasks like ARC to WARC migration. Standard network protocols such as **HTTP/S** will secure web-based communications. Data transfer rates and synchronization mechanisms will be optimized to handle large volumes of archive data efficiently. All communications involving sensitive metadata or archive content will incorporate appropriate security and encryption measures.

systemFeatures.name: Migration Application
systemFeatures.description: The Migration Application provides a robust workflow for converting ARC files to the WARC format, ensuring verifiability through content and metadata validation. This feature offers significant user value by automating a complex and error-prone process, allowing archivists to efficiently transition vast collections to a standardized preservation format. Technically, it introduces a configurable workflow system capable of managing large-scale operations across distributed environments. This application is foundational for maintaining the integrity and accessibility of web archives. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User accesses the Migration Application Web UI. Response: The system displays available migration configurations and options.

Stimulus: User defines migration parameters via the Web UI (e.g., source ARC files, output path, metadata). Response: The system persists the configuration to a database or file and confirms its validity.
Stimulus: User initiates a migration job with a selected configuration. Response: The system triggers the `arc_warc_migrate` tool and provides real-time progress updates through a console or Web UI.
Stimulus: Migration tool encounters a bad record during conversion. Response: The system logs the problem, skips the record if configured, and continues processing other records.
Stimulus: User requests a 'dry-run' migration. Response: The system simulates the conversion process, generates performance reports and identifies issues, but does not write actual WARC data.
functionalRequirements: WI-REQ-001: The system shall provide a clear Application Programming Interface (API) to handle the migration process and default configuration.
WI-REQ-002: The system shall ensure the migration API requires the inclusion of only one header file for simplified integration.
WI-REQ-003: The system shall generate errors and require operator intervention if a migration configuration is not explicitly provided.
WI-REQ-004: The system shall allow users to provide custom metadata (e.g., institution, context) to be stored in the converted WARC files.
WI-REQ-005: The system shall automatically generate and store migration-specific metadata (e.g., OS type, original ARC name, conversion timestamp) in the converted WARC files.
WI-REQ-006: The system shall be able to call external tools and services (e.g., database queries, shell commands, web services) during the migration workflow.
WI-REQ-007: The system shall use persistent, opaque, unique, and global identifiers (e.g., Noid, ARK, UUID) for records access during migration.
WI-REQ-008: The system shall integrate a default external tool (e.g., ClamAV) to scan files for malware before conversion.
WI-REQ-009: The system API shall be flexible enough to allow external tools such as JHOVE and DROID to be used for file format identification.
WI-REQ-010: The system shall support running the ARC to WARC migration across multiple machines, facilitated by a simple messaging infrastructure.
WI-REQ-011: The system shall permit logging during the migration process to be turned on or off at any time.
WI-REQ-012: The system shall incorporate software checkpoints (e.g., start-trans, end-trans) for managing atomic transactions during processing, where operating system limitations allow.
WI-REQ-013: The system shall allow users to perform a 'dry-run migration' to generate reports on performance, bad ARCs, and time estimation without writing actual WARC data.
WI-REQ-014: The system shall detect and report WARC records with identical checksums using a centralized database for deduplication.
WI-REQ-015: The system shall allow migration configurations to be built and managed through a Web User Interface.

systemFeatures.name: Validation Tool
systemFeatures.description: The Validation Tool ensures the accuracy and integrity of the ARC to WARC migration process by comparing content and metadata between original and converted files. This provides significant user value by building confidence in the migration outcome and verifying data fidelity for archival purposes. Technically, it employs checksum-based comparisons and metadata matching, capable of handling large collections by offering random sampling options. The tool serves as a critical quality gate for the entire migration workflow. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User invokes `arc_warc_verify` with ARC and WARC file paths. Response: The system extracts content from both file types and computes checksums for comparison.
Stimulus: The validation process identifies a mismatch in payload checksums. Response: The system flags the discrepancy and reports it as a validation failure.
Stimulus: User enables random sampling for validation. Response: The system selects a statistically representative percentage of records, proportionally by MIME type, for verification.
Stimulus: User specifies the use of Heritrix's `arcreader` for validation. Response: The system integrates the external `arcreader` to cross-check the conversion of original ARC records.
functionalRequirements: WI-REQ-016: The system shall use metadata included in newly generated WARC files (from FR-4 and FR-5) to match records with their corresponding ARC files for validation.
WI-REQ-017: The system's core validation process shall utilize payload checksum comparisons to verify content integrity.
WI-REQ-018: The system shall provide sampling functionality to quickly validate conversions, including proportional sampling of record types based on MIME-Types.
WI-REQ-019: The system shall integrate Heritrix’s `arcreader` to double-check the correct conversion of original ARC records to WARC.
WI-REQ-020: The system shall be capable of performing a round-trip validation by migrating newly created WARC files back to ARC and comparing checksums.

systemFeatures.name: Repackaging Tool
systemFeatures.description: The Repackaging Tool enables users to extract and curate specific WARC records from a collection, generating new, focused WARC files. This feature offers substantial user value by facilitating testing, quality assurance, initial crawl planning, and selective data transfer to third parties. Technically, it applies advanced filtering mechanisms based on various WARC metadata fields and record attributes, supporting pre and post-operation hooks. This tool enhances the flexibility and reusability of archived content. Priority: Medium.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User invokes `warc_repackage` with input WARC files and a URL pattern filter. Response: The system extracts records matching the URL pattern and writes them to a new WARC file.
Stimulus: User specifies a MIME-Type filter during repackaging. Response: The system processes only records whose MIME-Type matches the provided criteria.
Stimulus: User provides an output WARC pattern and size limit. Response: The system creates new WARC files, adhering to the specified size constraints and naming convention.
Stimulus: User specifies custom metadata for the repackaged WARC file. Response: The system embeds the user-defined metadata, describing the extraction context, into the newly created WARC file.
functionalRequirements: WI-REQ-021: The system shall allow repackaging of WARC files by filtering records based on URL using regular expressions.
WI-REQ-022: The system shall enable repackaging of WARC files by filtering records based on their MIME-Types.
WI-REQ-023: The system shall support repackaging of WARC files by filtering records based on their size.
WI-REQ-024: The system shall facilitate repackaging of WARC files by filtering records based on timestamp intervals.
WI-REQ-025: The system shall allow filtering of records for repackaging based on any field defined in the WARC specification.
WI-REQ-026: The system shall provide pre and post-record, file, and job operations during repackaging.
WI-REQ-027: The system shall ensure pre-operations can prevent a file or record from being processed by returning a specific value.
WI-REQ-028: Each repackaged WARC file shall include a user-defined metadata record describing the extraction context.

systemFeatures.name: Reporting Application
systemFeatures.description: The Reporting Application provides tools for generating comprehensive summaries and reports from WARC file collections, integrated with a web-based user interface. This feature delivers significant user value by offering insights into archive content, facilitating data analysis, and enabling quick status checks without requiring manual data extraction. Technically, it creates various report types (e.g., summary, mimetypes, hostnames, status codes) and supports export into different formats. This application enhances the discoverability and analytical capabilities of web archives. Priority: Medium.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User invokes `warc_summary` on a collection of WARC files. Response: The system generates a default crawl-like summary report, showing document counts and date ranges.
Stimulus: User requests a MIME-Type report with a breakdown by host. Response: The system processes the WARC files and outputs a frequency table of MIME types, grouped by host.
Stimulus: User requests to export a summary report. Response: The system provides the report in specified formats, such as **XML** or **CSV**.
Stimulus: User accesses the WARC Browser UI. Response: The system displays aggregated WARC summaries and a manifest of WARC files with links to detailed reports.
functionalRequirements: WI-REQ-029: The system shall provide tools within the migration framework to build reports from WARC files.
WI-REQ-030: The system shall allow for the export of summaries in various formats, including **XML** and **CSV**.
WI-REQ-031: The system shall enhance the WARC browser to display aggregated WARC summaries within its user interface.
WI-REQ-032: The system shall enhance the WARC browser to display a manifest of WARC files and their storage locations.

systemFeatures.name: Quality Assurance Tool
systemFeatures.description: The Quality Assurance Tool, including the WARC Comparator and Enhanced WARC Browser, provides functionalities for comparing multiple web crawls and improving the browsing experience. This offers crucial user value by enabling archivists to identify important differences (deltas) between repeated crawls, thereby ensuring the quality and consistency of their archival efforts. Technically, it integrates filtering capabilities with graph generation and server-side rewriting for dynamic content. This tool is vital for maintaining high standards in web archiving and user interaction. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User invokes `warc_compare` with two sets of WARC files. Response: The system performs a 'diff' operation based on criteria like timestamp or hostname, and generates a comparison report.
Stimulus: User requests a graphical representation of crawl differences. Response: The system generates a graph visualizing the deltas between the WARC collections.
Stimulus: User accesses the Enhanced WARC Browser for a specific archived page. Response: The system performs server-side rewriting to ensure a high-quality browsing experience.
Stimulus: User initiates a full-text search within the WARC Browser. Response: The system integrates with the **Search Tools** project to provide indexed search results for WARC content.
functionalRequirements: WI-REQ-033: The system shall provide a 'diff' tool to compare WARC sets based on defined criteria such as timestamp and hostname.
WI-REQ-034: The system shall provide a tool to generate difference graphs between WARC collections.
WI-REQ-035: The system shall provide a mechanism to view crawl deltas for quality assurance purposes.
WI-REQ-036: The system shall implement server-side rewriting functionality within the WARC browser.
WI-REQ-037: The system shall integrate a proxy mode within the WARC browser.
WI-REQ-038: The system shall integrate the full-text search module (from **Search Tools**) with the WARC browser to provide WARC indexing and searching capabilities.

systemFeatures.name: General Tool Behaviors
systemFeatures.description: This feature defines the foundational operational characteristics inherited by all **warc III** tools, ensuring consistency and adherence to core project philosophies. It provides user value through predictable and scalable processing capabilities, making the entire suite of tools robust and efficient for handling large data volumes. Technically, these behaviors ensure distributed processing, optimal performance, flexible input handling, and robust logging across all modules. This common baseline significantly contributes to the overall usability and reliability of the system. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User provides a wildcard name match for selecting WARC files as input. Response: The system processes all files matching the pattern concurrently.
Stimulus: A tool processes a large collection in a distributed environment. Response: The system scales processing effectively, maintaining best possible performance (I/O bound).
Stimulus: A command-line tool executes a major command. Response: The system provides logging facilities to follow command progression, including duration and output levels.
Stimulus: User requires access to a specific collection subset. Response: The system operates in a mode that restricts its effects to the selected subset, using criteria like name or random sampling.
functionalRequirements: WI-REQ-039: The tools shall be able to process a set of multiple WARC files concurrently, allowing selection by explicit naming, wildcard name matching, size limitation, or number of items.
WI-REQ-040: The tools shall scale to process large collections efficiently using distributed processing and optimized data transport mechanisms.
WI-REQ-041: The tools shall offer best possible performance for correctly processing large, I/O-bound collections.
WI-REQ-042: The tools shall be capable of running on multiple machines.
WI-REQ-043: The implementation of the tools shall avoid unnecessary technology dependencies.
WI-REQ-044: The implementation of the tools shall not include the development of partner-specific integration technologies.
WI-REQ-045: The tools shall be compliant with **Java** development environments, utilizing **web services** and **RESTful APIs**.
WI-REQ-046: The tools shall provide logging facilities to monitor command progression, process duration, and output levels.
WI-REQ-047: The tools shall provide enhanced usability through easy-to-adapt shell script wrappers for major commands.
WI-REQ-048: The tools shall be capable of operating in modes that restrict their effects to selected collection subsets, such as by name or random sampling.

performanceRequirements: WI-REQ-NFR-001: The system shall be capable of processing large collections of WARC files (e.g., 2 billion URLs as per Norwegian NL's plans) using distributed processing within a reasonable timeframe. This is critical for meeting the scale requirements of IIPC member institutions. The core migration and validation processes shall be I/O bound to optimize throughput, ensuring that disk access rather than CPU is the primary bottleneck. The system must maintain minimal memory usage during all operations to handle vast datasets without resource exhaustion. Response times for generating standard reports on multi-terabyte collections shall not exceed 10 minutes.

WI-REQ-NFR-002: The system shall provide best possible performance for all tools when correctly processing large collections, particularly for I/O-bound operations. This is vital to ensure efficient and timely completion of archive processing tasks. The latency for accessing and processing individual WARC records during migration should be minimized to achieve high overall conversion rates. The system shall support concurrent execution of multiple client processes, enabling parallel `arc2warc` conversion strategies for faster completion.
safetyRequirements: WI-REQ-NFR-003: The system shall ensure data integrity throughout the ARC to WARC migration process by performing checksum-based comparisons during validation (WI-REQ-017). This is paramount to prevent data corruption or loss, upholding the trustworthiness of the converted archives. Any discrepancies detected during validation must be reported clearly, preventing the acceptance of invalid WARC files into the archive. The system shall implement atomic transactions for critical file operations (WI-REQ-012), ensuring that either all changes are applied, or none are, to avoid partial and corrupted states.

WI-REQ-NFR-004: The system shall provide robust error logging facilities (WI-REQ-046) to record all operational issues, including invalid configurations, skipped records, and external tool failures. This is necessary for auditing, debugging, and identifying potential safety hazards in data handling. The ability to perform 'dry-run' migrations (WI-REQ-013) further enhances safety by allowing pre-testing of configurations without risking actual data modifications.
securityRequirements: WI-REQ-NFR-005: The system shall ensure that all user interfaces, particularly the Web UI for migration configuration and monitoring, implement robust authentication and authorization mechanisms. This is essential to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive archive management functions and to protect the integrity of the migration workflow. Access controls shall differentiate between user roles, ensuring only authorized personnel can initiate, pause, or modify migration jobs.

WI-REQ-NFR-006: The system shall support secure communication protocols (e.g., HTTPS) for all web-based interactions and data transfers. This is necessary to protect metadata and archive content from eavesdropping and tampering during transmission across networks. Integration with external security scanning tools (WI-REQ-008) further strengthens the security posture by identifying potential threats within archive content before conversion.
softwareQualityAttributes: WI-REQ-NFR-007: **Usability**: The tools shall provide enhanced usability through easy-to-adapt shell script wrappers for major commands (WI-REQ-047) and an intuitive Web User Interface for configuration (WI-REQ-015) and monitoring (WI-REQ-NFR-010). This improves the user experience for crawl engineers and archivists by simplifying complex operations and reducing the learning curve. Clear error messages and comprehensive logging also contribute to improved diagnosis and user guidance.

WI-REQ-NFR-008: **Maintainability**: The implementation shall avoid unnecessary technology dependencies (WI-REQ-043) and adhere to a modular, **UNIX-style** architecture with atomic tools. This design choice facilitates easier understanding, modification, and debugging of the codebase, reducing long-term maintenance costs. The provision of APIs (WI-REQ-001) also promotes cleaner extensions and integrations.

WI-REQ-NFR-009: **Interoperability**: The tools shall be compliant with **Java** development environments, utilizing **web services** and **RESTful APIs** (WI-REQ-045). This ensures seamless integration with other systems and applications commonly found in archival institutions. The flexibility to integrate external file identification services (WI-REQ-009) and other tools further enhances its interoperability with diverse existing infrastructures.

WI-REQ-NFR-010: **Reliability**: The migration workflow system shall have a configurable management strategy (WI-REQ-NFR-011) that supports monitoring, pausing, and restarting active migration processes (WI-REQ-NFR-010). This provides resilience against transient failures or planned interruptions, ensuring that long-running tasks can be reliably completed. Logging facilities (WI-REQ-046) are crucial for diagnosing and recovering from unexpected issues.

WI-REQ-NFR-011: **Testability**: The system's modular design and comprehensive logging facilities (WI-REQ-046) enhance its testability. The capability for 'dry-run' migrations (WI-REQ-013) allows for pre-deployment testing of configurations and performance. The active involvement of IIPC member institutions in acceptance testing (WI-ASS-001) provides real-world validation of the tools' functionality and performance in diverse environments.
businessRules: WI-REQ-BR-001: The tools shall be capable of processing multiple WARC files simultaneously, allowing selection by explicit naming, wildcard name matching, size limitation, or number of items (WI-REQ-039). This rule defines the fundamental input handling capability.
WI-REQ-BR-002: The migration workflow system shall feature a configurable management strategy for ARC to WARC migration (WI-REQ-NFR-011). This dictates the flexibility in defining migration policies.
WI-REQ-BR-003: Migration configurations must be explicitly provided (WI-REQ-003) and can be built using a Web User Interface (WI-REQ-015). This ensures control and ease of setup for migration jobs.
WI-REQ-BR-004: IIPC members should provide the default metadata they wish to be included for migration. This rule specifies a collaborative requirement for content enrichment.
WI-REQ-BR-005: Deduplication, based on checksums, may be run as a pre-migration or in-batch process (WI-REQ-NFR-014). This outlines a key operational strategy for managing archive content volume.
WI-REQ-BR-006: The repackaging and summary modules shall be able to utilize the same filtering criteria (WI-REQ-NFR-016), ensuring consistency across reporting and extraction functionalities.

otherRequirements: WI-REQ-OTH-001: The system shall be implemented as free and open-source software, making it publicly available for manipulation and management of web archive files.
WI-REQ-OTH-002: The system shall provide dynamic language bindings and **Java** packages for `libwarc`, enabling programmability in various environments.
WI-REQ-OTH-003: The system shall include extensions to existing tools and simple web applications for accessing WARC content.

glossary.definition: A file format traditionally used for storing web content by early web crawlers, such as Heritrix, superseded by the WARC format.
Web ARChive file format, a standard (ISO 28500:2017) for storing web content in a structured manner, including requests, responses, and metadata.
International Internet Preservation Consortium, an international organization dedicated to the collection, preservation, and accessibility of online content.
A core open-source software library developed in previous WARC Tools phases, providing low-level functionalities for WARC file manipulation.
Data that provides information about other data, such as creation date, author, or content type, crucial for discovery and management of archive records.
A small-sized datum computed from a block of digital data for the purpose of detecting errors that may have been introduced during its transmission or storage.
An architectural style for an application program interface (API) that uses HTTP requests to access and use data. It is a flexible, lightweight standard, widely used for web services.
A simulation of a process, performed without actually executing destructive or irreversible actions, used for testing and validation.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Flowchart diagram illustrates the high-level process flow for the ARC to WARC migration within the **warc III** system. Nodes represent distinct steps or states in the process, with diamonds indicating decision points. Arrows show the flow of control. Rectangular nodes denote processes, while rounded rectangles signify start and end points. Double-quoted labels are used for nodes containing spaces or special characters.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
    A["Start Migration Process"] --> B{"Select ARC Files (Wildcard/Size)"}
    B --> C["Configure Migration Settings (Web UI/File)"]
    C --> D{"Configuration Valid?"}
    D -- No --> E["Display Configuration Error"]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F["Execute Migration Tool (arc_warc_migrate)"]
    F --> G{"Migration Complete?"}
    G -- No --> H["Log Errors / Pause Migration"]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I["Perform Validation (arc_warc_verify)"]
    I --> J{"Validation Successful?"}
    J -- No --> K["Report Validation Failures"]
    J -- Yes --> L["Generate Summary Reports"]
    L --> M["Update WARC Browser"]
    M --> N["End Migration Process"]
flowchartDiagram.caption: High-level ARC to WARC Migration Flow.

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Sequence Diagram depicts the interaction between a user and the **warc III** Migration Application's web interface for configuring and monitoring a migration job. Participants are represented by vertical lifelines, with messages illustrating the temporal order of communications. Arrows with plus and minus signs (`->>+`, `-->>-`) implicitly manage activation periods, showing when a participant is active. `alt` and `loop` blocks denote conditional and repetitive interactions.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as "IIPC User"
    participant UI as "Web UI"
    participant MA as "Migration App"
    participant MT as "Migration Tool"
    participant DB as "Config DB"

    User->>UI: Access Migration Configuration
    UI->>+MA: Request new configuration
    MA->>DB: Query existing configurations
    DB-->>MA: Return configurations
    MA-->>-UI: Display configuration options
    User->>UI: Define migration parameters
    UI->>+MA: Save configuration
    MA->>DB: Store new configuration
    DB-->>-MA: Confirmation
    MA->>UI: Configuration saved
    User->>UI: Start Migration Job
    UI->>+MA: Initiate migration (with config ID)
    MA->>MT: Trigger arc_warc_migrate
    loop Migration Progress
        MT->>MA: Send progress updates / logs
        MA->>DB: Persist progress data
        DB-->>MA: Acknowledge
        MA-->>UI: Update dashboard
        User->>UI: Monitor progress
    end
    alt Migration Completion
        MT->>MA: Migration finished (success/fail)
        MA->>DB: Log final status
        DB-->>MA: Acknowledge
        MA-->>-UI: Display completion report
    else Migration Paused
        User->>UI: Pause migration
        UI->>+MA: Request pause
        MA->>MT: Send pause command
        MT-->>-MA: Acknowledge pause
        MA-->>UI: Update status to 'Paused'
    end
sequenceDiagram.caption: Migration Application Configuration and Monitoring.

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) illustrates the key data entities within the **warc III** system and their relationships. Entities represent distinct sets of information, with attributes describing their properties. Relationship lines indicate how entities are connected, with cardinality (e.g., one-to-many, zero-to-many) expressed by standard symbols. `PK` denotes a Primary Key, `FK` a Foreign Key, and `UK` a Unique Key. Entity names with spaces are double-quoted, while attribute names are alphanumeric.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
    "ARC File" ||--o{ "WARC File" : "migrates_to"
    "Migration Configuration" ||--o{ "ARC File" : "uses_for"
    "Migration Configuration" ||--o{ "WARC File" : "generates_from"
    "WARC File" ||--|{ "Record" : "contains"
    "Report" ||--o{ "WARC File" : "summarizes"

    "ARC File" {
        int id PK
        string path
        string original_name
        int size
        string digest
    }

    "WARC File" {
        int id PK
        int migration_config_id FK
        string path
        string warc_id UK
        string context_metadata
        datetime created_at
    }

    "Migration Configuration" {
        int id PK
        string config_name
        string settings
        string status
        datetime created_at
    }

    "Record" {
        int id PK
        int warc_file_id FK
        string record_id UK
        string url
        string mime_type
        datetime timestamp
        string checksum
        string payload_digest
    }

    "Report" {
        int id PK
        string report_type
        string content_summary
        datetime generated_at
    }
