safetyRequirements: W-REQ-024: The **watcom** tools shall exhibit robust error handling mechanisms, preventing crashes or data corruption when processing malformed or corrupted input object files. This is important to ensure the stability of the development environment and protect user data. Specific error codes and messages shall be generated for diagnostics without terminating unexpectedly.
securityRequirements: W-REQ-025: The **watcom** compiler and linker shall ensure that generated executables and shared objects do not introduce exploitable vulnerabilities due to incorrect **ELF** file formatting or improper handling of relocation entries, which could lead to privilege escalation or arbitrary code execution. This is critical for producing secure software artifacts. Strict adherence to the **Linux** **ABI** specifications will mitigate known risks related to executable formats.
softwareQualityAttributes: W-REQ-026: Reliability: The **watcom** compiler and linker shall consistently produce correct and functionally equivalent output for identical input source code and options across multiple runs. This ensures deterministic builds and trust in the generated binaries.

W-REQ-027: Maintainability: The modifications to the **Open Watcom** codebase for **Linux** porting shall follow established coding standards, with clear comments and modular design, to facilitate future enhancements and bug fixes. This reduces the long-term cost of ownership.

W-REQ-028: Portability: While specifically targeting **Linux** on **Intel386™ Architecture**, the architectural changes shall be designed to minimize dependencies on highly specific **Linux** kernel versions, allowing for broader compatibility with different **Linux** distributions. This ensures wider applicability.

W-REQ-029: Testability: The new functionalities for PIC generation and shared object building shall be amenable to automated testing, including unit tests for core modules and integration tests for the full compilation/linking process. This supports continuous quality assurance.

W-REQ-030: Compatibility: The generated **ELF** executables and shared objects shall be fully compatible with the **Linux** dynamic linker and loader on target systems, adhering strictly to the **SYSTEM V APPLICATION BINARY INTERFACE**.
businessRules: W-REQ-031: All enhancements and modifications made during the **watcom** Linux porting effort shall remain consistent with the open-source licensing model and contribution guidelines of the **Open Watcom** project. This ensures legal compliance and community engagement.

W-REQ-032: The development team shall prioritize features and bug fixes that directly contribute to achieving full **Linux** **ABI** compliance for the compiler and linker. This aligns efforts with the core project goal.

otherRequirements: W-REQ-033: All existing **Open Watcom** unit tests and regression tests shall be adapted and successfully pass on the **Linux** platform for the unchanged functionalities of the compiler and linker. This ensures no regressions are introduced by the porting effort.

glossary.definition: Application Binary Interface, a set of conventions for how executable code interacts with the operating system.
Executable and Linking Format, a common standard file format for executables, object code, shared libraries, and core dumps on **Linux** and other Unix-like systems.
Object Module Format, a file format for object code, primarily associated with Microsoft development tools of earlier eras, used natively by existing **watcom** components.
Object Reading Library, an internal **Open Watcom** component designed for reading various object file formats.
Position Dependent Code, code that assumes a fixed load address and must be re-linked if loaded elsewhere.
Position Independent Code, code that can execute correctly regardless of its absolute address in memory, crucial for shared libraries.
The **Open Watcom C Compiler** for the **Intel386™ Architecture Processor**.
The **Open Watcom Linker**, responsible for combining object files and libraries into executables or shared objects.
Code Generator for 32-bit x86 CPUs, an internal **Open Watcom** component.
Synthetically selected part of the **Open Watcom Linker**, performing basic linking tasks like relocations.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart visualizes the high-level compilation and linking process within the **watcom** environment. Nodes represent distinct activities or decisions, while arrows indicate the flow of control. `TD` denotes a top-down direction. IDs are alphanumeric, and labels with spaces are double-quoted for strict compliance.
flowchartDiagram.caption: watcom Compilation and Linking Process Flow

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram illustrates the temporal interactions between key **watcom** components during a typical compilation and linking cycle. Participants represent software modules, and messages show the flow of information and control. Standard `->>` and `-->>` arrows are used to denote synchronous and asynchronous calls, respectively, without explicit `activate`/`deactivate` blocks.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant WCC386 as "wcc386 (Compiler)"
    participant GC386 as "GC386 (Code Gen)"
    participant WLink as "wlink (Linker)"
    participant ORL as "Object Reading Library"
    participant WLCore as "WLCore (Linking Core)"
    participant LoadELF as "Load ELF (Writer)"

    User->>WCC386: "Invoke Compiler w/ Source"
    WCC386->>GC386: "Generate Intermediate Code"
    GC386-->>WCC386: "Return Object Code"
    WCC386->>User: "Output Object File"

    User->>WLink: "Invoke Linker w/ Objects"
    WLink->>ORL: "Read Object File (ELF/OMF)"
    ORL-->>WLink: "Return Mapped ORL Data"
    WLink->>WLCore: "Perform Linking Operations"
    WLCore-->>WLink: "Return Linked Data"
    WLink->>LoadELF: "Write ELF Output File"
    LoadELF-->>WLink: "Confirm File Write"
    WLink->>User: "Output Executable/Shared Object";
sequenceDiagram.caption: watcom Compiler and Linker Interaction

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) models the structural relationships between the primary data entities and components within the **watcom** project, especially concerning object file processing and code generation. Entities are defined with their key attributes, and relationships indicate how entities interact, including cardinality (`||--o{` for one-to-many, `}|--||` for many-to-one). Only `PK`, `FK`, and `UK` are used for key identification.
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: watcom Core Data Entities and Relationships

tbdList: W-REQ-020: Full support for R_386_PC32 relocations in **wlink** (details to be determined through further investigation).
W-REQ-021: Complete handling of STT_NOTYPE symbols (behavior and implications need to be fully specified).
W-REQ-022: Final precise definitions for accurate segment mapping in **LoadELF** (specific offsets and sizes require meticulous calculation).

introduction.purpose: The purpose of this requirements specification is to provide a repository for the high-level and detailed requirements governing the design of the **clarus low** system. These requirements capture the expression of general needs articulated in the **clarus low** Concept of Operations (ConOps), stakeholder meetings, and subsequent foundational documents. This document establishes the foundational criteria for the design verification and validation of the entire system.
introduction.documentConventions: This document adheres to the structure and content guidelines stipulated by the IEEE 830-1998 Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications. All requirements and components are assigned unique identifiers, utilizing the prefix CL-, to ensure comprehensive traceability throughout the system's development lifecycle. Specific formatting conventions for text, such as selective bolding for system components and platforms, are employed to enhance readability and clarity.
introduction.intendedAudience: The intended audience for this document includes project decision-makers, sponsoring stakeholders, system designers, development teams, and quality assurance testers. It serves as a definitive reference for understanding the functional and non-functional requirements of the **clarus low** system. Furthermore, it aids in aligning technical implementation efforts with the strategic objectives and operational needs of the initiative.
introduction.productScope: The **clarus low** initiative is sponsored by the U.S. Department of Transportation (U.S. DOT) with the overarching goal of consolidating and enhancing surface transportation weather and road condition observation capabilities. This system will serve as a North American resource, collecting, quality-checking, and disseminating environmental data to improve safety and mobility. The scope encompasses the development of a network for sharing surface environmental data and the creation of tools that leverage this data effectively. It focuses on integrating diverse data sources to provide a unified data management solution.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.

overallDescription.productPerspective: The **clarus low** Initiative establishes a 'network of networks' for surface transportation environmental data, much akin to the **Internet**. This system interconnects autonomous environmental data collection networks, including weather, pavement, and water level conditions, enabling participants to share and receive data. **clarus low** functions as a meta-librarian, collecting, organizing, and quality-checking environmental data before publishing it to service providers and other participants. This design aims to present a unified 'one-stop Internet portal' for all surface transportation environmental observations, irrespective of the underlying centralized or decentralized architectural choices. Data ownership remains with the original provider, with dissemination managed through specific agreements.
productFunctions: Data Collection: Ingest real-time environmental data from diverse, autonomous sensor stations and networks.
Data Organization: Structure and categorize collected observations and associated metadata for efficient management.
Quality Checking: Implement automated and continuous quality assurance processes on all incoming data streams.
Data Publication: Disseminate validated and quality-checked environmental data to authorized service providers and network members.
Configuration Management: Provide administrative tools for managing system parameters, data sources, and user access rights.
System Monitoring: Continuously monitor the health and performance of all system components to ensure operational stability.
Scheduled Operations: Automate the execution of recurring tasks, such as data acquisition and processing cycles.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: This user class includes federal, state, local, and private institutions that own and operate environmental sensor stations. Their primary characteristic is the need to integrate their existing data collection infrastructure with **clarus low** and to receive feedback on the quality and performance of their data contributions. They require secure interfaces for data submission and tools to monitor their data’s journey through the system. Technical expertise within this group can vary, necessitating user-friendly integration and reporting mechanisms.

Comprising system owners, their contractors, and other entities directly accessing **clarus low** data, this class requires timely, accurate, and quality-checked environmental observations. Their use cases range from supporting real-time operational responses to weather events to long-term research and modeling. Users in this class benefit from flexible data retrieval tools and comprehensive metadata, supporting diverse analytical needs. A spectrum of technical proficiency is expected, from basic data consumers to advanced data scientists.

This category encompasses organizations such as the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) and other general weather service providers. They utilize **clarus low** data to augment and enhance their existing weather forecasting models, particularly for surface transportation and atmospheric boundary layer predictions. These users demand high data reliability, consistency, and timely availability to improve the accuracy and utility of their meteorological services. Their technical needs focus on robust programmatic interfaces and data feed integrations.

System administrators are responsible for the overall configuration, management, and operational oversight of the **clarus low** system. This includes managing data sources, user access controls, quality checking rules, and system health. They possess advanced technical expertise in system administration, database management, and network operations. Their critical needs involve comprehensive administrative interfaces, detailed monitoring dashboards, and the ability to define and adjust system parameters securely and efficiently.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The **clarus low** system is designed to operate within a distributed and heterogeneous computing environment, serving as an intermediary between numerous independent data collection networks and various data consumers. It must seamlessly integrate with a multitude of hardware platforms, including diverse environmental sensor stations (ESS) and remote sensing devices. The backend infrastructure will likely reside on enterprise-grade server hardware, running industry-standard operating systems such as **Linux** or **Windows Server**, supporting robust database management systems like **PostgreSQL** or **Oracle** for data storage and retrieval.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The **clarus low** system must adhere to specific industry standards, including **NTCIP 1204** for Environmental Sensor Station (ESS) interfaces, ensuring broad interoperability with existing and future sensor technologies. A crucial constraint is the retention of data ownership by the contributing organizations, with the system required to enforce data dissemination restrictions based on formal data sharing agreements. The system architecture, while flexible regarding centralization, must consistently present a 'one-stop' portal experience to all participants. Furthermore, specific contractual obligations, notably **U.S. Department of Transportation Federal Highway Administration Contract No. DTFH61-05-C-00022**, govern the entire development and deployment process.
userDocumentation: The **clarus low** system will be accompanied by comprehensive user documentation tailored to its diverse audience. This will include detailed user manuals guiding observation system owners and direct data users on data submission, retrieval, and interpretation procedures. Online help functionalities will be integrated into the web portal and administrative interfaces, offering context-sensitive assistance for common tasks. Additionally, tutorials or quick-start guides may be developed to facilitate onboarding for new contributors and service providers, ensuring efficient and effective utilization of the system's capabilities.
assumptionsAndDependencies: It is assumed that existing data collection networks participating in **clarus low** will maintain their autonomous operations and continue to provide data reliably without direct control from the **clarus low** system. A critical dependency is the availability and cooperation of these external data sources, whose data quality and consistency directly impact the overall utility of **clarus low**. Furthermore, the project assumes the continued evolution and adoption of relevant industry standards, such as **NTCIP 1204**, to facilitate seamless integration of diverse sensor technologies. The project also relies on established **Internet** infrastructure for communication between distributed components and external entities.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: The **clarus low** system's primary user interface will be a 'one-stop Internet portal' accessible via standard **Web** browsers. This portal will support interaction for various user classes, offering capabilities for data access, system administration, and potentially manual data entry. The interface design shall prioritize clarity, responsiveness, and ease of navigation, presenting complex environmental data and quality flags in an understandable format. Distinct user interfaces, such as the **Configuration & Administration User Interface (CAUI)** and the **Manual Entry User Interface (MEUI)**, will cater to specialized administrative and data input tasks, adhering to consistent graphical user interface (GUI) standards and accessibility guidelines. Error messages and system feedback will be clear and actionable.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The **clarus low** system will establish logical and physical interfaces with a wide array of hardware components, predominantly environmental sensor stations (ESS) and various field instrumentation. These interfaces are crucial for acquiring atmospheric, pavement, and hydrologic data from distributed sources. The system must support diverse data collection methods and communication protocols, potentially including those mandated by the **NTCIP 1204** standard. Flexibility is paramount to accommodate future integrations with emerging technologies such as vehicle-based sensors, traffic cameras for visibility data, and other remote sensing platforms. These interfaces will involve continuous data streams, requiring robust and resilient connection management.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: The **clarus low** system will interface with numerous internal and external software components. Internally, it will interact with specialized services such as the **Configuration & Administration Service (CAS)**, **Collector Services (CS)**, **Environmental Metadata Services (EMS)**, and **Quality Checking Services (QChS)**. Externally, it will connect with other specific software components like existing weather networks, database systems, and service provider applications. Data exchange will encompass observation data, sensor metadata, and quality flags, utilizing common protocols and data interchange formats such as **NetCDF** for programmatic access. The system must provide robust **APIs** to enable seamless data integration and allow third-party applications to leverage **clarus low**'s data publication capabilities.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: The **clarus low** system requires robust communications interfaces to facilitate the exchange of surface transportation environmental data across its distributed network. This includes secure data acquisition from contributing sensor stations and efficient data dissemination to various service providers and participants. The system shall employ standard communication protocols such as **HTTP** for web-based interactions and potentially **FTP** or dedicated **TCP/IP** channels for high-volume data transfers. All communications involving sensitive or proprietary data will incorporate encryption protocols to ensure data integrity and confidentiality. Requirements include defining pertinent message formatting, adherence to communication standards, specifying security measures, and managing data transfer rates and synchronization mechanisms to support real-time data flows.

systemFeatures.name: Data Acquisition and Collection
systemFeatures.description: The **clarus low** system shall provide robust data acquisition and collection capabilities to gather heterogeneous surface transportation environmental data from various distributed sensor stations. This feature enables the system to receive atmospheric, pavement, and hydrologic data, ensuring a comprehensive data input stream for subsequent processing. The primary user value is the consolidation of diverse environmental observations into a single, accessible platform, maximizing existing sensor investments and enhancing data coverage. The technical impact involves designing flexible interfaces to accommodate varied data formats and communication protocols from multiple external data sources. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: An external sensor station transmits environmental data. Response: The system ingests the data via **Collector Services**, performing initial validation.
Stimulus: A new data source is configured by an administrator. Response: The system updates its collection parameters to begin receiving data from the newly configured source.
functionalRequirements: CL-REQ-001: The system shall ingest real-time environmental data from diverse sensor stations and networks.
CL-REQ-002: The system shall support data reception conforming to the **NTCIP 1204** standard.
CL-REQ-003: The system shall validate the format and completeness of all incoming environmental data.
CL-REQ-004: The system shall manage concurrent connections with multiple, independent data collection networks.
CL-REQ-005: The system shall accept new environmental data types without requiring significant architectural changes.

systemFeatures.name: Environmental Data Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature is responsible for the effective storage, retrieval, and management of environmental data and associated metadata within the **clarus low** system. It encompasses components such as the **Environmental Metadata Cache (EMC)**, **Environmental Metadata Services (EMS)**, **Qualified Environmental Data (QEDC)**, and **Qualified Environmental Data Services (QEDS)**. The user value lies in providing organized, discoverable, and accessible environmental data, enriched with crucial metadata for contextual understanding and reliable usage. Technically, this requires a robust data model and efficient caching mechanisms to support high-volume data access and querying, enabling rapid data publication and historical analysis. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A user requests environmental data for a specific location and time range. Response: The system retrieves qualified environmental data and associated metadata via **QEDS** and **EMS**.

Stimulus: Quality-checked data is made available by the quality checking services. Response: The system updates the **Qualified Environmental Data Cache (QEDC)** and notifies relevant downstream services.
functionalRequirements: CL-REQ-006: The system shall store environmental observations and their associated metadata.
CL-REQ-007: The system shall provide services for querying and retrieving environmental metadata based on various criteria.
CL-REQ-008: The system shall maintain a cache of frequently accessed environmental metadata for expedited retrieval.
CL-REQ-009: The system shall store and manage qualified environmental data after successful quality checking.
CL-REQ-010: The system shall provide services for accessing and publishing qualified environmental data to authorized consumers.

systemFeatures.name: Quality Checking and Processing
systemFeatures.description: The **clarus low** system shall implement comprehensive **Quality Checking Services (QChS)** to ensure the accuracy, consistency, and reliability of all ingested environmental data. This feature performs various validation tests on raw observations, assigns appropriate quality flags, and provides feedback to data originators. The user value is the assurance of high-quality data, which is critical for making informed decisions in transportation safety, mobility, and accurate weather forecasting. From a technical standpoint, it demands a flexible rule-based engine capable of executing numerous quality tests, coupled with efficient data processing to handle continuous streams of incoming data without introducing significant latency. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Raw environmental data is received and made available for processing. Response: The system applies a predefined series of automated quality checks and assigns corresponding quality flags.
Stimulus: A data point fails one or more quality checks. Response: The system generates a quality feedback notification and logs the anomaly for review by administrators or originating stations.
functionalRequirements: CL-REQ-011: The system shall apply a suite of automated quality checks to all incoming environmental data.
CL-REQ-012: The system shall assign precise quality flags to environmental data points based on validation results.
CL-REQ-013: The system shall provide actionable feedback on data quality to originating sensor station operators.
CL-REQ-014: The system shall log all quality check failures, anomalies, and associated metadata for auditing purposes.
CL-REQ-015: The system shall support configurable quality checking rules, thresholds, and test methodologies.

systemFeatures.name: System Configuration and Administration
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides the necessary tools and services for configuring, managing, and maintaining the **clarus low** system, including its data sources, user accounts, and operational parameters. It encompasses the **Configuration & Administration Service (CAS)**, **Configuration & Administration User Interface (CAUI)**, and **Manual Entry User Interface (MEUI)**. The user value is the ability for authorized administrators to effectively control and adapt the system to evolving requirements and new data contributors, ensuring operational continuity and data integrity. Technically, this involves secure access control, robust data validation for configuration changes, and a user-friendly interface for managing complex system settings. Priority: Medium.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: An authorized administrator modifies a system configuration parameter via the **CAUI**. Response: The **CAS** validates and applies the configuration change, updating all relevant system services.
Stimulus: A user manually enters environmental data via the **MEUI**. Response: The system validates the input, integrates it into the data stream, and subjects it to standard quality checks.
functionalRequirements: CL-REQ-016: The system shall provide a secure user interface (**CAUI**) for comprehensive system configuration and administration.
CL-REQ-017: The system shall allow authorized personnel to manage user accounts, roles, and access rights within the system.
CL-REQ-018: The system shall enable the configuration of new data collection sources and their associated parameters.
CL-REQ-019: The system shall support manual entry of environmental data through a dedicated user interface (**MEUI**).
CL-REQ-020: The system shall provide backend services (**CAS**) for programmatic configuration management and API access.

systemFeatures.name: System Monitoring and Scheduling
systemFeatures.description: The **clarus low** system shall incorporate robust monitoring (**Watchdog - DOG**) and scheduling (**Schedule Service - SS**) functionalities to ensure its continuous operation, health, and timely execution of automated tasks. This feature proactively monitors system components for anomalies, manages task queues, and orchestrates scheduled operations such as data collection intervals or quality check routines. The user value is enhanced operational stability and reliability, minimizing downtime and ensuring that critical data processes are executed as planned and on schedule. Technically, this requires a persistent scheduling mechanism, a resilient monitoring agent, and clear alerting capabilities to address system issues promptly. Priority: Medium.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A critical system component becomes unresponsive or reports an error. Response: The **Watchdog (DOG)** detects the anomaly, logs the event, and triggers an alert to administrators.

Stimulus: A scheduled data collection task is due for execution. Response: The **Schedule Service (SS)** initiates the corresponding data acquisition process, coordinating with relevant collector services.
functionalRequirements: CL-REQ-021: The system shall continuously monitor the health and operational status of its components via the **Watchdog (DOG)**.
CL-REQ-022: The system shall log all monitoring events and generate alerts for critical issues and system failures.
CL-REQ-023: The system shall provide a **Schedule Service (SS)** to manage and execute recurring system tasks.
CL-REQ-024: The system shall allow administrators to define, modify, and manage task schedules via the administration interface.
CL-REQ-025: The system shall ensure the timely execution of all scheduled data processing and publication routines.

performanceRequirements: CL-NFR-001: The **clarus low** system shall process incoming real-time environmental observations with an end-to-end latency not exceeding 5 seconds from sensor acquisition to internal system ingestion for 95% of data points. This stringent requirement is critical for enabling timely operational responses to weather conditions impacting surface transportation, directly affecting public safety and mobility decisions.

CL-NFR-002: The system shall support a sustained data ingestion rate of up to 10,000 observations per second during peak demand periods without any degradation in processing performance or data loss. Given the potential vast volume of data from a 'network of networks', high throughput is essential to prevent data backlogs and ensure the continuous availability of real-time environmental data.

CL-NFR-003: The system shall retrieve qualified environmental data for a specified location and time range within 1 second for 90% of all user and API requests. Rapid data retrieval is necessary for service providers to generate timely forecasts and for decision support systems to provide up-to-date information, thereby enhancing the overall usability and utility of the **clarus low** system.
safetyRequirements: CL-NFR-004: The **clarus low** system shall incorporate robust mechanisms to prevent the dissemination of demonstrably erroneous, corrupt, or unverified environmental data to downstream consumers. All data must successfully pass a comprehensive suite of quality checks before being flagged as qualified and published, mitigating the risk of misleading information that could lead to hazardous transportation decisions or inaccurate weather forecasts.

CL-NFR-005: In the event of a critical system failure or major component outage, the system shall provide clear, immediate diagnostic information to enable rapid identification of the root cause and facilitate prompt recovery. This capability is paramount as **clarus low** supports critical infrastructure, where minimizing downtime is essential to maintain continuous service for safety-related applications and decision-making processes.
securityRequirements: CL-NFR-006: The **clarus low** system shall implement strict role-based access control (RBAC) to restrict all configuration and administrative functionalities exclusively to authorized personnel. This measure ensures the integrity of system settings and prevents any unauthorized modifications that could compromise data quality, system operations, or overall security posture.

CL-NFR-007: The system shall encrypt all sensitive data in transit, both between internal components and during transmission to external service providers and participants, utilizing industry-standard cryptographic protocols such as **TLS 1.2** or higher. This protects proprietary data and ensures privacy as required by contractual agreements and data ownership policies, safeguarding against interception and tampering.

CL-NFR-008: The **clarus low** system shall provide robust multi-factor authentication (MFA) mechanisms for all administrative and user interfaces, including the **Configuration & Administration User Interface (CAUI)** and **Manual Entry User Interface (MEUI)**. This critical security control prevents unauthorized access to system resources, data manipulation, and ensures that only legitimate users can interact with the system's sensitive functionalities.
softwareQualityAttributes: CL-NFR-009: **Reliability**: The **clarus low** system shall achieve an annual uptime of 99.9%, excluding pre-scheduled maintenance windows. This high reliability is crucial because continuous availability of environmental data directly impacts transportation safety, mobility management, and operational decision-making processes across North America.

CL-NFR-010: **Maintainability**: The system architecture shall be highly modular, allowing for independent updates, maintenance, and deployment of individual components such as **Collector Services**, **Quality Checking Services**, and **Environmental Metadata Services**. This modularity significantly reduces the risk and complexity associated with system changes, thereby facilitating long-term evolution and adaptation of the **clarus low** system.

CL-NFR-011: **Scalability**: The **clarus low** system shall be capable of scaling horizontally to seamlessly accommodate a growing number of data contributors, increasing data volumes, and expanding user demand without requiring significant re-architecting or performance degradation. The 'network of networks' concept inherently implies potential for substantial growth, necessitating a highly scalable architecture.

CL-NFR-012: **Interoperability**: The system shall support data exchange with diverse external systems and data formats using open standards and common protocols, including **NTCIP 1204** and **NetCDF**. This attribute is fundamental to its role as a central data hub, enabling seamless integration with existing and future data sources and consumers across the surface transportation and weather communities.

CL-NFR-013: **Usability**: The administrative and manual entry user interfaces of **clarus low** shall be intuitive and require minimal specialized training for users with relevant technical expertise. An easily navigable and understandable interface for administrators and data entry personnel ensures efficient system management, reduces operational errors, and promotes broader adoption and effective utilization of the system.
businessRules: CL-BR-001: Data ownership shall be explicitly retained by the organization that provided the data to the **clarus low** system, with the system acting as a facilitator for data consolidation and distribution. This rule ensures legal clarity and fosters trust, thereby encouraging broader participation and data sharing within the network.

CL-BR-002: Data providers shall have the right to define and enforce specific restrictions on the dissemination of their contributed data through formal data sharing agreements with the **clarus low** program. This enables granular access control, balancing the goals of widespread data availability with proprietary concerns and privacy requirements.

CL-BR-003: All data collected, processed, and disseminated by the **clarus low** system must strictly adhere to applicable U.S. Department of Transportation (U.S. DOT) regulations and the terms stipulated in Contract No. DTFH61-05-C-00022. This overarching rule ensures legal and regulatory compliance for all aspects of the project's deliverables and ongoing operations.

otherRequirements: CL-OTH-001: The **clarus low** system, in its current phase of development, shall not include direct archiving capabilities for the large volume of historical environmental data. This specific functionality will be externalized to other systems or explicitly deferred to a subsequent phase of development, managing the scope and complexity of the initial implementation.

glossary.definition: An initiative sponsored by the U.S. Department of Transportation to collect, quality check, and make available surface transportation weather and road condition observations.
Concept of Operations, a document detailing the operational context, high-level objectives, and user needs of a system.
Detailed Requirements Specification, this document specifying the high-level and detailed requirements for the system.
Environmental Sensor Station, a type of field instrumentation that collects various environmental data parameters.
National Transportation Communications for ITS Protocol, a family of standards for electronic traffic control devices and related systems.
Configuration & Administration Service, a core backend service responsible for managing system configurations and parameters.
Configuration & Administration User Interface, the primary graphical user interface for system administrators to manage **clarus low** settings.
Collector Services, the system component responsible for ingesting environmental data from various external sources.
Watchdog, a monitoring component that continuously checks the health and operational status of other system services.
Environmental Metadata Cache, a system component designed for caching frequently accessed environmental metadata.
Environmental Metadata Services, services dedicated to managing, storing, and providing access to environmental metadata.
Manual Entry User Interface, an interface allowing authorized users to manually input environmental data into the system.
Qualified Environmental Data Cache, a temporary storage or cache for environmental data that has successfully passed quality checks.
Qualified Environmental Data Services, services responsible for accessing, managing, and publishing quality-checked environmental data.
Quality Checking Services, the system module responsible for performing automated data quality validation tests.
Schedule Service, a system service that manages and orchestrates the execution of recurring system tasks and processes.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart illustrates the high-level data acquisition, processing, and publication flow within the **clarus low** system. Nodes represent processes or states, and arrows indicate the direction of data or control flow. Decision points are diamond-shaped, representing conditional logic. All labels are double-quoted as required for clarity and adherence to naming conventions.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
    StartProcess["Sensor Station Data Feed"] --> CS["Collector Services (CS)"]
    CS --> InitialValidation{"Initial Data Valid?"}
    InitialValidation -- No --> LogError["Log Error and Alert"]
    InitialValidation -- Yes --> QCS["Quality Checking Services (QChS)"]
    QCS --> QualityCheck{"Data Qualified?"}
    QualityCheck -- No --> GenFeedback["Generate Quality Feedback"]
    QualityCheck -- Yes --> EMS["Environmental Metadata Services (EMS)"]
    EMS --> QEDS["Qualified Environmental Data Services (QEDS)"]
    QEDS --> EndProcess["Publish to Service Providers/Users"]
    LogError --> EndProcess
    GenFeedback --> EndProcess
flowchartDiagram.caption: clarus low Data Acquisition and Publication Flow

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram details the interaction sequence for data ingestion and quality checking within the **clarus low** system. Participants represent system components or external entities, and arrows denote messages exchanged over time. The 'alt' block illustrates conditional flows based on the outcome of quality checks, showing both successful qualification and rejection scenarios. Message labels are clear and descriptive, avoiding special characters for strict compliance.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant SensorStation as "Sensor Station"
    participant CollectorServices as "Collector Services (CS)"
    participant QualityCheckingServices as "Quality Checking Services (QChS)"
    participant EnvironmentalMetadataServices as "Environmental Metadata Services (EMS)"
    participant QualifiedEnvironmentalDataServices as "Qualified Environmental Data Services (QEDS)"
    participant ServiceProvider as "Service Provider"

    SensorStation->>+CollectorServices: "Transmit Raw Observation Data"
    CollectorServices->>QualityCheckingServices: "Submit Data for Quality Check"
    QualityCheckingServices->>QualityCheckingServices: "Perform Quality Tests"
    alt Data is Qualified
        QualityCheckingServices-->>CollectorServices: "Return Qualified Data + Flags"
        CollectorServices->>EnvironmentalMetadataServices: "Store Data with Metadata"
        EnvironmentalMetadataServices->>QualifiedEnvironmentalDataServices: "Make Qualified Data Available"
        QualifiedEnvironmentalDataServices-->>ServiceProvider: "Publish Environmental Data"
    else Data Fails Quality Check
        QualityCheckingServices-->>CollectorServices: "Return Rejected Data + Error Flags"
        CollectorServices-->>SensorStation: "Send Quality Feedback/Alert"
    end
    CollectorServices-->>-SensorStation: "Acknowledge Receipt"

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) models the core entities and their relationships within the **clarus low** system's data model. Entities represent key data concepts, attributes define their properties, and relationships illustrate how entities are connected, including cardinality. Primary keys (PK) and foreign keys (FK) are explicitly denoted to indicate data integrity constraints. All entity and relationship labels with spaces are double-quoted, and attribute names are alphanumeric, adhering to strict Mermaid ERD syntax rules.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
    USER ||--o{ STATION : "owns"
    STATION ||--o{ SENSOR : "hosts"
    SENSOR ||--o{ OBSERVATION : "generates"
    OBSERVATION ||--o{ QUALITY_LOG : "has quality check"
    QUALITY_RULE ||--o{ QUALITY_LOG : "applied"
    USER ||--o{ CONFIGURATION : "modifies"

    USER {
        int id PK
        string username
        string email
        string role
    }

    STATION {
        int id PK
        string name
        decimal latitude
        decimal longitude
        string type
        int owner_id FK
    }

    SENSOR {
        int id PK
        int station_id FK
        string type
        string parameter
        string unit
    }

    OBSERVATION {
        int id PK
        int sensor_id FK
        datetime timestamp
        decimal value
        string quality_flags
    }

    QUALITY_RULE {
        int id PK
        string name
        string description
        string rule_expression
    }

    QUALITY_LOG {
        int id PK
        int observation_id FK
        int rule_id FK
        boolean passed
        string error_message
        datetime check_timestamp
    }

    CONFIGURATION {
        int id PK
        string config_key
        string config_value
        datetime last_updated
        int last_updated_by FK
    }
