introduction.purpose: The purpose of this Software Requirements Specification (SRS) is to meticulously define the operational and functional requirements for the **gemini** Control System software. This document aims to serve as a foundational tool, guiding the comprehensive development of critical controls and data acquisition systems. It ensures that all system components operate cohesively and consistently within the broader **gemini** System framework, establishing both general criteria and specific functional requirements for software and control design.
introduction.documentConventions: This document adheres strictly to the guidelines set forth by IEEE 830-1998 Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications. All requirements are numbered sequentially with a 'G-REQ-' prefix, while use cases are identified as 'G-UC-'. Definitions of terms, acronyms, and abbreviations are provided in Appendix A, ensuring clarity and consistency throughout the specification.
introduction.intendedAudience: This SRS is primarily intended for the development teams responsible for designing, implementing, and testing the **gemini** Control System software and its associated data acquisition components. Secondary audiences include project managers, quality assurance personnel, and system architects who require a detailed understanding of the system's intended behavior and constraints. This document is not specifically tailored for the end-user science astronomers, but rather provides the technical foundation for their operational tools.
introduction.productScope: The **gemini** system encompasses all software directly involved in the control and operation of the **gemini** telescopes, including essential commercial off-the-shelf packages and custom-developed solutions. This specification focuses on defining the non-commercial software components and their interfaces with commercial packages. It categorizes software into developed, supported (commercial/public domain), and external components, detailing interface specifications for the latter two. Excluded from detailed specification are embedded software components that do not present a software interface to the main **gemini** system.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.
Gemini Science Requirements, Version 1.1 (SPE-PS-G001, November 11, 1992)
Goals and Requirements for Software and Controls (SPE-C-G0026, August 23, 1993)
Operational Concept Definition (SPE-C-G0015, April 26, 1993)
Software and Control Management Plan (PG-C-G0005, April 5, 1993)
Software Design Specification (SPE-C-G0037, April 20, 1994)
Software Configuration Control Plan (SPE-C-G0011, August 23, 1993)
Gemini Electronic Design Specification (SPE-ASA-G0008, June 21, 1994)

overallDescription.productPerspective:
The **gemini** Control System is conceived as a new, self-contained product designed to manage the sophisticated operations of the Gemini 8-meter Telescopes. It integrates various subsystems to provide comprehensive control over astronomical observations and data acquisition, rather than being a follow-on product. The system's architecture accounts for a multi-telescope, multi-instrument, and multi-user operational context, ensuring robust performance across diverse scenarios.

A central tenet of the **gemini** system's design is the Virtual Observatory System (VOS) model, which aims to minimize intersystem dependencies and abstract subsystem implementation details from higher-level operations. This approach facilitates modularity and maintainability, crucial for a complex astronomical facility. The system must also support various operational levels, including observing, maintenance, and testing, with defined access restrictions for each, ensuring operational integrity and security.

Furthermore, the **gemini** system anticipates integration with external components and visitor instruments. While core functionalities are tightly integrated, external software, such as user-supplied data reduction tools or operating systems, interacts via well-defined interfaces. The system also supports guest instrumentation through a standardized interface subset, ensuring interoperability without compromising the stability of the primary control system or requiring full integration.
productFunctions: Enable efficient planning and scheduling of astronomical observations.
Provide real-time control and monitoring of the **gemini** telescopes and scientific instruments.
Facilitate high-volume astronomical data acquisition, processing, and storage.
Support multiple operational modes including interactive, queue-based, and remote observing.
Manage user authentication, authorization, and operational privileges across different user classes and access modes.
Integrate and manage data flow for control information, astronomical data, and video feeds.
Support robust fault notification, fault tolerance, and redundancy mechanisms for system reliability.
Provide a standardized interface for integrating visitor instruments with limited functionality.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: These are the end-users of the **gemini** system, varying in experience from occasional to highly experienced staff astronomers. Their primary goal is the efficient acquisition of astronomical data and on-line assessment of data quality, rather than direct control of the telescope. They operate in Planning, Observing, and Monitoring access modes, utilizing simple, safe user interfaces focused on observation activities and data assessment. Astronomers typically have control access to instruments but no direct telescope control.

On-site personnel responsible for monitoring data acquisition and validating data integrity for astronomers. They ensure the science plan functions as intended, often working in conjunction with the Telescope Operator. Science Observers are typically very experienced users of the system, operating in Observing and Monitoring access modes with interfaces similar to astronomers but with a focus on data validation and quality control.

The on-site controller of the telescope and instruments, tasked with ensuring system integrity and accurate functioning during observations. This highly experienced user works with the Observer to optimize data collection, operating in Observing, Maintenance, and Test operational levels. Their access modes include Observing, Monitoring, Operation, and Testing, requiring interfaces organized for quick control response and the ability to monitor all subsystems for unsafe conditions.

On-site or near-site personnel responsible for system maintenance, both hardware and software, and managing subsystem installations and configuration changes. They are critical for system uptime and reliability. Support Staff primarily operate in Maintenance and Test levels, requiring Monitoring, Operation, and Testing access modes to allow full access to all subsystems for diagnostic and maintenance activities.

Responsible for designing, testing, configuring, and upgrading subsystems. Developers possess a deep understanding of the **gemini** software architecture. They require the highest privilege levels to modify system components, operating primarily in Maintenance and Test levels with Monitoring and Testing access modes. Strict configuration control guidelines are enforced to prevent interference with ongoing normal operations, especially during integration tests.

Responsible for high-level functional control of the integrated **gemini** system. Administrators work with other user classes to determine observation scheduling changes, maintenance downtime, and system modifications. They typically operate across all operational levels (Observing, Maintenance, Test) with Monitoring and Administrative access modes, using interfaces for status inquiries and scheduling information without direct control capabilities.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment:
The **gemini** system operates within a complex, distributed environment across multiple physical facilities. The core operational environment is the Gemini 8m Telescopes site, comprising the Enclosure (housing the telescope) and the Control Facility (for nightly operations). Additionally, personnel may be located at the Site Support Facility for off-duty residence, and the Base Facility which houses offices and shops for administrative and logistical support functions.

Further extending the operational reach, the system must support Remote Operations Facilities, which are super sites in partner countries linked directly to the telescopes via high-speed communication lines, offering remote operations capabilities. Satellite Site Facilities, connected to super sites, provide remote operations at a reduced level due to varying bandwidth. Institutional Site Facilities, typically at existing observatories or universities, offer connectivity via satellite or super sites, with remote operation capabilities dependent on link capacity.

The software must coexist with diverse hardware platforms across these locations, including workstations at control facilities and various client devices at remote sites, potentially running different operating systems. It is expected to interact with specialized telescope and instrument hardware controllers. The system needs to accommodate operational levels such as Observing, Maintenance, and Test, each imposing specific access controls and system configurations.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The **gemini** software system must adhere to a modular design philosophy, specifically leveraging the Virtual Observatory System (VOS) and Virtual System Interface (VSI) to minimize intersystem dependencies and abstract subsystem implementation details. This architectural approach aims to enhance maintainability and expandability, supporting future upgrades and new instrument integrations efficiently.

Hardware limitations impose significant constraints, including specific timing requirements for control systems, memory limitations for data acquisition, and predefined interfaces to existing telescope and instrument hardware. The system design must accommodate multiple scientific instruments simultaneously mounted on the telescope, managing active and inactive states while ensuring no adverse impact on the active instrument.

Integration with specific technologies, tools, and databases is mandated, including the use of on-line database subsystems and defined communication protocols. The system must support the Experimental Physics and Industrial Control System (EPICS) framework for developers, implying adherence to EPICS components, task breakdowns, and required developer skills.

Regulatory policies and **gemini** project standards, such as those for software configuration control and development environments, must be strictly followed. The system design must also anticipate parallel operations and ensure robust communication protocols across distributed facilities, including high-bandwidth links for remote operations and handling varying levels of connectivity.

Security considerations necessitate the implementation of robust privilege and protection levels for different user classes and operational modes, ensuring system integrity and data security. The design must incorporate fault tolerance mechanisms, redundancy, and explicit fault notification procedures to maintain high availability and reliability, especially during critical observing periods.

User interface design must balance ease of use with the flexibility required for complex control operations. While different access levels may present distinct 'look-and-feels,' a uniform interface standard across all subsystems is required for consistency. The system must also accommodate visitor instruments through a standardized, stable, and long-lived interface subset, prioritizing basic functionality over complex coordinated motions.
userDocumentation: The **gemini** system will be delivered with comprehensive user documentation, including a detailed User Manual providing step-by-step instructions for astronomers and science observers on observation planning, data acquisition, and quick-look data reduction. Online help resources will be integrated into the user interface, offering contextual assistance and troubleshooting guides.

For telescope operators and support staff, specific operational guides will detail system control procedures, maintenance routines, and diagnostic workflows. Developers will receive technical documentation covering API specifications, software architecture, and guidelines for integrating new modules or visitor instruments. All documentation will adhere to established **gemini** project standards for format and content.
assumptionsAndDependencies: It is assumed that the external communication links to Remote Operations Facilities, Satellite Site Facilities, and Institutional Site Facilities will provide varying but sufficient bandwidth to support the required level of remote operations. The specific capabilities of these links (private vs. public networks) will directly influence the achievable remote functionality, representing a critical dependency.

The successful integration and operation of commercial off-the-shelf software packages, as well as publicly available domain software used for telescope control and instrumentation, are assumed. The **gemini** system relies on the stability and defined interfaces of these third-party components.

The project depends on the finalization of **gemini** 8m Telescopes standards for the acquisition and storage of detector data, as well as the chosen link for data transfer. Until these are defined, specific data handling and communication implementations remain dependent variables. The desirable hardware specifications and standards for online software and development environment are also pending definition, impacting design choices.

The availability of appropriately skilled EPICS developers is assumed for the implementation and maintenance of relevant subsystems. The supportability plan for the entire **gemini** system also represents a significant dependency, affecting long-term maintenance and operational readiness.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces:
The **gemini** system shall provide graphical user interfaces (**GUI**) for astronomers, science observers, and telescope operators, adhering to a consistent aesthetic and functional design across all subsystems. These interfaces will be organized to group controls for convenience of observation activities, quick response to unsafe conditions, and comprehensive monitoring capabilities. A Command Line User Interface (**CLUI**) may also be provided for developers and advanced support staff requiring direct, granular control.

User interfaces for different access levels shall maintain a uniform appearance across all subsystems, even while presenting distinct 'look-and-feels' to differentiate operational contexts. Standard buttons, navigation paradigms, and error message display standards shall be consistently applied. The system shall support both local and remote user interface access, with remote capabilities adapting to the available network bandwidth and access mode privileges, ensuring a responsive and intuitive user experience.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces:
The **gemini** software system shall interface with various hardware components, including the telescope mount, scientific instruments, detector systems, and environmental sensors. These interfaces will be governed by defined communication protocols, such as those inherent to the Experimental Physics and Industrial Control System (**EPICS**) framework where applicable. The software must accommodate the real-time data and control interactions necessary for precision pointing, tracking, and instrument configuration.

Specific requirements include interfacing with detector hardware for astronomical data acquisition, managing beam splitters or field splitters for multi-detector instruments, and coordinating motions between telescope components and instruments. The system shall also interface with safety-critical hardware mechanisms for fault notification and emergency shutdowns. The design must account for hardware limitations such, as timing requirements and memory constraints, as specified for various telescope and instrument subsystems.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces:
The **gemini** system shall establish robust interfaces with various internal and external software components. This includes the Observatory Control Software (**OCS**), Telescope Control Software (**TCS**), Instrument Control Software (**ICS**), and Input/Output Control Modules (**IOC**). These interfaces will facilitate control information flow, astronomical data flow, and video information flow, ensuring seamless command routing and data exchange across the system. An on-line database subsystem, likely a **Relational DBMS (RDBMS)**, will serve as a central repository for operational data, configurations, and astronomical observations.

Interprocess Communication (**IPC**) mechanisms and Remote Procedure Call (**RPC**) protocols will be employed to enable distributed software components to interact efficiently, potentially across a Local Area Network (**LAN**) and Wide Area Network (**WAN**). For visitor instruments, a standardized interface subset will be provided, allowing external software to acquire status information, submit preprogrammed observing sequences, and offset telescope position and focus. This interface will also aim to support coordinate transforms, archiving, and maintenance of operation tables, although these are considered goals rather than strict requirements for visitor instruments.

The system will also interface with external software for purposes such as star catalogs (e.g., **STARCAT**), and potentially commercial or public-domain software for specific functions. Data formats, such as Flexible Image Transport System (**FITS**) for astronomical data, will be adhered to for interoperability. The software interfaces must support transparent access to physical units where necessary, while adhering to privilege and protection levels.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces:
The **gemini** system shall implement comprehensive communication functions to support its distributed operational environment. This includes network server communications protocols for both Local Area Network (**LAN**) and Wide Area Network (**WAN**) connections, facilitating data transfer between various facilities. Communication protocols will include positive/negative acknowledgment (**ACK/NAK**) and command handshaking for reliable message exchange between subsystems.

Support for remote operations will necessitate robust communication links, including high-speed direct links to the **gemini** telescope for full control and monitoring, and slower links for screen-copying and compressed data transfer. The system will support voice communication, compressed digitized video, and FAX capabilities for sites with limited connectivity, such as those relying on Integrated Services Digital Network (**ISDN**) connections. Security considerations for communications, including potential encryption and secure data transfer rates, must be addressed to protect sensitive control and scientific data.

systemFeatures.name: Observation Planning and Scheduling
systemFeatures.description: This feature allows astronomers to create, manage, and submit formal descriptions of their science plans, known as Science Programs, for execution on the **gemini** telescopes. It provides a software environment for developing observing software and facilitates the efficient allocation of telescope resources, thereby optimizing scientific data collection. Technically, it integrates with various system components to ensure that scheduled observations are dispatched and executed correctly, enhancing overall observatory productivity and supporting flexible scheduling.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Astronomer initiates the creation of a new Science Program. Response: The system presents a planning interface, allowing the astronomer to define observation parameters and sequences.
Stimulus: Astronomer submits a completed Science Program to the scheduler. Response: The system validates the program, adds it to a queue, and provides confirmation to the astronomer.
functionalRequirements: G-REQ-001: The system shall provide an interface for astronomers to define and edit Science Programs, including instrument configurations, target coordinates, and exposure parameters.
G-REQ-002: The system shall validate submitted Science Programs against current telescope and instrument capabilities and operational rules.
G-REQ-003: The system shall manage a queue of approved Science Programs, allowing for resequencing and suspension of observations based on operational needs or data quality assessments.
G-REQ-004: The system shall allow operations staff to enable or disable direct interactive observation, overriding the automatic sequencer when necessary.

systemFeatures.name: Telescope and Instrument Control
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides the core functionality for direct control and configuration of the **gemini** telescopes and mounted scientific instruments. It empowers Telescope Operators to ensure system integrity and accurate functioning during observations, crucial for collecting high-quality scientific data. Technically, it handles command routing, command handshaking, and direct access to physical units, abstracting complex hardware interactions through the Virtual System Interface (VSI) model and ensuring safe, controlled execution of all operational commands.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Telescope Operator issues a command to slew the telescope to new coordinates. Response: The system validates the command, executes the slew, and updates the telescope status in real-time.

Stimulus: Telescope Operator selects an active instrument from the mounted instruments. Response: The system reconfigures the telescope beam to the selected instrument and updates its status to active.
functionalRequirements: G-REQ-005: The system shall provide a user interface for Telescope Operators to issue direct commands for telescope pointing, tracking, and focusing.
G-REQ-006: The system shall manage the state of multiple mounted instruments, ensuring only one instrument is active (has access to the telescope beam) at any given time.
G-REQ-007: The system shall enable inactive instruments to perform parallel operations such as calibration or flat-field exposures.
G-REQ-008: The system shall support command handshaking protocols for reliable command acceptance and execution confirmation with subsystems.
G-REQ-009: The system shall provide mechanisms for developers to access Input/Output Control Modules (IOC) for local database access, time handling, and remote database access.

systemFeatures.name: Data Acquisition and Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature encompasses the processes for acquiring, compressing, storing, and managing astronomical data generated by the **gemini** instruments. It is vital for astronomers to collect high-resolution scientific data and for ensuring its integrity and accessibility for post-processing and archival. Technically, it involves managing high-bandwidth astronomical data flow, potentially employing loss-less and data-loss compression techniques, and interfacing with an on-line database subsystem for metadata and observational logs. The system must support efficient data transfer to various facilities and ensure data persistence.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Active instrument completes an exposure and generates raw data. Response: The system acquires the data, applies specified compression, and initiates storage to the designated data repository.
Stimulus: Science Observer requests a quick-look assessment of recently acquired data. Response: The system retrieves and displays a compressed or processed version of the data for immediate review.
functionalRequirements: G-REQ-010: The system shall acquire astronomical data from active instruments at specified rates and resolutions.
G-REQ-011: The system shall support both loss-less and data-loss compression algorithms for astronomical data, configurable per observation.
G-REQ-012: The system shall store acquired astronomical data in a structured format, such as **FITS**, to an on-line database subsystem or network file system.
G-REQ-013: The system shall provide mechanisms for retrieving, cataloging, and archiving astronomical data, including associated metadata and observing logs.
G-REQ-014: The system shall manage the flow of video information, allowing for monitoring of telescope and instrument status.

systemFeatures.name: System Monitoring and Diagnostics
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides continuous oversight of the **gemini** system's health, performance, and operational status, enabling operators and support staff to quickly identify and address anomalies. It is crucial for ensuring the integrity, reliability, and safety of complex telescope operations. Technically, it involves collecting status information from all subsystems, providing fault notifications, and allowing detailed diagnostic access. This enhances the system's availability and facilitates proactive maintenance, minimizing downtime and optimizing scientific output.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A subsystem reports a critical fault condition. Response: The system generates an immediate fault notification to relevant operators and logs the event for diagnostic purposes.

Stimulus: Telescope Operator requests the status of a specific instrument. Response: The system retrieves and displays real-time operational parameters and health indicators for the requested instrument.
functionalRequirements: G-REQ-015: The system shall continuously monitor the operational status and performance metrics of all connected telescope and instrument subsystems.
G-REQ-016: The system shall provide real-time fault notification to operators upon detection of critical errors or unsafe conditions.
G-REQ-017: The system shall log all system events, operational status changes, and fault conditions for post-analysis and auditing.
G-REQ-018: The system shall allow support staff to access full diagnostic capabilities for any subsystem operating in maintenance or test levels.
G-REQ-019: The system shall implement redundancy where critical to ensure continuous operation and fault tolerance.

performanceRequirements: G-REQ-020: The system shall ensure that critical telescope pointing and tracking commands execute with a response time of less than 100 milliseconds to maintain observational accuracy. This is critical for high-resolution astronomy.

G-REQ-021: The system shall be capable of acquiring astronomical data from active instruments at a sustained rate of up to 50 MB/s, supporting the output of high-speed detectors. This capacity is necessary for large datasets generated during scientific observations.

G-REQ-022: The system shall achieve an overall availability of 98% during scheduled observing periods, including planned maintenance windows. This high availability is required to maximize scientific output from the multi-million dollar telescope infrastructure.

G-REQ-023: The system shall update critical status displays for Telescope Operators within 1 second of a change in subsystem state to enable rapid response to operational events. Fast updates are vital for real-time control and safety.
safetyRequirements: G-REQ-024: The system shall implement interlocks to prevent hazardous operations, such as telescope movement conflicting with instrument safety zones or maintenance activities. This is paramount to protect personnel and extremely valuable equipment.

G-REQ-025: The system shall provide immediate fault notification and automatic graceful shutdown procedures upon detection of critical hardware failures or environmental conditions (e.g., high winds). Rapid response is necessary to mitigate damage to the telescope and its components.

G-REQ-026: The system shall ensure that any permitted action by an inactive instrument cannot adversely impact the active instrument's performance or safety. This prevents unintended interference during multi-instrument operations.

G-REQ-027: The system shall log all safety-critical events and operator overrides, providing an auditable trail for incident analysis. Comprehensive logging supports continuous improvement of safety protocols.
securityRequirements: G-REQ-028: The system shall implement role-based access control (RBAC) to restrict user access to commands and data based on their assigned user class (e.g., Astronomer, Telescope Operator, Developer) and operational level. This granular control is essential to prevent unauthorized operations and maintain system integrity.

G-REQ-029: The system shall require strong authentication for all users accessing control interfaces, employing secure credential management practices. Robust authentication protects against unauthorized access to critical telescope operations.

G-REQ-030: The system shall ensure that monitoring and administrative access modes do not affect the performance of an ongoing observation. This is critical to maintain the quality and continuity of scientific data collection.

G-REQ-031: The system shall protect sensitive configuration data and astronomical observations at rest and in transit through appropriate encryption and access restrictions. Data privacy and integrity are paramount for scientific research and operational security.
softwareQualityAttributes: G-REQ-032: Simplicity: The system shall prioritize user interface simplicity for astronomers and science observers, grouping controls logically for observation activities. A simple interface reduces cognitive load and minimizes operational errors, enhancing user efficiency.

G-REQ-033: Supportability: The system shall be designed to facilitate easy diagnosis and repair of issues by support staff, including comprehensive logging and remote diagnostic capabilities. High supportability is crucial for minimizing downtime in a complex distributed system.

G-REQ-034: Reliability and Availability: The system shall implement fault tolerance, redundancy, and robust error handling to minimize service interruptions and maximize uptime during critical observing windows. Consistent operation is fundamental for scientific productivity.

G-REQ-035: Maintainability: The system shall be developed with modular components and clear internal documentation to allow for efficient updates, bug fixes, and feature enhancements. Good maintainability reduces life-cycle costs and extends the system's lifespan.

G-REQ-036: Human Engineering: User interfaces shall be intuitive and consistent across different access levels, balancing ease of use with the complexity of telescope control. Effective human-computer interaction minimizes operator fatigue and errors.

G-REQ-037: Testability: The system shall provide clear interfaces and mechanisms for comprehensive testing of all subsystems and their interactions, facilitating both unit and integration testing. High testability ensures system correctness and reliability before deployment.

G-REQ-038: Expandability: The system architecture shall support the seamless integration of new scientific instruments, software modules, and future telescope enhancements without requiring major redesigns. Expandability ensures the system's longevity and adaptability to evolving scientific needs.

G-REQ-039: Modularity: The system shall be composed of loosely coupled, well-defined modules with clear interfaces to enhance maintainability, testability, and parallel development efforts. Modularity is essential for managing the complexity of a large-scale control system.

G-REQ-040: Contingencies: The system shall include robust mechanisms for fault notification, fault tolerance, and graceful degradation in the event of partial system failures. Effective contingency planning preserves system integrity and operational continuity.

G-REQ-041: Concurrency: The system shall efficiently manage concurrent access by multiple users and subsystems, ensuring data consistency and preventing resource contention. Concurrency support is critical for multi-user, multi-instrument operations.
businessRules: G-REQ-042: Only one scientific instrument shall have access to the telescope beam (be active) at any given time, regardless of the number of mounted instruments. This rule prevents observational interference.

G-REQ-043: Monitoring and administrative access modes shall never adversely affect the performance or integrity of an ongoing observation or system operation. This ensures data quality and operational stability.

G-REQ-044: Direct control commands to the telescope shall typically be mediated through an automatic sequencer, with direct interactive control reserved for operations staff under specific circumstances. This establishes a single point of control and responsibility.

G-REQ-045: Software development and maintenance staff require the highest system privileges, but all modifications must adhere to strict configuration control guidelines. This balances flexibility with system stability.
G-REQ-046: The system must support flexible scheduling of observations, allowing for dynamic adjustments based on real-time conditions or scientific priorities. This optimizes telescope usage.

otherRequirements: The **gemini** software system shall support its own development and maintenance within a defined life cycle, including provisions for system generation, installation of new software packages, and new releases according to established test and validation procedures. This ensures that the system can evolve and be sustained over its operational lifetime.

The system shall provide facilities for easy and nearly automatic inclusion into the **gemini** software environment for all developed and supported software components. This streamlines deployment and configuration management, reducing manual overhead and potential errors.

The system shall provide detailed information regarding its installation aspects, including hardware and software prerequisites, installation procedures, and configuration settings for all supported operational environments. This ensures consistent and reliable deployment across various facilities.

glossary.definition: Acquisition and Guiding.

A method of allocating resources that works using both access method and access privileges. For example, the Telescope Operator may be able to issue direct commands to the TCS, even while the TCS is under the control of a planned observing program. An on-site observer might have privileges that allow the monitoring of systems that are allocated to others.
(network) Any communication protocol that mandates either a positive or negative acknowledgment to all messages.
The use of hardware to isolate sections of a network, so messages with both source and destination on the same section are not transmitted to other sections.
Computer-Aided Software Engineering.
Command Line User Interface.

Any compression algorithm that results in the loss of original data (resolution) after uncompression. Typical compression savings from these algorithms depend upon the amount of data loss that is acceptable, but can exceed 99%. Also see 'loss-less compression'.
Database Management System.
Allowing an observer to watch an observation from a secondary site.
European Southern Observatory.
Flexible Image Transport System.
Graphical User Interface.
(command) A software protocol where subsystems acknowledge acceptance of a command. Unlike ACK/NAK, handshaking acknowledges acceptance of the message contents.
High-level Operations Software.
Instrument Control Software.
Input/Output Control Module.
Interprocess Communication.
Local Area Network.
Any compression algorithm that results in no data loss on uncompression. Typical compression savings with loss-less compression are 25-75%. Also see data-loss compression.
Network File System.
The 'styles' of observing supported by the **gemini** Control System.
Observatory Control Software.
A software-environment for developing observing software.
Relational DBMS.
Read-Only memory.
Remote Procedure Call.
The portion of the **gemini** Control System that is responsible for dispatching Science Programs to the Sequencer for execution.
A formal description of an observer's plan for using the **gemini** 8-m Telescopes, suitable for near-automatic execution.
The command interpreter for Science Programs and telescope control.
Software Requirements Specification (this document).
Space Telescope Archive and Catalog.
To Be Determined.
Telescope Control Software.
ESO’s Very Large Telescope.

Virtual Observatory System - a model for designing the **gemini** Control System that seeks to minimize both intersystem dependencies as well as hide subsystem implementation details from system level operations.
Virtual System Interface - a layer of interface abstraction within the VOS that serves to isolate and hide implementation-specific interface requirements.
Wide-Area Network.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: A Flowchart visualizes a process or workflow, showing the steps and decisions involved. Nodes represent process steps or decisions, and arrows indicate the flow direction. `TD` or `LR` specify the diagram's orientation (Top-Down or Left-Right). Node IDs must be alphanumeric and unique. Labels for nodes with spaces or symbols must be enclosed in double-quotes, e.g., `NodeID["Node Label"]`. Decision nodes are diamond-shaped, and start/end nodes are rounded rectangles or circles. Critical rule: Avoid 'end', 'subgraph', 'class' as Node IDs.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
    A1["Start Observation Planning"] --> B1{"Science Program Defined?"}
    B1 -- No --> C1["Refine Program Parameters"]
    B1 -- Yes --> D1["Submit Science Program to Scheduler"]
    D1 --> E1{"Observation Scheduled & Approved?"}
    E1 -- No --> C1
    E1 -- Yes --> F1["Sequencer Initiates Observation"]
    F1 --> G1["Telescope & Instrument Setup"]
    G1 --> H1["Data Acquisition Started"]
    H1 --> I1{"Data Quality Acceptable?"}
    I1 -- No --> J1["Adjust Parameters / Re-Observe"]
    J1 --> G1
    I1 -- Yes --> K1["Data Stored & Archived"]
    K1 --> L1["End Observation"]

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: A Sequence Diagram illustrates the interaction between various participants in a system over time. Participants (actors, objects, systems) are placed at the top, and vertical dashed lines represent their lifelines. Messages are horizontal arrows between lifelines. Solid arrows indicate synchronous calls (`->`), and dashed arrows indicate asynchronous calls or responses (`-->`). `->>+` and `-->>-` are used for activation/deactivation, showing when a participant is active. `activate` and `deactivate` blocks should be balanced. `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, and `par` blocks can model conditional, optional, iterative, or parallel interactions. Avoid quoting alias IDs (e.g., `participant U as User` is valid, not `participant "U" as "User"`). Messages should avoid special characters like `{}`.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Astronomer
    participant GUI as "gemini GUI"
    participant S as Scheduler
    participant SEQ as Sequencer
    participant TCS as "Telescope Control Software"
    participant ICS as "Instrument Control Software"
    participant DAS as "Data Acquisition System"
    participant DB as Database

    A->>GUI: "Login"
    GUI->>S: "Authenticate(credentials)"
    S-->>GUI: "Authentication OK"
    A->>GUI: "Select Science Program G-SP-001"
    GUI->>S: "Request G-SP-001 details"
    S-->>GUI: "Return G-SP-001 config"
    A->>GUI: "Initiate Observation G-SP-001"
    GUI->>+S: "Schedule G-SP-001"
    S->>+SEQ: "Dispatch G-SP-001"
    SEQ->>+TCS: "Configure Telescope"
    TCS-->>-SEQ: "Telescope Ready"
    SEQ->>+ICS: "Configure Instrument"
    ICS-->>-SEQ: "Instrument Ready"
    SEQ->>+DAS: "Start Data Acquisition"
    DAS-->>-SEQ: "Acquisition Started"
    SEQ->>S: "Notify observation active"
    S-->>-GUI: "Observation G-SP-001 Active"
    DAS->>DB: "Store raw data"
    DB-->>DAS: "Data stored confirmation"
    DAS->>GUI: "Stream preview data"
    A->>GUI: "Monitor Data Quality"
    GUI->>DAS: "Request latest data"
    DAS-->>GUI: "Send preview"
    alt Data Quality OK
        DAS->>DB: "Finalize data storage"
    else Data Quality Low
        A->>GUI: "Request re-observation"
        GUI->>S: "Reschedule G-SP-001"
    end
    SEQ->>+TCS: "Stop Telescope"
    TCS-->>-SEQ: "Telescope stopped"
    SEQ->>+ICS: "Power Down Instrument"
    ICS-->>-SEQ: "Instrument powered down"
    SEQ->>S: "Observation G-SP-001 complete"
    S-->>-GUI: "Observation Complete"
    A-->>GUI: "Logout"

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: An Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) models the relationships between entities in a database or system. Entities are rectangles, attributes are listed within entities, and relationships are shown by lines connecting entities. Cardinality (e.g., `||--o{`) defines the number of instances of one entity related to another (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many). Relationship labels must be double-quoted. Attributes must specify `PK` (Primary Key), `FK` (Foreign Key), or `UK` (Unique Key) if applicable. Attribute names must be alphanumeric, without spaces or quotes. Entity names with spaces must be double-quoted.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
    USER ||--o{ OBSERVATION : "initiates"
    OBSERVATION ||--|{ OBSERVATION_LOG : "records"
    OBSERVATION ||--o{ SCIENCE_PROGRAM : "executes"
    OBSERVATION ||--o{ TELESCOPE : "uses"
    OBSERVATION ||--o{ INSTRUMENT : "configures"
    TELESCOPE ||--o{ INSTRUMENT : "mounts"
    OBSERVATION ||--|{ RAW_DATA : "generates"
    RAW_DATA ||--|{ PROCESSED_DATA : "derived from"
    SCIENCE_PROGRAM ||--o{ USER : "defined by"

    USER {
        int user_id PK
        string username UK
        string email
        string role
        datetime created_at
    }

    OBSERVATION {
        int obs_id PK
        int user_id FK
        int sp_id FK
        int tel_id FK
        int instr_id FK
        datetime start_time
        datetime end_time
        string status
        string obs_mode
    }

    SCIENCE_PROGRAM {
        int sp_id PK
        int user_id FK
        string program_name UK
        text program_details
        datetime created_at
        string status
    }

    TELESCOPE {
        int tel_id PK
        string tel_name UK
        string location
        string status
    }

    INSTRUMENT {
        int instr_id PK
        string instr_name UK
        string type
        string status
        int mounted_on_tel_id FK "Currently mounted telescope"
    }

    RAW_DATA {
        int data_id PK
        int obs_id FK
        string file_path
        string format
        long file_size
        datetime timestamp
    }

    PROCESSED_DATA {
        int proc_data_id PK
        int raw_data_id FK
        string proc_file_path
        string proc_format
        datetime proc_timestamp
        string processing_pipeline
    }

    OBSERVATION_LOG {
        int log_id PK
        int obs_id FK
        datetime event_time
        string event_type
        text event_description
    }
